content:
Good morning, and welcome.

Please take your seats. Some people may be arriving a little late after last night’s networking, but it’s great to see you here.

You have received emails and text messages about the event app. Please download it to your phone, or use it through your browser. If you go to the settings, you will find the link to the actual app as well. Through the app, you can follow the two streams we are using today, and in the stream for this room you can also access polls. At the moment, there is a first poll asking how many events in this conference series you have attended. We may use polls during the day, and if you are a presenter or chair and want one arranged, you can ask the organizers.

This is the sixth time we have arranged this event. We started during the COVID period in 2020. Since then, we have had three fully virtual editions, and this is now our third hybrid event. People have really loved the hybrid format because it gives us a much better way to network.

If you go to the event website, you can also find information from previous editions, including shared slides and other materials. You can review past programs and slides that were made available to participants.

This year we also have something a little new. In the event platform there is a Snapsite feature. You can select a language, and it will automatically create notes for you, including key takeaways, idea clouds, and similar outputs. We will see how well it understands my English, but please use it if it helps you make notes automatically.

We have more than 300 registered participants from 38 countries, which is the highest number so far for this event. Of those, 82% registered to attend here onsite at the venue.

You can also see the partner desks. Thanks to all our partners for helping us keep the event price moderate, and thanks as well to all the supporters involved. Altogether, we have almost 50 presentations from four different continents.

Each regular presentation, excluding keynotes, has a 20-minute slot. That slot includes both Q&A and time to change rooms, because we have two rooms next to each other. If you prefer a presentation in another session, you can move easily during the transition.

Each regular presentation, excluding keynotes, has a 20-minute slot. That slot includes both Q&A and time to change rooms, because we have two rooms next to each other. If you prefer a presentation in another session, you can move easily during the transition.

Tomorrow, in our closing session, we will also publish the feedback link, and you will receive emails and reminders about it. Please give us feedback. We read it very carefully and continue developing the event based on it.

If I look at what we received last year, people were generally satisfied, but there were also comments that pushed us to improve. For example, some felt the venue should be a little bigger, so we took that advice into account.

Soon I will announce our first keynote speaker, Dr. Sam Solakyan—hopefully I pronounced that correctly. That presentation will be the main presentation in this opening session, and it is a very fitting way to begin these two days. It paints the scenery for everything we are going to discuss.

After the coffee break, we will divide into two separate rooms. Here, we continue with AI implementation, and I will chair that session. At the beginning of each session, there is a short five-minute introduction where the chair explains the idea of the session, why the selected presenters are included, and what the red line or storyline of the session is.

In the other room there is a demo session. Then we have lunch, with enough time to eat, talk, and also catch up on email if needed.

After lunch, we again separate into two sessions: multi-agent AI and workflows, and AI technology and systems.

After the last coffee break, we all come back to this room for the final session of the day. We begin with a keynote, and that then leads into the panel discussion, which has typically been very successful in these events. People have really liked these panels. There will be four top-level panelists and two moderators, so it should be very interesting.

If you have booked the evening dinner, there is a 2.5-hour gap before it begins. The dinner venue is about 26 minutes from here, if I remember correctly—less than two kilometers away. You can take a tram or taxi, or walk if the weather is good.

If you have booked the evening dinner, there is a 2.5-hour gap before it begins. The dinner venue is about 26 minutes from here, if I remember correctly—less than two kilometers away. You can take a tram or taxi, or walk if the weather is good.

On the second day, the keynote is set up a little differently. There is already a teaser for it in the platform, so you can watch that there. We are not going to stream it here; we only have live presentations onsite. Again, we divide into two rooms. Depending on how late your discussions go today, you can choose accordingly tomorrow.

Then we have coffee, divide into two rooms again, and continue with sessions on sustainability, business models and concepts, and AI design.

After lunch, we move into computational design and construction. That then links into our final session after a short coffee break: the closing session, where we will hear a keynote about future construction sites.

That is something I believe will happen in the next phase of AI implementation. We are heading into a time where physical AI will play a much larger role. If you look at the material in the blog, some of these tools are already mentioned, along with where we are heading in this field.

In that sense, I think the first keynote today paints the scenery, while the last keynote tells us what the future looks like, where we are heading, and what kind of presentations we will most likely have at this event in the years to come.

When these kinds of big changes happen, it also has implications for universities. This is something I have discussed a lot with universities. At least in our country, I am happy that construction-focused universities have taken this seriously, more so than some universities in other industries.

It is very important that young people learn how to use these tools already at school and university, because we need those kinds of people in working life. We are in a business that needs a lot of resources, and we currently have a shortage of them. These tools will help us in our work.

If you think about the future, organizational structures may also change. If today we have a pyramid-shaped structure, it may become more like an obelisk. That could make it a little more challenging for younger people to enter working life in the traditional way. But if they are strong in AI skills, they will be very warmly welcomed into companies.

One more thought is worth keeping in mind. I heard this last month from Risto Siilasmaa, former chairman of Nokia, founder of the cybersecurity company F-Secure, and an investor in more than 15 companies. If you think about AI implementation—which is also the subject of Sam’s keynote—it is of course good to develop your culture, and it is very important to take small steps. Step by step will move you forward.

But parallel to that, you should also ask what something much bigger might look like. I think he said this very well: with AI, you should not aim for 50% improvements, but for 500% improvements, or even 5000%. Only then will you have to rethink the entire process, and then AI shows its true power. If you want to make a big leap, that requires you to think differently.

So, welcome everyone. There are also seats in front if anyone wants to come closer.

I am happy to introduce our first keynote speaker.

AI is no longer a future technology. It is reshaping industry right now, and the organizations that learn how to harness it will define the next era of business. Our first keynote speaker today helps leaders understand how to do exactly that.

Dr. Sam Solakyan, please welcome.

You are a strategist, bestselling author, and co-founder of Yegate. One of your books, Disrupt It, was published last year in March, and you will also publish a book called Future by Design, which focuses on individual development and how we can prepare ourselves as humans for the age of AI. This book focuses on the organizational level.

I also have to say that your background and work are very impressive. If we think about generative design—as we all know it today—you were already working with it in 2016. Ten years ago, you were using generative design to figure out how to design airplane components to make them lighter so they could be 3D printed, saving fuel and improving sustainability. In Europe, sustainability and the future of humanity are major concerns, so that was an important purpose behind that initiative.

I am very happy that we have you here. Please enjoy the presentation.

[Substantive sponsor and partner remarks that followed the opening were omitted, as they were separate introductions and organizational announcements rather than part of the opening overview itself.]

Speaker: Dr. Sam

Companies have bought licenses for GPT, deployed AI, even released their own AI agents, and now they are waiting for transformation to happen.

But it isn’t happening.

What’s the problem?

The problem is that they are trying to fit something into organizations that were not built for it. For the last 50 years, we built organizations around human limitations. We tried to fit ourselves into the system. But now something big has changed.

We are no longer only in the age of human intelligence. We are in the age of algorithmic intelligence—an age where algorithms can analyze millions of data points and give us more insight.

Let me give you some examples.

In the past, we spent hours or weeks on proposals. We would do analysis, look at the client’s past projects, see what was happening in the market, and make our best guess. That sounds familiar, especially for go/no-go decisions. It took weeks or months, and we were still guessing.

Now that is changing. We have AI tools that do not rely on guesswork. They act based on data. They look at your past projects, the client’s preferences and specifications, and market trends to tell you whether it is a go or no-go project. You can also look at the skills you have in the company and make informed decisions.

Let me share a personal experience from early in my career. I used to count walls, doors, and windows and do quantity takeoff. My numbers never matched—never. I tried averaging them, and it still took days or weeks depending on the size of the project.

Now there are tools that can help with that. You can use tools like Togal. There is no connection between me and the tools I am mentioning—I just want to show what is possible today for AEC firms. These tools can analyze your drawings and perform quantity takeoff. Looking back, that was such a waste of my time. They can do quantity takeoff more accurately and much faster—in minutes rather than weeks or months.

And it is not just proposals and quantity takeoff. There are also tools for code checking, design review, and specifications. These are all time-consuming manual processes.

And it is not just proposals and quantity takeoff. There are also tools for code checking, design review, and specifications. These are all time-consuming manual processes.

There is no shortage of AI tools. That is what I am trying to say. I bet that after this conference, many of you will go back to your organizations with one or two tools you want to try.

There are many AI tools in the market, but the transformation is still not happening.

Why?

Because companies are trying to install a Formula One engine on a horse carriage. The system was designed around human limitations—around what people could do. That is why the transformation is not happening.

So we are still waiting for ROI. We say, “I invested in Copilot, GPT, and other AI tools, but I am not seeing ROI. What should I do? Why is adoption low?” One of the challenges I hear every day is, “We bought 100 or 150 Copilot seats, and adoption is only five seats. What should we do?”

Let’s look at other industries to understand why we are in an age of disruption and how we can get ourselves ready.

Adobe is renowned for creative work. We use its products for design, PDFs, and everything else. It has 32,000 talented employees and has been around for more than 40 years. Yet it has been disrupted by Midjourney, which at one point had only 11 employees.

A company with 32,000 employees and more than four decades in the market was disrupted by a company with just 11 employees.

No wonder everyone is confused.

Google built a trillion-dollar search company. You entered a question, it gave you a list of links, and your job was to search through them and find the answer.

Then companies like Perplexity disrupted that model. Perplexity does not give you a list of links, half of them ads. It gives you the answer.

We have moved from the age of search to the age of synthesis. We want the answer.

And it is not just those companies. Look at transportation. The taxi industry around the world was disrupted by ride-sharing apps like Uber.

We have moved from the age of search to the age of synthesis. We want the answer.

And it is not just those companies. Look at transportation. The taxi industry around the world was disrupted by ride-sharing apps like Uber.

Do you know how many AI models Uber has running in the background to get you to your destination? Around 250. Uber has said it has roughly 250 AI models operating behind the scenes just to run the app and the system. Yet most users do not notice any of that. For us, it feels like zero.

While the taxi industry was fighting with ride-sharing apps, something else happened. Both were then disrupted by self-driving cars.

If you come to San Francisco, they are everywhere. Roughly 30% of the cars are self-driving—Waymo and others. I tried one two years ago. The car drove all around the city by itself. I loved it. I am an introvert, so I do not have to say hi to the driver. I just get in and leave. For me, it is heaven. I just hope it does not take me to heaven.

The point is that these AI technologies are disrupting the way we live and work.

Some people say, “Sam, we build infrastructure. We build buildings. We are not a tech company.”

I want to challenge that mindset, because we are in a new era.

Two years ago, I used a large vision model app. I gave it an image and a simple prompt in plain English: “How many workers are in this image?” The model analyzed the photo, told me how many workers were in it, and also generated code I could use to build an app that counts workers on site. It took me only a few minutes.

Imagine your employees having access to tools like that—being able to build things themselves without a computer science background and without coding experience.

That is why companies like AECOM acquired an AI company. They do not want to be disrupted; they want to disrupt. They know the change is happening.

The world is changing. Technology is becoming more accessible. We can build applications ourselves. I am not saying we are all going to become software companies like Trimble or Autodesk. My point is that there are problems and opportunities inside our companies that we can solve ourselves without computer science backgrounds.

That means every industry is changing, and every company can become, in some sense, a tech company through what AI makes possible.

The question is not whether AI is going to disrupt us. It will.

The key question is whether we are ready for that.

There is no shortage of tools, I promise. But is our organization ready? Are we going to get the ROI?

How do we make it happen? How do we prepare our organizations for the age of AI?

I want to share three angles:
1. Fostering a culture of innovation
2. Reimagining your business model
3. Revisiting your operating model

Everything I am going to share is based on work we have done over the last few years with AEC firms. We have worked with more than 10,000 employees, mostly in the U.S., on adopting AI and helping their companies become leaders in this new age. Those lessons are also captured in my book, Disrupted.

Before I continue: I mentioned that there is no shortage of tools. Based on our work over the last few years, I documented the most common AI use cases and tools. If you share your email address, I can send that to you.

Let’s start with the first angle: fostering a culture of innovation.

When a new technology arrives, it is easy to get excited, bring it into the company, and expect adoption. We are excited about the tool and want everyone to use it in projects.

But there is a challenge.

To explain it, I want to take you back to 1926, when microphones were invented. There is a scene in the movie Babylon that shows how a new technology created chaos. In that scene, the director brings microphones onto the set and expects Nellie, the actress, to still perform perfectly. But she struggles to adapt—body gesture, tone, positioning, everything changes.

The point is that they just brought new technology onto the scene and expected people to perform the same way immediately.

Did that work? No.

What is the solution?

It does not have to be complex or expensive.

I like to share how Citibank solved this problem. Citibank is one of the top five banks in the U.S. It has about 200,000 employees across 82 countries. Training everyone to use AI would be expensive and time-consuming.

It does not have to be complex or expensive.

I like to share how Citibank solved this problem. Citibank is one of the top five banks in the U.S. It has about 200,000 employees across 82 countries. Training everyone to use AI would be expensive and time-consuming.

So they focused first on one high performer in each area and trained those people deeply on AI—how to use it and what it looked like in practice. Those people became AI champions for the company.

They went back to their regions and trained colleagues. Citibank used a network-effect model. It was not a huge investment, nor was it excessively time-consuming. As a result, employees across the company are now using AI to reduce repetitive manual tasks and better serve clients.

For AI champions to make that happen, they need time for experimentation and time to train others.

In AEC, especially in the U.S., many firms make money by maximizing employee utilization. The more hours billed to projects, the more revenue they generate. That makes it difficult to create space for innovation and experimentation.

But there is a simple solution.

Look at 3M—Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing. For a long time, it has been listed as one of the most innovative companies. How does a mining company surpass companies like Tesla in innovation?

William McKnight joined 3M as a bookkeeper’s assistant in 1917 and eventually became president. In 1947, he introduced the “15% culture.” That meant every employee could spend 15% of their time on innovation, experimentation, and breaking the way they normally worked.

Post-it Notes came out of that culture. Today, around 35% of 3M’s revenue comes from ideas generated through that 15% innovation culture.

By simply giving employees time to experiment and rethink how they work, 3M remained one of the most innovative companies in the Fortune 500.

The point is that our employees need time—to experience, to learn, to experiment. If they are focused only on day-to-day work, it becomes very difficult to find ways to experiment with AI.

How does that work in our industry, where utilization matters so much?

SSOE, one of the top AE firms in the U.S. and a leader in semiconductor design, offers a useful example. They work on data centers, schools, hospitals, and more. To foster a culture of innovation, they combined the Citibank approach with the 3M approach.

SSOE, one of the top AE firms in the U.S. and a leader in semiconductor design, offers a useful example. They work on data centers, schools, hospitals, and more. To foster a culture of innovation, they combined the Citibank approach with the 3M approach.

They provided AI training to a portion of their employees and to board members, because they wanted buy-in for the investment and wanted everyone to share the same understanding of AI. They also gave those people space for innovation. The AI champions then returned to their offices and shared what they had learned using that network-effect approach.

They started this three and a half years ago—not recently. The moment they felt the industry was changing, they chose to take that approach and lead innovation in the industry.

Their CEO described the result this way: when they really got into it, they created a pipeline of projects where everyone could see that AI could help the business right away. That was the outcome: a pipeline of opportunities where AI could create value.

So culture of innovation is the foundation of this new era.

If we want to know whether we are ready for the age of AI, we need to ask:
- How strong is the culture of innovation in our company?
- What is our employees’ mindset?
- Are they excited about AI, or are they afraid it will take their jobs?
- Do we give them enough space to experiment and rethink their workflows?
- What is our approach to balancing utilization with innovation time?

The second element of readiness is the business model.

With AI, everything is changing. Over the next decade, it will not be our competitors doing what we do better that matter most. The next decade belongs to those who make what we do irrelevant by building something new.

There are many opportunities to reshape business models with AI. I want to focus on three:
1. Personalization
2. Insights
3. Scaling expertise into new markets

Take Levi’s. For 147 years, it relied on resellers to sell its jeans. Now, with AI, it wants to take back control and create a direct relationship with customers. It wants to personalize style, fit, and the overall product experience.

Take Levi’s. For 147 years, it relied on resellers to sell its jeans. Now, with AI, it wants to take back control and create a direct relationship with customers. It wants to personalize style, fit, and the overall product experience.

That kind of personalization is evolving into what I call anticipatory intelligence. Amazon is an example. It often has products positioned in warehouses before we even place the order. It monitors our behavior to estimate the probability that we will order something and prepares for it.

Most of Amazon’s warehouses now use robots for much of the work.

Netflix does something similar. To maximize the time we spend on its platform, it uses AI to personalize our experience and predict what we may want to watch next. Even the thumbnail image for the same movie can differ from one user to another. If you like romance, you might see a kiss. If you like action, you might see a car chase.

The same movie is presented differently to different people.

What does that mean for us?

How can we personalize our clients’ experience? Earlier, I talked about go/no-go decisions for proposals. AI can help us personalize our approach based on what clients prefer, what they have done in the past, and what projects we have completed with them. It can help us stand out by creating something more connected to their needs, rather than a generic output that a GPT simply generates.

That is the power of personalization with AI.

The second business model shift is moving from products to insights.

Consider JCDecaux. Back in 1960, the company began by fabricating street furniture—kiosks, bus stations, and similar infrastructure—for cities. Many of its customers were municipalities, which often lacked enough money.

So the company asked: How do we expand the market?

Its answer was not simply to sell products. Instead, it offered to build, install, and maintain the infrastructure for free—and then monetize it through advertising. That is how it built a multibillion-dollar company.

Now, with AI, JCDecaux is partnering with an AI company because it wants to take the model further. It wants to determine which ad should be displayed based on weather, day of the week, and time of day. That means moving from simply selling products or ad space to selling insight.

And because the ads are more targeted, the company can charge more.

And because the ads are more targeted, the company can charge more.

For us, the question is: how do we move from delivering products or services to delivering insights?

The third shift is scaling expertise.

Knowledge is becoming abundant. In the past, it took years to build expertise. Now it is much easier to access knowledge.

Does that create a race to the bottom for our industry because clients can get online access to some of the insights we used to sell them?

Maybe not.

Take WRA, an engineering company in the top tier of U.S. water and reservoir work. Like many firms in this space, it serves municipalities, and 65% of municipalities in the U.S. are small and constrained financially.

WRA asked: how can we use AI to provide our expertise to those municipalities at a lower cost?

The answer was to use AI to optimize the design process for pipelines, reservoirs, and related infrastructure so those municipalities could afford access to expertise. This does not mean simply charging less. It means reducing operating costs through AI so the expertise can scale into a larger market.

So we have examples of:
- Levi’s using AI for personalization
- Amazon and Netflix using anticipatory intelligence
- JCDecaux shifting from products to insights
- WRA scaling expertise to broader markets

What might that look like for our companies?

Maybe we can know what our clients need before they know it themselves. We have their data. We know their past projects. We can connect that to market trends and develop better insights. Instead of waiting for clients to send us RFPs, maybe we go to them first.

That means being proactive rather than reactive.

The third piece of readiness is the operating model.

Culture is the foundation. Business model is the new value generator. But we also need to change our processes. We cannot do all of this using systems built around human limitations.

If you look at most organizational structures, they are hierarchical. People work in layers, and workflows are built around those structures.

Right now, many companies are using AI to write emails, draft proposals, summarize reports, and support VDC or BIM-related submissions. Those are useful applications. Why should we spend hours reading a 100-page report if we can work smarter?

But those uses often deliver only 10% or 20% efficiency gains.

How do we get 10x?

But those uses often deliver only 10% or 20% efficiency gains.

How do we get 10x?

Let me share an example: AIG.

AIG is a major insurance company in the U.S. One of its main tasks is risk analysis. It evaluates projects to determine how risky they are. The relevant data sits in PDFs, cloud systems, market data sources, and many other disconnected locations. It used to take weeks or months to gather everything, analyze it, and determine whether a project was worth taking on.

With AI, AIG changed the workflow itself.

It created its own AI system to analyze internal data, market trends, and client preferences and perform risk analysis in minutes. The projected impact was dramatic: from approximately $0.3 billion in business premiums in 2018 to a target of $4 billion by 2030.

They could handle more projects and do more analyses because AI made the process faster.

The key point is that they did not try to force a tool into an old workflow. They changed the workflow itself. That is why AI worked for them.

And these changes are not limited to digital work. They also affect the physical world.

Consider PepsiCo. It has warehouses and factories all over the world. If it wants to optimize a factory layout, that is risky because changing the layout usually means disrupting production. Engineers may come up with a few scenarios they think are lower risk, then implement one and see how it goes.

Now, with partners like Siemens and Nvidia, PepsiCo is creating digital twins of factory layouts. That allows it to generate more scenarios and simulate them digitally before making changes in the real world.

The process becomes data-driven rather than dependent on a handful of engineers making educated guesses.

When we change workflows, one part of our organization is affected heavily: our people.

Now we need people with new skills. We need them to work differently.

Kodak did not fail because it did not know about digital photography. It literally invented digital photography. It failed because it kept hiring chemists. Its legacy business was chemistry-based, and it kept staffing for the old model rather than the new one. What it needed were software engineers.

The point is that as we change workflows, we need to upskill people, reskill them, and perhaps rethink hiring.

The point is that as we change workflows, we need to upskill people, reskill them, and perhaps rethink hiring.

Procter & Gamble studied this question. It tested whether AI could act as a “cybernetic teammate”—in other words, whether AI could work alongside employees and augment them.

P&G had marketing teams and business teams that tended not to talk to each other and did not share each other’s expertise. It divided participants into four groups:
- Teams with AI
- Teams without AI
- Individuals with AI
- Individuals without AI

The surprising result was that individuals with AI performed better than teams without AI, and they did it in less time.

The research showed that AI can indeed function as a cybernetic teammate.

That means our teams may become smaller, but mightier, because they can partner with intelligence to get work done.

Companies like Shopify and Duolingo are already asking, before they hire a human, whether AI agents can do the work in partnership with employees.

As a result, the hierarchy of our organizations will change. It will no longer be only people. It will be a combination of humans and AI agents working together.

So the transformation is not happening at the level of individual tasks. It is happening at the organizational level.

Teams are changing. Workflows are changing. And our teams may get smaller but stronger. They will be able to do more with less—but more intelligently—because they will have visibility into more data and more context.

That is where we can revisit our operating model and find the path to 10x value.

As I wrap up, let me return to the three themes: fostering a culture of innovation, reimagining the business model, and revisiting the operating model.

The companies I mentioned—Perplexity, Midjourney, SSOE, WRA, and others—do not necessarily have better AI tools than everyone else. The difference is that they are not asking, “How do I increase efficiency by 10%?” They are asking, “How do I change my organization for this new era? How do I help change the whole industry?”

And when I think about how our industry may change, the first thing that comes to mind is you.

And when I think about how our industry may change, the first thing that comes to mind is you.

In this new age of AI, I do not think you are just executives running companies. You are change-makers. That is why you are here—because you want to understand how AI is going to change our industry and how it can bring more value to your employees, your organizations, your clients, and your stakeholders.

You are the orchestrators trying to change not just your organizations, but eventually the whole industry through what AI makes possible.

Thank you.

Q&A

Audience Member: Thank you very much for this exciting presentation. Most of your examples are large companies, but in Finland and across the EU, most architecture firms are SMEs—often fewer than 50 people, sometimes fewer than 20. How do you see AI helping those smaller firms, which are not really technology companies?

Dr. Sam: Great question. I only had 45 minutes, so I did not include all the slides I could have. You are right that the majority of firms in our industry are small. I remember a call with an architecture firm of only 10 people. They reached out because they wanted to build an AI strategy that would help them stand out in the market.

This goes back to my point that AI can make organizations mightier. Small firms are seeing value in using AI to do similar work—or smarter work—with fewer people. There are also firms of 100 or 120 people that have started hiring AI experts internally. Some examples I mentioned publicly are larger, but there are also smaller firms doing this, including some whose names I cannot share. One of them has about 100 people and now has its own AI team.

These firms are pairing AI experts with domain experts so they can improve the work and reduce operating costs within projects.

Audience Member: Thank you, Sam. That was a great talk. I am curious about the bottom of the AEC pyramid, where the work is actually happening. That is where the true productivity challenge is. I recently heard a mechanical or electrical subcontractor say, “If AI can tell my people how to bend a pipe better, then talk to me about it.” Beyond working with the top of organizations, have you worked where the work is actually happening?

Dr. Sam: I had a conversation before the session about research from Anthropic. At least right now, the part of the industry most affected by AI is the AE side. There are many opportunities there for firms to apply AI directly to the work they do.

If your question is about the construction site—the physical world—that is a different area. There are use cases there too, such as pipe bending, pipe cutting, or figuring out how much leftover pipe from one project can be reused on the next project by a fabricator.

But right now, AE firms are the ones being most impacted by AI. Some see that as a race to the bottom, while others see it as a new opportunity and are trying to reinvent themselves. My focus today was on those firms that are trying to reinvent themselves through culture, business model, and operating model.

Speaker 1: Thank you. I’m glad to be here and to bring a perspective from the United States. Our company is focused on transportation, so we work with many transportation authorities across the country, especially state DOTs.

At HNTB, our approach to AI is to develop all 7,500 employees into digital innovators. That is really our strategy. We are also turning that approach into our consulting work, helping many clients—mainly state DOTs—adopt a culture of innovation. The change-management and human side of AI is probably the most challenging part of this transformation.

Within the U.S., state Departments of Transportation should, in theory, work together to support mobility across the entire country. In reality, they are very unique organizations. Several associations help them collaborate and innovate together.

One is TRB, the Transportation Research Board, which many of you may know. It helps drive research and funds much of the collaboration. It is also helping organize the AI peer exchange that we are discussing today.

Another is AASHTO, the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials. That is where DOT executives collaborate, share best practices, and meet through conferences across the country.

A third is ITS America, originally centered on intelligent transportation systems but now broadened to include many technology patterns, including AI, data governance, data approaches, and culture-of-innovation training and resources for DOTs. Through ITS World Congress and related activities, this creates a strong exchange of expertise, especially in transportation operations, management centers, and mobility strategies.

What we want to highlight today is the diversity across DOTs, but also the growing level of AI adoption. My colleague Jesse Newberry has organized these peer exchanges and will share some of the findings.

Jesse Newberry: Hi everyone. I’m Jesse Newberry with HNTB, formerly with the Massachusetts Department of Transportation. What was really interesting about these AI-focused DOT peer exchanges is that common themes emerged very clearly.

We saw honest conversations between states about what they are trying, what is working, what feels messy, and what still feels unresolved. Much of the discussion was not about shiny AI tools. It was about practical things that make AI successful in a DOT: governance, workforce readiness, data, procurement, and related issues.

There were two main peer exchanges in 2025. One was in San Diego, hosted by Caltrans, and the other was in Boston, hosted by MassDOT in partnership with Northeastern University. That academic partnership is important, and I will return to that point. The California exchange happened in January and had about 10 states attending. The Boston exchange expanded to 14. In total, about 17 states have now been represented, with strong geographic diversity across the U.S.

We intentionally held one on the West Coast and one on the East Coast. The next one will likely be in a more central location. Across both events, the recurring themes were governance, workforce, ethics and security, data management, use cases, ROI, and procurement challenges.

One of the biggest takeaways was that governance really matters. Many agencies are experimenting with AI, but without a strategic framework, efforts get fragmented quickly.

Texas and California are strong examples of states taking this seriously. They are using a more structured approach through staff interviews, focus groups, and use-case inventories. The broader message was that states would benefit from a shared national perspective—principles that allow flexibility in local implementation.

Data was the next major theme. AI tools are only as good as the information behind them, and DOTs have a treasure trove of data.

Take Massachusetts DOT as an example. It is really four companies in one: rail and transit, the registry of motor vehicles, aeronautics, and highways. They all operate separately, and they do not naturally talk about their data together. That is a lost opportunity, because the organization collectively knows where people go, why they go, where their cars are garaged, and how they get to airports. AI creates an opportunity to connect those datasets.

States talked about data quality and data preparation as major barriers. That makes sense, because AI is only as good as the information behind it. The conversation focused on breaking down silos, creating common standards, improving quality control, and treating data work not just as governance but as part of AI readiness itself.

Then we moved to workforce, which is a particularly important topic. The best tools do not go far without people who have the skills to use them.

Then we moved to workforce, which is a particularly important topic. The best tools do not go far without people who have the skills to use them.

This is a major challenge for DOTs, where the workforce is aging and institutional knowledge can leave with retiring staff. If we do not have tools to help capture and transfer that knowledge to the next generation, we are in trouble. States talked a great deal about shortages, acquiring talent, and the difficulty of creating the right technical roles within government structures. In the U.S., union activity can make it very difficult to change job descriptions, so that was a major point of discussion.

We also discussed the balance between building capability internally and buying from vendors. There needs to be balance there.

MassDOT, for example, partnered with Northeastern University for upskilling and hiring. They combined the two: building an internship pipeline from Boston-area universities into the DOT, then creating a path for those people to remain involved and start their careers there.

Texas is also taking workforce training very seriously. In addition, a nonprofit called InnovateUS offers free AI training specifically for government staff, and many states are making use of it.

Procurement was another central issue. In U.S. government, traditional procurement models can take six to twelve months to get a product in place. In the AI world, that simply does not work. By the time a product is procured, the technology may already be obsolete.

Agencies are starting to experiment with low-cost pilots, sandbox environments, expedited tracks, and ways to move from pilot to adoption without starting over. They are also putting more structure around risk through assessments, procurement reviews, and frameworks like NIST, the National Institute of Standards and Technology. There is also a clear role for human oversight and transparency in procurement.

There is still much to do to reform procurement in the U.S.

Then we looked at use cases and ROI. If you take the time to build an ROI case and present it to executives, adoption improves.

A good example is MassDOT’s computer-vision sidewalk review work. It significantly reduced schedule and cost, and the value was tangible. Executives could see it right away. That success generated more buy-in for additional AI projects.

A good example is MassDOT’s computer-vision sidewalk review work. It significantly reduced schedule and cost, and the value was tangible. Executives could see it right away. That success generated more buy-in for additional AI projects.

Texas DOT also found that doing ROI analysis with executives at the table led to better support for AI initiatives. Texas was also the first state in the U.S. to publish an AI strategic plan. It was released in the middle of last year and updated again around December or January. Other states are now taking notice and adapting that model for themselves.

Looking ahead, not every DOT has to do the same thing. They can move faster and more confidently if they build shared learning together—shared frameworks, shared tools, and shared experiences.

One of the most important outputs from these peer exchanges has been the idea of creating a pooled-fund study. In the U.S., a pooled fund allows states to come together financially to support travel, speakers, and collaboration. It is an important first step in helping DOTs navigate this together.

New Jersey stepped up to sponsor and make the first investment. The target had been $300,000. When I checked before coming here, it had already grown to $1.1 million through state contributions.

The goal is to keep states connected, support additional peer exchanges, build strategic frameworks, and create shared resources such as repositories, training resources, and governance tools. What I like most is that it supports coordination without forcing every state to do exactly the same thing. We are better off working together.

There is a lot of momentum. When agencies were asked where they wanted to be in 12 months, none said they wanted to stay the same. The priorities were consistent: governance, stronger data foundations, more workforce readiness, and better ways to share tools, training, and lessons learned. Fragmented processes are not sustainable. National coordination will matter if agencies want to move from pilots into real AI capability.

In closing, this is a very active and ongoing conversation among U.S. states. We already have 17 states involved. Partnerships matter. No one agency has to figure this out alone.

Sharing what works—and just as importantly, what does not work, and what can be reused—is probably the fastest way to advance AI in government transportation.

Sharing what works—and just as importantly, what does not work, and what can be reused—is probably the fastest way to advance AI in government transportation.

As chair of one of the TRB committees, the Data Systems and Governance Committee, I want to personally invite all of you to participate. We welcome an international audience. We want to hear about research, new ideas, and lessons learned, and I would love to connect and share those with U.S. DOTs.

Audience question: We are a much smaller government organization with just one department. I found a lot of inspiration in your story. One thing I was wondering about is the balance between human-in-the-loop processes and automation. Governments have a lot of responsibility, so we often keep humans involved, but we also know that many of the real benefits come from automation. Do you have real-world examples of how that balance is being found in the U.S.?

Jesse Newberry: Human in the loop is very important, and the DOTs spent a lot of time talking about that issue.

The way I like to describe it is this: AI is your partner. It can help you with many parts of your job. You may have an engineer who thinks they understand AI, then they sit down with a business user and discover what that person actually does every day. That can be eye-opening.

When you give someone a tool and they realize it will genuinely help them, there is a lightbulb moment. They think, “This is going to save me a lot of time and effort.” Then they start thinking about everything else they do, especially in a DOT environment where employees are overwhelmed by the amount of work that needs to happen in the state. They begin to see what is possible.

But it takes time, effort, and energy to get each person to that point individually. That is very important work.

Max Leander: It’s exciting to be here. I’m going to walk you through a CEO’s playbook for AI implementation, which is really a five-part framework for getting it right. It builds on many of the themes already mentioned today.

I’m from AFRY Group. I have worked extensively with company boards and executive teams, and I’ve seen repeatedly that it is not easy to get this right at the leadership level.

Many companies are adopting AI. We all agree that AI is changing white-collar work. But only a fraction of companies report that the CEO is overseeing governance and AI implementation. That matters because this is a systemic change. We are not just adding technology. We are reshaping how the company works, what it offers, and how it creates value. That means the entire C-suite and leadership team must support the transformation across their respective areas of responsibility.

A lot of CEOs and executives are struggling with how to get out of pilot mode and how to produce gains that stack up and improve bottom-line results.

Here are the five steps.

First, strategic intent. If you do not have a strategy for how AI ties into the broader company strategy, you are setting yourself up for failure. You need to be clear on why you are implementing AI. What is its purpose in the company? What is the purpose of the change, and how does it connect to the overall strategic framing of the business?

That clarity helps you move beyond opportunistic, scattered experiments. It also means you should apply the same measures you use for other strategic changes. Use your standard KPIs to evaluate progress.

It is also important to link AI strongly to human strategy, HR strategy, and people strategy. Who are you hiring? What skills do you require in different roles? In AEC companies especially, the value comes from the capabilities of our experts.

Second, ownership and governance. One single person or function cannot own everything AI-related. You need to distribute ownership across the layers of the organization. The exact model may vary depending on company size, but clear governance and decision rights must be established at each level.

This ownership should extend down to the P&L owner, because that is where you measure real bottom-line impact.

This ownership should extend down to the P&L owner, because that is where you measure real bottom-line impact.

From an operating-model perspective, a network of AI champions is a very good framework. You can have a central enablement team while pushing ownership out into the organization. When that is tied to funding, you can really turbocharge implementation.

Third, the technology model: what to buy and what to build. This is a critical question, and the answer is company-specific. There is no universal answer, but there is a good method for finding yours.

You should build your advantage on your own data. Where your company creates distinctive value, that is where you should invest in bespoke solutions. Commodity layers should generally be bought, because it is often cheaper to buy that technology than to develop it internally, and doing so frees up your talent to focus on high-value, proprietary solutions.

In other words, direct your own people toward your own value-creating solutions and procure the rest.

This is not about how much technology you have developed. Engineers often like to brag about that. What matters is how much you can move the needle with the complete system you have in place.

Fourth, timing and pace. We are still in the early stages of how AI will transform companies, and speed may be more important than getting everything exactly right.

The best way to work is with small minimum viable solutions or products that can be shipped in weeks rather than quarters. You need tight loops so you can iterate, refine, and reinvest in the capabilities that are actually producing bottom-line results.

In AEC, timing matters even more because project cycles are often long. It is important to move up the learning curve early—not just to gain technology, but to gain experience and organizational learning. Late movers will have a very difficult time catching up in a few years.

Fifth, portfolio logic. I like to think of the AI initiative portfolio or change-management portfolio like an investment portfolio.

You need focus, and that focus comes from strategy. You should go hard on the priorities you have identified. But you should also mix in a few bold options.

You need both types of initiatives: those that generate value now or in the next quarter, and those that build the foundation for more systemic change.

You need both types of initiatives: those that generate value now or in the next quarter, and those that build the foundation for more systemic change.

It also makes sense to cluster related efforts over a six-month or one-year period. That way, you build on what you have already done and can accelerate the pace at which you create capability.

As with an investment portfolio, you should double down on what is working. You also need to accept that not every initiative will succeed. You must be willing to stop or pause those that underperform.

So the five factors that make or break AI implementation are: strategy, ownership, the right mix of bought and bespoke technology, a strong cycle time and pace of innovation, and a focused but balanced portfolio with room for high-upside bets.

Now I will give a few concrete examples of how we have used this playbook in our company.

We released our AI strategy in late 2024 as part of a broader strategy update. That allowed us to incorporate the changes required for AI adoption into HR and people strategy, and into product and offering strategy. We are working not only with AI and technology, but also with how we enable people to make the transition and how what we sell to clients is changing.

Our AI program is overseen by the company board. The board receives updates on what we are doing over each six-month period, and we follow progress through KPIs that are reported monthly.

The company board and C-level leaders are all invested in the change.

We also operate a champions network—a hub-and-spoke model—with representatives from each P&L department driving change at a grassroots level.

On differentiation and sourcing, we have built quite a lot of bespoke AI solutions. At the same time, we are shifting the software applications we buy so that they are aligned with our overall AI strategy. In many cases, that means changing the applications used in daily operations.

Even in bespoke solutions, we rely on external libraries and components. So bought and built technology can be mixed.

Even in bespoke solutions, we rely on external libraries and components. So bought and built technology can be mixed.

On cycle time, we are trying to practice what we preach. We aim to deliver fast, minimum viable solutions or products. Once we identify the target audience, we involve them as early as possible and try to ship quickly. Then, based on actual usage data, we decide whether to invest further and scale that capability.

To increase cycle speed, we also combine top-down and bottom-up approaches. From the top down, we push horizontal corporate capabilities. From the bottom up, we enable experts to build mini-bespoke solutions, and we democratize access to data for building those solutions. That is why we are training portions of our staff on low-code platforms.

On portfolio management, one practical approach we use is to include one risky bet in every half-year plan. That might mean testing an unusual technology or taking an optimistic position on something we simply want to explore. Sometimes the most valuable thing you gain is learning what does not work.

So my main point is this: strong leadership is needed across the C-level. That is how you get bottom-line impact.

You get that impact by distributing ownership and creating targets at each level.

You need to own your data and build your bespoke solutions where you create the most value.

You need a strong pace.

And you need to be systematic in how you build capabilities.

A synergetic portfolio will outperform a sequence of isolated pilots.

Audience question: If you want employees involved in AI, do you prefer a wide range of employees spending perhaps 50% of their time on AI, or a smaller team working on it 100%?

Max Leander: I think the most effective way to bring AI into the organization is often to bring it in behind the scenes, so the end user does not necessarily even know they are using AI. They simply receive solutions that get the job done and are intuitive to use. As a single strategy, that is the best way to deliver AI to people in the organization.

Nitzan Bartov: Thank you for the invitation and for choosing this session. We are here to talk about AI culture. I also want to thank Sam for raising culture in the opening presentation.

We are representing Henning Larsen, a global practice based in Denmark. We work in more than 30 countries. Since the late 1950s, the practice has grown to more than 650 employees. We are an interesting case for AI adoption because we work across a broad spectrum of fields: architecture, landscape, urbanism, interiors, and lighting.

Our guiding principles are sustainability and circularity, design excellence, research, and data. You do not immediately see “customer value” listed in the conventional way, and that is part of what makes architecture an unusual company context.

I am currently doing PhD research on AI within architecture and urban design, and I was tasked at Henning Larsen with figuring out how we should approach AI. When I arrived, the message was essentially: “We need some AI. We don’t really know how.”

The first instinct in many European companies is governance: if we want to use AI, what are the rules and the guardrails? Very quickly, however, we understood that the way we wanted to approach this was not through governance first, but through culture.

Why culture instead of governance? There are many reasons, but one of the most important is that many aspects of using AI are not truly governable. The real conversation is not only about AI. It is about the use of data—how we use it, how we justify decisions with it, and how we talk about it. Those things are shaped more by culture than by rules.

We talk a great deal about data-driven design, but when data is actually driving the design, is that always what we want? To what extent do we want that? Those are cultural questions.

One example is that AI is trained on datasets. In architecture, we use a great deal of non-empirical, ambiguous, and interpretable language. If we ask a data-driven system to produce a “beautiful building,” we are far more likely to get one type of result than another. That demonstrates that “data-driven” is not automatically the same thing as “good,” and deciding otherwise is a cultural choice.

Another example is statistics. If we operated purely as a statistics-based industry, we might not design handicap ramps, because statistically there may not be enough wheelchair users to justify the effort, cost, and time. But we do not design only for the majority. We design for everybody.

A third issue is accountability. We are always accountable for our output. We can never transfer accountability to AI. Therefore, governance around AI should not be the driving force of adoption.

Our approach at Henning Larsen is built on principles. We do not want AI to be locked in an ivory tower. We do not want a small department of AI experts to handle all AI tasks, all learning, and all capability development. Instead, we are pursuing collective learning and a bottom-up upskilling approach in which all employees are part of the AI transition.

We think in terms of horizontal and vertical adoption.

Horizontal adoption is what everyone in the company can do with AI. Vertical adoption refers to edge cases and to AI champions who push the boundaries of what AI can do for us.

On the horizontal side, we let all employees at Henning Larsen try existing AI tools as much as they want, and then we evaluate the experience. How beneficial was it? How valuable? How useful? How effective?

We gathered this data and found that some existing tools are already very good for us. These are some of the tools we use, including our internal Ramboll GPT, because we are part of the Ramboll Group.

Another important aspect is partnership. In addition to using tools, we are teaming up with industry leaders. We are developing a strategic partnership with Krea around AI visualization.

Our approach rests on four principles for AI adoption: literacy, transparency, agency, and accountability.

Literacy means that we do not merely teach people how to use AI. We do not think it is especially valuable to teach people a specific prompt or a specific program. We teach them what AI is. We teach the mechanics of large language models and the fundamentals of machine learning. When people understand the technology, they become more comfortable experimenting and choosing the right technology for the right problem.

Transparency means we are always open about the use of AI, internally and externally. Without a culture of transparency, people use AI without disclosing it. That makes it impossible to properly evaluate outcomes. We also need alignment with clients and collaborators about what AI was used for and why.

Agency is about how much agency we retain ourselves. Of course we can automate many tasks and hand them over to AI, but if we do that entirely, we lose touch with the project. In architecture especially, it matters that we remain hands-on rather than simply optimizing everything for speed.

Accountability is a mindset. Whatever we produce remains our responsibility. We cannot hand accountability to AI.

Emilian Niyere: I’m the head of digital adoption at Henning Larsen. Let me explain how we actually use AI in the company.

Do we write one prompt and get a project out of it? Obviously not. Architecture is extremely complex. We cannot get the whole result at once. But AI can help us with the many small slices that allow us to move faster.

Like many people in this room, we use AI for everyday productivity with tools like Copilot, writing emails, and scheduling meetings. We also use our internal ChatGPT called Ramboll GPT. But we also use AI in ways that are much more meaningful.

One of the most interesting applications is communication. AI works through natural language, which means that early in a project, with clients and stakeholders, we can talk our way toward a visual direction. Without building a 3D model or texturing anything, we can explore options and align much earlier and faster.

While doing that, something almost magical happens: AI also inspires us. It becomes a way to ideate architecture and then turn it into reality, such as in the pavilion project in Milan.

Architecture is mostly about life within a space, and one space can be many things. It can be an exhibition space, a disco, or a dining space. It can have different moods and atmospheres. AI helps us simulate all of that quickly, which helps clients understand possible uses and futures of a space.

This is especially interesting in disciplines such as landscape, where rendering vegetation is complex. AI opens new opportunities there. One thing I particularly value is the ability to simulate environmental risks such as flooding scenarios. People react differently when they can see the risks, and that helps us bring meaningful conversations forward earlier.

AI is also powerful when working with data. We bring together reports and regulations and use tools like NotebookLM to interact with that information, understand whether we are moving in the right direction, structure it, and brainstorm possible directions.

This also applies in other disciplines. For example, if the client or local architect provides a planting database, AI can help turn that into the right planting palette.

We can then present this work back to clients using combinations of tools. What matters is that this is bottom-up. The person doing this is not an AI expert; they are a designer. They may start with something simple in Revit, add color, and then produce something visually compelling. Even during construction, work can be engaging and tell a story.

AI is very strong in helping us tell stories about futures. We can produce sketches that align with our visual identity and our brand, but also with the specific project. We can customize outputs to specific workflows, including in Revit, and use them efficiently during projects.

If you do not want polished sketches, we can also create looser diagrams. That can be useful because it avoids overcommitting to materiality or detailing. At the other end of the spectrum, if you want high quality, we can add a great deal of detail and bring the output close to rendering-company quality, starting from a simpler render and combining it with location-specific imagery, such as from Google Earth.

AI also adapts well to people. You can sketch on top of it and add new features. You can start from almost anything, even a blank base. That is how we test facades and materiality. It opens opportunities to move from a sketch to a rough 3D model.

So AI helps us do what we already do in a better and more meaningful way, while also opening the door to things we could not do before.

Ashgan Arasteh: I sit in our development department and work on tools, but I come from an architecture background, and story matters a great deal in architecture.

Ashgan Arasteh: I sit in our development department and work on tools, but I come from an architecture background, and story matters a great deal in architecture.

I often think about tool development as a story. We begin with a challenge—for example, finding the best environmental analysis tool, or understanding how to bring biogenic materials into public architecture. In both architecture and development, the challenge is clearly defined.

Then, based on that story logic, we separate concerns through a narrative. We ask: what are the areas where digital agents can contribute, and where do human agents need to contribute? We gather those areas into one framework where we can define the logic and the workflow.

Through that framework, breakthroughs can happen. We find digital solutions that reflect the same kinds of design breakthroughs we seek in architecture—better mobility, better low-carbon material selection, and so on. These topics are especially significant in Denmark.

But this is not only about digital agents. It is also about human agents.

I am fortunate to work with talented colleagues across many domains. We have industrial PhDs contributing to the knowledge base, and colleagues with expertise in wood construction, BIM management, landscape design, urban design, and more. We cannot create one omniscient entity that does everything, but we can bring all of those people and their knowledge into an ecosystem of tools.

That is what we are building: tools where knowledge from noise simulation, material innovation, or any other specialty can be embedded into the design environment. It is not just about LLMs, agentic tools, toolchains, or technical components. It is about actual people with knowledge shaping the agency of the system.

That creates a good narrative within our company, and narrative matters to us because we tell stories.

Nitzan Bartov: To wrap up, what has the impact of this approach been at Henning Larsen?

The first thing we see very clearly is a cultural shift. In the last year, people across the company—not just in Copenhagen—have become much more open about talking about AI, much more curious about it, and much more willing to experiment. Colleagues now call one another and say, “I tried this thing—do you want to see it?”

To give one concrete example, we created an AI chat on Teams. Normally there is not much motivation to join another AI chat, but nearly half the company voluntarily joined this one and actively uses it. People share what they tried, what worked, what did not work, and what they think about new models.

We are also seeing people develop new skills. They are getting comfortable with prompting, with modeling for AI visualization, and with identifying new ways of modeling, communicating, and working comfortably in AI-supported environments.

We are also starting to gather performance data, and it is very clear that people using AI for specific tasks consistently report time savings of 70% to 80%. Across the board, they also say it improves quality because they are choosing the right tools for the right tasks.

We are also beginning to see cost savings, though we will need more time before we have more precise numbers.

Most importantly, people understand that this is not about “learning AI” once, putting that knowledge in a box, and then moving on. AI is developing at a pace that requires continuous learning. There is no endpoint, at least not in the foreseeable future.

That is the main lesson I would like to leave with you.

Thank you for listening.

Eric Stromberg: Let’s jump straight into relevance, and I want to make this personal for a moment.

Some of you may have noticed that I was limping slightly when I came on stage. I had minor hip surgery about a month ago. After surgery, I looked at my X-rays. As is usually the case, even if you trust the doctor and stay calm, your brain starts scanning the image for meaning without actually having the expertise to interpret it.

I looked at the X-rays and honestly could not tell what I was looking at or what the actual issue was.

After the appointment, I had that familiar combination of relief and worry. You meet with a doctor, get a 30-second summary, ask a couple of questions, then go home and realize you have two more.

So I uploaded the X-rays to an AI chatbot and asked, without additional context, whether it saw any abnormalities.

It gave me a clear explanation that aligned with what my doctor had told me. It was not a replacement for the doctor. It was reinforcement.

It helped remove some of the fear from the situation and gave me language I could use to think about it. In that moment, it mattered. My doctor made the diagnosis and treatment plan. AI was the partner that helped me process it at my own pace. It gave me insight beyond my expertise in terms I could understand, exactly when I needed it.

That is relevant, and I will come back to it.

For anyone curious, the issue was a small bump on the femur head that had to be removed. It was very hard to see with an untrained eye.

My name is Eric Stromberg. I’m an associate director and team manager at HLB Lighting Design. I’m an electrical engineer and an automation enthusiast almost by accident. These days, I spend most of my time helping design teams figure out how to use new technology in ways that make work better rather than more complicated.

I do not come from the perspective of having all the answers. I come from having tried a lot of things, broken a lot of workflows, and learned from very smart people. Today I want to share some of those lessons.

I do not come from the perspective of having all the answers. I come from having tried a lot of things, broken a lot of workflows, and learned from very smart people. Today I want to share some of those lessons.

HLB Lighting Design is the world’s largest women-owned independent architectural lighting design firm. Founded in 1968, we have more than 50 years of experience in how people experience the built environment. We have 10 studios across North America and the Middle East and are growing in Europe. We have nearly 200 professionals serving 20 market sectors. Our services include architectural lighting design, sustainability, visualization, and custom product development.

There are three key insights I hope you take home from this talk:

Urgency comes from relevance, not fear.

Pilots don’t scale—recipes do.

Give ownership, not tasks.

Our industry shares a few defining traits: it is knowledge-based, deeply proud of its craft, and very fast-moving.

Nothing threatens professional identity faster than hearing someone say they can do the same task faster, with less stress, and with better results.

Think of a carpenter who has used a hammer for 20 years. Then someone arrives with a shiny screwdriver. The new person shows off; the experienced person feels the threat and grips the hammer more tightly. Over time, they will learn from each other—the new one will learn to use a hammer, and the experienced one will eventually incorporate the screwdriver—but the time it takes them to meet can vary greatly.

When we introduce AI using fear, we trigger resistance.

During our Copilot onboarding sessions early last year, after covering GenAI fundamentals, compliance, structured data, and reverse engineering, I showed the team an image and asked whether they thought it was AI-generated.

The responses were about 50/50, which was what I expected. But what was remarkable was not the split—it was the energy in the discussion when I asked why.

These were lighting designers. They argued passionately about balance between daylight and internal lighting, whether the structured surface in the back looked plausible, and whether the fixtures were positioned correctly.

Then I told them the image had been generated by Copilot.

That was when I realized that urgency does not come from fear. It comes from relevance. If someone sees why it matters to their craft, they engage.

Then I told them the image had been generated by Copilot.

That was when I realized that urgency does not come from fear. It comes from relevance. If someone sees why it matters to their craft, they engage.

The real leadership question is not, “How do we roll out AI?” It is, “How do we help each person hold on to the pride they already have?”

That means tuning the message carefully. If you are too quiet, people ignore it. If you are too loud, you create resistance. The leaders who succeed are the ones who tune the message to the specific craft.

A recent MIT-affiliated study showed that 95% of organizations across industries are not seeing measurable returns from AI. Only 5% are extracting meaningful value.

So if AI feels frustrating, you are not behind. You are normal.

Most firms are stuck not because they lack ambition or intelligence, but because they have run pilots as isolated events focused on tasks, instead of designing new systems and processes end to end.

Pilots prove that something can work, but a recipe makes sure it works again—with different people, different projects, and different data.

When we started our pilots in early 2024, we thought we were being smart. We wanted something scalable, measurable, and intuitive. So we created a pilot workflow: identify the task, prepare the data, develop the prompts, execute, evaluate, and document.

What we did not understand was that these steps are not equally weighted.

In reality, data preparation dominates everything. Not the tools. Not the prompts. Data.

AI readiness turned out to be less about intelligence and more about organization.

Let me give you a concrete example. I went looking for the best proposals from our best projects because proposals seem like a natural AI task. They are high effort, repeatable, and time-consuming.

But the reality of our folders and file structures showed we were not ready. Should I trust the latest modified date? Should I ask the project manager? Which file is actually the right one?

So we deliberately decided not to pursue proposals as one of our first AI tasks—not because AI could not handle them, but because we were not ready.

That insight matters. As many of you know, collecting and managing qualitative data is one of the hardest parts of starting and scaling AI.

That insight matters. As many of you know, collecting and managing qualitative data is one of the hardest parts of starting and scaling AI.

During our pilots, we used a few key metrics. I tried to imagine what someone on the board would need to know before investing more money in the initiative.

We focused on three measures that most executives understand: time, effort, and quality, comparing non-AI and AI-assisted work.

These metrics were easy to measure, easy to communicate, and easy to revisit—not just at the end of a pilot, but later as tools evolved, which they obviously do.

As development sped up, we added a fourth measure: value alignment.

That is not a nice-to-have. Speed without value introduces risk.

For example, one of our core values at HLB is integrity. That means agentic AI can never be the sole decision-maker in talent selection.

Whether you buy tools, build them, or mix the two, what keeps progress from turning into chaos is a repeatable recipe for evaluating them.

What is interesting is that these metrics start to feel familiar, because they resemble the way you evaluate a person before hiring them: experience, knowledge, attention to detail, and cultural fit.

As we move toward autonomous agents, AI stops being a tool you use occasionally and starts to look more like a colleague or teammate that you manage.

If you hired 50 new employees, you would not simply assign them tasks. You would redesign management, training, review, and accountability.

That brings us to ownership.

With a traditional leadership style, if you assign tasks, you often get compliance. If you give ownership, you can get stewardship.

Because all of this is moving so quickly, we wanted to enable fast iterations by turning people into intrapreneurs.

Within predefined boundaries and with knowledge of our AI policy, we gave them enough room for individual experimentation, alongside frequent updates.

During our firmwide Copilot onboarding, we told everyone: “You are now a supervisor. This tool is your new colleague. What it produces, you are responsible for.”

Our AI champions—our guiding coalition—were set up with that in mind.

We defined their role as carrying updates from the strategic team back to their studios, and then feeding studio experience back to the broader champion group. They represent a group of people, not just themselves.

They are not merely prompt writers. They are feedback loops.

We defined their role as carrying updates from the strategic team back to their studios, and then feeding studio experience back to the broader champion group. They represent a group of people, not just themselves.

They are not merely prompt writers. They are feedback loops.

If you do not yet have an AI champions group, that is my recommendation. They are mostly volunteers, they speak honestly about both pros and cons, and they help protect others from pitfalls.

From my experience implementing software and processes—and especially now in the AI era—I believe a successful organizational structure needs three elements.

First, an executive sponsor: someone in the C-suite or management who understands risk, reputation, and long-term firm health.

Second, an AI council: ideally one representative from each department, but kept small—perhaps no more than 10 people. This group should prioritize processes, not just tools, and should be willing to be unpopular in the short term.

Third, and most importantly, an adoption owner in the center. The title matters less than the authority: AI transformation lead, head of AI enablement, digital change lead. What matters is that this person has clarity and authority.

This is the person who wakes up asking why pilots are not turning into habits, and what needs documentation, upskilling, or training.

The right profile for this role is hybrid: both technical and operational, deeply trusted internally, and a process thinker first and technologist second.

If that person has to ask permission for everything, the role is decorative. If they can make decisions, it is transformative.

So the sponsor sets the why, the council sets the what, and the adoption owner figures out the how.

To summarize the three key ideas:

Make AI meaningful for the people doing the work by tuning into relevance.

Make it repeatable by investing in a system for evaluation.

Empower teams to carry the work forward by giving ownership.

Leadership is not about having all the answers. It is about making it easier for others to win.

To close, if you had to approve next year’s budget and choose between more AI tools or better adoption systems, which would move ROI faster?

Leadership is not about having all the answers. It is about making it easier for others to win.

To close, if you had to approve next year’s budget and choose between more AI tools or better adoption systems, which would move ROI faster?

Most firms would probably choose more AI tools, because it sounds exciting and may come with fewer meetings. But our experience suggests the better strategic choice is adoption systems, because they are rare and because, as with my X-ray, the breakthrough was not that the technology existed. It was knowing when and how to use it.

Audience question: What role does leadership play in sustaining momentum after the initial excitement of an AI pilot fades? How do you keep adoption efforts from stalling?

Eric Stromberg: I think it comes back to the AI champions and the communication chain they create. You need champions who engage the people they work with and maintain a clear flow of communication between the strategic group and the people doing the work. Frequent updates matter more than large, infrequent updates. Again, the goal is to enable fast communication and fast iteration.

Joaquin Arakena: Thank you for being here before lunch. We’ll keep this concise. We want to talk about what we did together—the good, the bad, and the ugly.

It has been reassuring to hear other firms share many of the same problems. That tells me I’m at the right conference.
