I’m Joaquin from Proyectos. I’m a partner there, and I’m here with Abel Embrechts from Macadamia Labs. We’re going to talk about what we built together over the past year and share some insights.

Proyectos is a multidisciplinary design firm of roughly 100 people, depending on how you define small versus midsize in your market. We are based in Uruguay and work across the Americas. We are engineers and architects, and we were smart enough to know that we could not do AI by ourselves.

So I went to LinkedIn, looked through my network, and found these two gentlemen—now friends—from Macadamia Labs.

Abel Embrechts: We are a Y Combinator-backed startup focused on speeding up engineering deliverables for engineering firms, particularly in industrial infrastructure, of which Proyectos is a great example.

We have been working with Proyectos for about a year and have implemented four workflows.

The first is quality-assurance checks on technical documentation, such as isometrics, verifying whether design files comply with the basis of design.

The second is chatting with technical standards—making it easy to navigate complex technical documentation.

The third is RFQ vendor comparison. There are many vendors and many files that need to be compared quickly, so we use AI to provide engineers with a clear overview.

The fourth gets deeper into a software platform Proyectos uses, called Plant 3D. We built small automations for repetitive workflows—spooling, tagging, and quality assurance checks directly within the design software.

For those less familiar with AI agents, these are systems that can read unstructured or technical documents and use existing tools such as AutoCAD and Excel.

At a very high level, our platform takes in your technical documents and the tools your engineers already use during design. We then create custom workflows with custom interfaces based on how the firm operates. The end goal is faster engineering deliverables, whether that means QA reports or more detailed design outputs.

Joaquin Arakena: Let me describe the problem we were trying to solve.

Joaquin Arakena: Let me describe the problem we were trying to solve.

We do integrated design with all disciplines together in-house. Each discipline believes it is the most important, wants priority, and wants ownership of its BIM model.

Each discipline also has its own recipe—its own tools and ways of working—and each one is responsible for its deliverables. You cannot simply tell engineers exactly how to work.

We work through the full lifecycle, from early design to procurement to construction and commissioning. We are looking at AI across that full lifecycle, not just in design, even though design is the obvious low-hanging fruit.

Mistakes are very expensive. If something is wrong in a BIM model, you can change it. If something is wrong in the real world, someone can get hurt or killed. You cannot say, “Our new AI agent made a mistake.” So error checking and risk management are always present.

Every project manager and engineering designer also believes that their project is different. Anyone who has worked in integrated design has heard that before. That makes standardization difficult.

What we did was examine eight workflows and deploy four. We now have 54 users and 24 repeat users. We would like that repeat-user number to go up. That is where we stand today.

Let me share a few business insights—what worked, what did not, and what the results have been.

First: do not automate chaos.

We thought we were going to automate piping design processes. When we asked how piping design was actually being done, we discovered six different processes. We are not even that large a company—we probably have around 30 piping designers—but somehow we had six different ways of doing the same thing.

We learned that only when we tried to hand the work over to an agent.

So one major benefit was simply mapping our processes. That delivered value before the AI itself.

Second: error detection is part of the design process. You cannot just ask a system to produce something. It also needs to support checking whether that output is correct.

Third: the actual piping engineers need to help design the agent together with our external partner, because they are responsible for the result. You cannot hand a tool to a professional and simply say, “Please make sure you use this.”

Third: the actual piping engineers need to help design the agent together with our external partner, because they are responsible for the result. You cannot hand a tool to a professional and simply say, “Please make sure you use this.”

Fourth: adoption is the hardest part. It is easy to say, “I want an agent—please build it.” It is much harder to get other people to use it consistently.

Another major insight was to start with the business case. At the beginning, our mindset was often, “AI can do that, so let’s do it.” Later we realized that some of those tasks only happened once every four years. We had to ask whether automation still made sense.

So we developed criteria to evaluate ideas properly.

Another big lesson was to get ready to change your pricing strategy. If you are selling hours, AI disrupts your effort model. That is a tricky situation.

But the benefits have been real.

We showed a graph of the last eight years at Proyectos. In 2017 onward, sales were rising, billable hours were rising, and EBITDA was rising—but the growth was tied to selling more hours.

Last year was the first year in which we sold more with fewer hours.

We did not fire anybody. We simply spent more hours working with our partners and our agents.

So what do we sell now?

We are not only selling hours anymore. Sometimes we still do, because some clients insist on that model. But increasingly we are selling results.

That is a major cultural shift—for us, for our clients, and for our people.

And the results are there. This is not just because of four agents. It is also because AI forced us to confront the weakness of an hours-based pricing model and move more of our work to fixed-price projects. That improved our results.

What do our people think?

We surveyed roughly 80% of our design team.

Reported productivity gains were strong.

One area that concerned me as a manager was risk perception. Engineers largely reported low perceived risk. That worries me. I would actually prefer a bit more caution. If my engineers are not perceiving risk, I need to work on that.

Reported productivity gains were strong.

One area that concerned me as a manager was risk perception. Engineers largely reported low perceived risk. That worries me. I would actually prefer a bit more caution. If my engineers are not perceiving risk, I need to work on that.

We also tracked whether people were worried about losing their jobs. About 14% expressed concern. This is the first year we have measured that, so we will see how it changes. My sense is that many engineers have already realized that the greater risk is not using AI, rather than using it.

Another interesting result was what people were using AI for. The most surprising use case was learning and research.

We work across industries—oil and gas, semiconductors, pulp and paper—and each has different standards, such as how far supports should be spaced in piping. AI helped electrical and piping engineers move into new sectors much more quickly. That was a very interesting finding.

Abel Embrechts: Let me share some technical insights. Over the past year, we learned a lot in four areas: creating trust, handling quality and noise, co-designing with engineers, and driving adoption.

First, trust. Everyone knows LLMs hallucinate. We therefore tried to design the product in a way that engineers could trust.

Two basic methods helped. One is citations to source material. If the system flags an error in an isometric drawing, it should point to the evidence. If it finds no errors, it should still show what area it investigated.

The other is transparency through reasoning traces. We want to be open about what the agent is doing and how it is thinking, so the engineer can judge whether the logic is sound.

Second, quality and noise. AI produces a lot of output. For example, if we do a quick QA check, the system might return 50 to 80 points. The challenge is how to help engineers review that quickly, rather than slowing them down.

One lesson is to build around state-of-the-art models. When we started working with Proyectos, we were using earlier-generation models; by now, the performance gains in construction-related tasks are significant. If you build custom systems on top of strong frontier models, your solutions improve as the models improve.

One lesson is to build around state-of-the-art models. When we started working with Proyectos, we were using earlier-generation models; by now, the performance gains in construction-related tasks are significant. If you build custom systems on top of strong frontier models, your solutions improve as the models improve.

Another lesson is to use the company’s existing SOPs. Many firms already have strong standard procedures for reviewing drawings and generating reports—processes often used by interns. If you make that knowledge available to the agents, performance improves substantially.

UX design is also critical. We often build custom interfaces so that reviewing the outputs is straightforward.

Joaquin Arakena: The RFQ comparison interface we showed was probably the fourth version. Our project managers kept saying, “I don’t like it. Show me something else. I showed it to my client and they think it is too complex.” So we kept iterating.

That is one reason this partnership mattered. The version we are showing now is not the first iteration. A lot of lessons are embedded in it.

Abel Embrechts: We also observed that adoption is much higher when we integrate directly with the software engineers already use. For example, with Plant 3D, engineers do not want to export a PDF of an isometric just to check it. They want to run the check directly inside the software. That creates much more value.

Small things also matter for adoption, such as making it easy to export reports, share outputs internally, and access resources from one library.

Finally, the most-used use cases almost always came from close collaboration with engineers inside the firm. When an engineer from the organization proposes the idea, adoption is much stronger.

Joaquin Arakena: Exactly. If I go to an electrical engineer and say, “I have an idea for you,” the first question is likely to be, “How many electrical design projects have you done?” If the answer is zero, they are not going to care.

Sometimes you need to suggest ideas, but if someone originates the idea themselves, the chances of adoption are much higher.

Audience question: You mentioned using a state-of-the-art model like Gemini. How do you bridge the gap between very deep domain expertise and a general model?

Abel Embrechts: That is the key opportunity. Every company has deep internal knowledge that is not available to public LLMs because it lives behind closed doors. That is where the value lies.

Abel Embrechts: That is the key opportunity. Every company has deep internal knowledge that is not available to public LLMs because it lives behind closed doors. That is where the value lies.

As the models get better, your internal domain knowledge becomes even more powerful. If that knowledge exists in actual files—SOPs, PDFs, or other formats—it can be used to guide the models effectively.

We often describe these systems as increasingly smart interns. But what makes them useful is access to the firm’s proprietary expertise.

Also, because the design process is so complex, we break it down into many smaller building blocks and evaluate performance at that level. That helps us build the confidence needed before putting something in front of an engineer for verification.

Audience question: Your comments about user experience resonated with me. How long did it take to go from the first version of your design to the fourth, and how did you handle diverse expectations? Is the fourth version really what users wanted, or is this a never-ending journey?

Joaquin Arakena: The honest answer is that it is a never-ending journey.

We care most about quick iterations. Much of the collaboration has been remote—our design team is mostly in Uruguay, while our technical partners have been working from Silicon Valley and Europe. In fact, this conference is the first time we are meeting in person, which is interesting in itself.

What we tell engineers is: say what you want, but do not expect the first result to be exactly right. Get something out there and then give feedback.

So our priority is not perfection in the first version. Our priority is speed of iteration and making sure the output adds value to our clients.

That is the most important test—not whether one of our engineers likes it in isolation, but whether it solves a problem for the client.

You can easily end up with two engineers talking all day and iterating something that does not matter. The client must remain part of the conversation.

One final point: our average age at Proyectos is about 26. We used to describe ourselves as an infrastructure design company that uses technology. Now we increasingly think of ourselves as a technology company that does infrastructure design.

One final point: our average age at Proyectos is about 26. We used to describe ourselves as an infrastructure design company that uses technology. Now we increasingly think of ourselves as a technology company that does infrastructure design.

It used to be BIM. Now it is AI with BIM. People in their mid-20s are very excited about that. That makes some aspects easier, though of course it will not be the same for every firm.

Alexei, a project engineer in structural steel design and AI ambassador at Sweco Finland, presented a prototype that integrates AI into the daily workflow of structural engineers using Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis.

He explained that when he says “daily workflow,” he means something engineers can benefit from literally every day. In his experience, many AI solutions are text-oriented, while engineers often spend their day inside professional software performing actions such as copying, moving, mirroring, dragging, and trimming rather than writing text. His goal was to let AI “push the buttons” for engineers.

He described the technical approach as using tool calls: asking an AI model to respond in a specific format, then having software parse that output and make changes in the engineering application based on the response. He built this by taking Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis, coding tools for it, creating a small chat interface, and connecting it to AI.

A live demo was attempted, but repeated network and connection issues prevented it from running reliably. Alexei noted that the prepared presentation also contained GIFs of the intended demonstrations, so he continued using those instead.

Using the presentation examples, he showed that a user can send text prompts to the assistant and have the AI modify the structural model opened in Robot. One example was asking the system to make a steel truss steeper by changing its slope. He noted that response times are typically around 30 seconds to one minute because the model generates the solution token by token.

He then discussed where these AI-assisted engineering applications perform especially well. First, they are very strong at data analysis tasks such as “find me all…” queries. He attributed this to the attention mechanism in large language models. In simplified terms, when the model receives tokens in context, it evaluates their importance and compresses them into a weighted summary. When users ask it to locate or identify specific items, it is effectively using that attention mechanism to highlight the most relevant tokens.

Second, the applications also work well when generating content from scratch. He explained that large language models are trained on long token sequences where each next token makes sense relative to the previous summary, so they are naturally good at producing similar coherent sequences. He had planned to demonstrate generating a steel hall from scratch, but due to the failed live demo he relied on the GIFs in the slides instead.

Alexei then turned to the challenges. He emphasized that this is a fast-moving area and that some of the problems he encountered may already be less severe with newer models. He framed this section as more of a historical debrief based on his own implementation experience.

The first challenge was what he called “task understanding” hallucinations. He gave an example of a building with six load-bearing frames that he wanted to convert into a building with five load-bearing frames while keeping the same overall length. In the failed case, the AI understood the geometry in general but hallucinated intermediate bars that should not exist.

He attributed this to the nature of model training. Large language models are trained first on internet-scale data and then on a comparatively small set of instruction examples. There is far less engineering content online than general content, so the model’s weights are not naturally tuned for many engineering-specific transformations.

His solution was to change the context by breaking a complex instruction into a sequence of basic tasks. Instead of saying, “Convert this building to a five-frame building,” he would decompose the job into simpler operations: find all load-bearing frames, confirm there are six, delete the first frame, then move the remaining frames so the building keeps the same total length. He found that the model handled these basic operations—find, move, copy, create, delete—very well.

The second challenge was what he described as a “messed up output” problem, where no matter what the user asked, the model would corrupt the software model and still behave as if it had succeeded. He believed this was caused by overloaded context.

The second challenge was what he described as a “messed up output” problem, where no matter what the user asked, the model would corrupt the software model and still behave as if it had succeeded. He believed this was caused by overloaded context.

He explained that even if the user stays within the model’s allowed token limit, context quality still matters. Since all context is compressed into a weighted summary, too many low-value or irrelevant tokens can pollute that summary. He illustrated this with an experiment using a small local language model. When he asked it to draw a triangle with a simple prompt, it correctly produced three points. But when he added several pages of “useful” extra information about geometric shapes and coordinate systems, the model became confused and sometimes produced only two points or four points instead of three.

His conclusion was that the context must be kept clean. There is no need to expose thousands of tools, include full software manuals, or overwhelm the prompt with system instructions, tool definitions, output formatting rules, and other noise. If all of that is compressed into the same summary, the model may focus on JSON formatting or tool syntax rather than the substance of the user’s request.

In closing, Alexei argued that AI can deliver value in engineering for anyone who spends their day operating software to produce design packages under deadline pressure. He said there is work for multiple roles in making this practical:

- Developers need to write plugins and tools for the engineering software people already use and connect those tools to AI.
- AI ambassadors or AI integration leads need to educate colleagues, especially non-coders, because many still see AI as a black box.
- Engineers themselves need to stay open-minded and be willing to change how they work, much as people adapted when the internet became widespread.

He estimated that even relatively small assistants can save one to four days per month per employee, depending on how much they are used. Scaled to a division of 500 people, even if only 20–30% of employees use the tools, the savings could amount to thousands of hours—roughly 4,000 to 10,000 hours—which he said is a substantial amount of engineering effort in structural steel design.

During Q&A, one attendee asked whether the same outcome could have been achieved with copy and paste. Alexei replied that his target use case is not repetitive duplication but model modification. He described scenarios in which a customer asks to extend a building by a few meters, which in finite element software requires many manual adjustments: moving walls, fixing nodes that do not follow connected geometry, and updating detailed components. He said copy-paste helps when repeating geometry, but his prototype is aimed at the tedious modification work common in detailing and design development. Based on stopwatch measurements, he estimated time savings of about five to ten minutes per request, depending on complexity.

Another question asked whether the prototype was already being used within Sweco. Alexei said that this year’s target is broader introduction and adoption. The tool had been released in a testing phase, some colleagues had tried it, and at least one person was already using it in production. He said the goal for the year was to introduce this way of working across the business unit, and that Autodesk Robot was only one of several programs in which they hope to enable similar workflows.

Dan Eng, a MicroStation product expert at Bentley, and Dr. Louis Martin Lozier, Senior Product Manager for AI in MicroStation, presented Bentley’s AI-driven workflows for accelerating infrastructure project delivery.

Dan opened with the broader market context. Citing a forecast from McKinsey, he said that by September 2025, around $106 trillion in investment was expected over the next 15 years to meet demand for new and upgraded infrastructure. He noted that everyone in the room is part of the ecosystem needed to deliver that work.

He argued that infrastructure projects are becoming larger, more complex, and subject to tighter deadlines, while traditional workflows are no longer sufficient to keep up. The challenge is compounded by rapid urbanization, aging infrastructure, climate resilience pressures, and a shortage of engineers in the workforce.

Bentley’s stated vision is to empower the next generation of infrastructure design by leveraging AI to accelerate project delivery without compromising quality. Their goals were to develop purpose-built AI solutions for infrastructure professionals and to create context-aware AI tools and features that address users’ most pressing problems.

Louis Martin emphasized that infrastructure generates huge amounts of data, but AI can only use that data effectively when it is standardized, connected, and contextualized. He said Bentley Infrastructure Cloud is intended to connect and contextualize data so that AI can produce meaningful and reliable outcomes. He also stressed that engineering requires trustworthy AI because precision and consequences matter. AI in infrastructure must remain human-in-the-loop.

He explained Bentley’s AI principles around data governance and trust. Unlike some software vendors, Bentley’s position is that users’ data remains their data. Their approach to data sharing is opt-in rather than opt-out. Users retain control over whether and how their data is used for AI, whether it contributes to model training that benefits others, and Bentley aims to be transparent about how AI models are created and what data was used. He acknowledged that this limits how fast Bentley can move, but said it is foundational to responsible AI development and user trust.

Dan then described Bentley’s continuous discovery process. Bentley held workshops in Kansas City and London with product managers, UX designers, developers, product experts, and users. Through interviews and user journey mapping, they focused on real pain points in infrastructure project delivery. They asked users where workflow bottlenecks occur, where delays happen in daily work, and which tasks consume the most time.

From those workshops, Bentley identified several recurring themes:

- Manual processes dominate and create long delays.
- Knowledge gaps between peers and colleagues slow decision-making.
- Drawing production and labeling remain major bottlenecks and often lead to costly rework.

Dan recalled one engineer summarizing the issue by saying, “This isn’t an engineering problem, it’s a time problem.”

From this discovery process, Bentley organized its work into three themes:

1. Automation
2. Learning and help
3. Drawing production

Under automation, Bentley introduced a Python editor and Python Assistant inside MicroStation. These are intended to enable scripting and workflow automation for manual tasks, freeing engineers to focus on higher-value work.

Under learning and help, Bentley introduced Bentley Copilot to support onboarding and contextual guidance. Under drawing production, Bentley developed AI-assisted annotation and labeling tools.

Louis Martin then walked through a series of demos.

First, he showed an example in which an engineer needs to place safety barriers along a complex roadway. Traditionally, this would involve hours of repetitive manual work and rework whenever the alignment changes. With the Python Assistant, the engineer describes the intent in natural language, and AI generates a script that places the barrier automatically and aligns it to the design geometry. If the road changes, the engineer can rerun the script in seconds. Louis Martin characterized this as democratizing automation because the user does not need to be a programmer; they can simply ask for the automation they need.

A second example focused on dimensioning, one of the most repetitive and time-consuming parts of drawing production. Engineers often spend hours placing and updating dimensions manually, especially as designs evolve. Using the Python Assistant, the engineer describes the desired dimensioning behavior and AI helps generate a script that applies dimensions automatically based on the model geometry. When the design changes, the dimensions can be updated consistently by rerunning the script. He noted that after that, the engineer can still manually adjust the dimensions if needed.

Another example addressed onboarding and software transition. He described an engineer familiar with AutoCAD joining a project in MicroStation. Even with experience, switching software can reduce productivity. With Bentley Copilot, the user provides their background and intent, and Copilot uses contextual memory to translate concepts, commands, and terminology into something familiar. It guides the user step by step within the active design context, including where the tools are located and how equivalent terminology differs between AutoCAD and MicroStation. The result is faster onboarding, less disruption, and more confident execution.

Louis Martin also demonstrated a compliance-oriented use case. An engineer working as a consultant for the Florida Department of Transportation needs to ensure that a design complies with FDOT standards. Using Bentley Copilot, the engineer can attach the FDOT manual as a PDF and ask Copilot to compare the project’s level naming conventions against the standard. Copilot reviews the file and highlights where the design is compliant and where it is not. Instead of manual checking, the engineer receives immediate, actionable feedback before submission, reducing downstream rework.

A drawing production example in OpenRoads Designer showed an intersection with standard annotations that were difficult to read and inconsistent. Adjusting labels manually would take time and likely create rework. Using an AI label optimizer, labels were automatically repositioned and refined for clarity based on a trained annotation model. The engineer then used Bentley Copilot to ask how to annotate specific geometry, run a geometry report, and query applicable design standards, such as minimum design speed based on traffic volume. The combination of AI-driven labeling and contextual assistance produced clearer, more standards-aligned drawings more quickly and with fewer manual edits.

A final example in OpenSite Plus focused on site civil design. An engineer designing a land development project needs to move quickly from concept to detailed design without switching tools or losing context. In OpenSite Plus, data can be brought in easily and geometry created contextually—for example, generating a pad site based on required size and slope, then positioning it precisely using northing and easting coordinates. In the demonstration, the terrain was re-optimized, which reduced earthwork and potentially saved project cost.

Dan concluded that Bentley’s AI-powered features provide value through:

- Automation with Python scripting and AI assistance using natural language
- AI-powered drawing production support
- Copilot assistance that reduces manual information retrieval and supports optimized analysis and layout decisions

Dan concluded that Bentley’s AI-powered features provide value through:

- Automation with Python scripting and AI assistance using natural language
- AI-powered drawing production support
- Copilot assistance that reduces manual information retrieval and supports optimized analysis and layout decisions

He emphasized that these are purpose-built AI tools for infrastructure delivery, that AI-assisted design is not about replacing engineers, and that the industry is still only at the beginning of what can be unlocked.

In Q&A, an attendee asked how Bentley trains systems if customer data remains private and cannot be used freely for training. Louis Martin responded that Bentley relies on internal expertise and curated materials. Bentley has many subject matter experts and experienced engineers who create design examples and training content. The same applies to documentation and learning content: they create validated question-and-answer sets and train models from those. In some scenarios, users can also provide their own documents, such as PDFs, which the system can ingest and query without turning that into Bentley-owned training data.

Another attendee asked about distributing Python snippets or components created through this workflow. Dan replied that users can create their own Python scripts, and Bentley provides API access and a GitHub repository with approximately 170 examples to help users get started. Additional examples are added with MicroStation releases. If users want to contribute, they would likely do so through GitHub.

Peter, CEO and co-founder of VIKTOR, presented how VIKTOR supports engineering automation through Python-based web apps and an app store model, and demonstrated how AI-assisted “vibe coding” can dramatically speed up development.

He introduced VIKTOR’s mission as empowering engineers to build, connect, and share their own tools. He emphasized that he is an engineer himself and that before founding VIKTOR with friends from Delft University of Technology, they had started a bridge-building company that manufactured fiber-reinforced plastic bridges and fully automated much of that process. That engineering background shaped the platform.

When VIKTOR started in 2017, the team believed the platform needed a few core building blocks:

- It should be Python-based.
- Engineers should be able to create web apps so tools are easy to share.
- Those apps should live in an app store so engineers can discover and use them.

Over roughly eight years, VIKTOR grew to around 100,000 apps on the platform and is used on major projects and by large engineering firms and contractors around the world.

Peter said the company learned that automation makes engineers much more productive, but there was a major bottleneck: skills and time. Python is powerful and effective, but only a small percentage of engineers in most organizations can write good Python code. He showed this as a persistent gap across experience levels.

That became the major opportunity for VIKTOR. Because Python is so widespread, it is well represented in the data used to train large language models. VIKTOR recognized early that AI could help bridge the skills gap. Around three years ago, shortly after ChatGPT launched, the company began experimenting with an internal initiative called “Hector” as a play on the company name.

To demonstrate why this matters, Peter returned to his background in bridge design. Designing one of their fiber-reinforced plastic bridges involved several activities:

- Structural analysis, often in Excel
- Reporting
- Production documentation

He used that workflow as the basis for a live demonstration.

Before building the app, he briefly showed the VIKTOR platform: a project structure, development environment, app store, user management, and integration layer for both local and cloud systems.

He then loaded a prompt into VIKTOR’s app builder instead of coding from scratch. He explained that, as an engineer, he thought about the design process step by step and encoded that logic in a prompt:

He then loaded a prompt into VIKTOR’s app builder instead of coding from scratch. He explained that, as an engineer, he thought about the design process step by step and encoded that logic in a prompt:

- Define a cross-section with top, bottom, sides, and webs.
- Visualize that geometry and make it parameterized.
- Determine stiffness, using material properties relevant to fiber-reinforced plastic.
- Combine the 2D geometry and the mechanical properties of each layer to derive the mechanical properties of the bridge as a whole.
- Determine bridge deformation according to a relevant standard.

At that moment, VIKTOR was using Anthropic Claude 4.6. Peter explained that the LLM receives not only the prompt but also all the context VIKTOR has built over the years: the software development kit, geometry tools, reporting tools, and other engineering-specific functionality. Within VIKTOR’s own instance, the model then generates Python code that is specific to the VIKTOR framework.

The result was a working app that let him define mechanical properties, calculate bending stiffness, and calculate bending deformation. He described this as effectively replacing an Excel sheet, but in a more powerful and shareable form.

Peter then extended the app further. He added new capabilities through additional prompting:

- Create a 3D geometry of the bridge
- Include an arch in the bridge geometry rather than a flat profile
- Add a person for scale
- Enable geometry download
- Generate reporting automatically
- Fix a bug

He said this transformed the tool from an Excel replacement into something much more capable.

He also demonstrated adding an LLM directly into the app as an agentic component. This is built into VIKTOR, so when a user logs in they can add an LLM to the app itself. One reason for doing so is trust and transparency. Users may want to understand what the app does rather than treat it as a black box. He showed the app being asked to explain what it does, what design guidelines it uses, and to list all thirty vectors. This illustrated how users can interact not only with the app interface but also with the app logic through natural language.

He noted that VIKTOR also supports collaborative prompting: one engineer can begin building an app and another can continue prompting and refining it.

He noted that VIKTOR also supports collaborative prompting: one engineer can begin building an app and another can continue prompting and refining it.

Peter then showed a more developed version of the application with a stepwise editor for bridge design, generation of 3D geometry, and automatic reporting. The report could be generated without a template, or with a template if desired.

Because of time, he skipped a more advanced demo but described another capability: integrating VIKTOR apps with both local and cloud software. He said engineers can build apps on top of local software while simultaneously integrating cloud platforms such as Autodesk Construction Cloud. In other words, users can “vibe code” an app that connects to both on-premise and cloud systems.

He summarized VIKTOR’s broader direction as not just making individual apps, but orchestrating many small, valuable components into larger workflows. Engineers can create multiple focused apps and then coordinate them into broader agentic processes—for example, a civil design workflow involving geotechnical and structural components.

On what differentiates VIKTOR, Peter highlighted several aspects:

- An engineering-focused DNA in the founding team
- A software development kit built specifically for engineering
- Emphasis on deterministic tools rather than probabilistic outputs
- Infrastructure designed for project-based work
- Integrations with Autodesk, ACC, Nemetschek, Trimble, Bentley, and others
- Support for both cloud and on-premise deployment
- Engineering-grade validation, quality assurance, and transparency
- Support structures tailored to engineering use cases

During Q&A, one attendee asked whether VIKTOR had been applied beyond structural analysis. Peter said yes: while his demo was specific to one domain, VIKTOR is used across business lines in major engineering firms, including water engineering, environmental engineering, geotechnical engineering, process engineering, and by contractors and asset owners as well.

Another audience member raised a broader concern: if engineers rely on code they did not write or fully understand, especially in safety-critical fields like bridge design, is there a danger that future civil engineers may not really understand what they are doing? Peter acknowledged the concern and distinguished between programmers and engineers. He said VIKTOR’s audience is civil and other domain engineers, not software specialists, and that the platform is designed to automate the boring work so engineers can focus on more meaningful tasks. He also noted the industry-wide shortage of engineers and the growing complexity of societal challenges such as climate adaptation, housing shortages, and aging infrastructure. He argued that the profession needs tools that fit the current era, while maintaining traceability, determinism, and transparency.

A further question concerned compliance, given that apps are built with the help of non-deterministic LLMs but need to be used in real-world, regulated environments. Peter responded that VIKTOR has over eight years of experience with engineering apps being used on major infrastructure projects. The company already knows how to make apps reliable. The new challenge is to provide the right governance structure around AI-assisted app creation. He said VIKTOR supports workflows such as sharing code with a colleague for peer review, publishing to a testing environment, validating with a larger group, and only then promoting an app to production. There are multiple stages before something becomes a verified production app.

Geron Niemi from Granlund presented a demo on AI agents for construction product selection, with a focus on cost, carbon reporting, and reusing workflow knowledge.

He framed the problem around the construction phase, where product selection workflows are repeated again and again. The core question was how to turn these repeated selections into a reusable flow, while also storing what has been learned so that later projects can benefit from earlier work.

He briefly introduced Granlund’s broader data platform and then outlined the agenda: the product selection dilemma, data challenges, how the demo was built, the role of human skills versus agent skills, and then the live demo itself.

He explained that in a typical project, the upstream inputs include design documents, IFC models, drawings, price lists, product documents, EPDs, and other files. These cannot usually be used directly, so they must be preprocessed. AI is one possible solution for this preprocessing step.

Once the information has been parsed and understood, data from documents and drawings can be combined into a larger whole and stored in a process database. From there, it can support cost calculation, carbon calculation, scheduling, design steering, procurement, logistics, and eventually handover into facility management after the building is complete.

Geron described the current “product selection dilemma” as a multi-criteria problem. One common issue is local optimization: it is easy to choose the first product that appears to tick all the boxes, even if it is not the best solution overall.

He also noted that designers often do not have all the necessary data available when selecting products. In particular, life-cycle assessment data and pricing information may not be accessible to them. As a result, product selection is often based more on prior habits, precedent, or intuition than on complete data. In some cases there is feedback from previous projects, but often there is not, which means lessons learned are not carried forward.

He then discussed the underlying data problem. Product information is spread across many documents, the data is often not clean, there is no common ontology, and previous data cannot easily be reused. There is also no continuous digital flow: people repeatedly fill in documents and forms across multiple systems.

To address this, he proposed the combination of MCPs and AI agents. He said that while command-line tools are also becoming popular, the essential ingredients for this type of system are:

To address this, he proposed the combination of MCPs and AI agents. He said that while command-line tools are also becoming popular, the essential ingredients for this type of system are:

- A general AI agent
- Tools to understand and work with files
- A way to read IFC files and extract information
- A PDF reader for the many documents that come in PDF format
- A mechanism, such as an MCP setup, to share and scale the same solution within an organization

Geron then introduced the idea of “skills.” Traditionally, human skills are learned through doing and training. In the agent context, a skill can instead be a textual description of how a task should be performed. These descriptions can encode domain knowledge, workflow logic, which tools to use at each phase, and how to check that a workflow has been completed correctly. In that sense, agent skills become reusable procedural knowledge.

He then walked through three demos.

The first demo showed a basic agent workflow. The demo app contained only a few tools:

- An IFC tool to open and inspect IFC files and extract data
- A PDF reader for reading documents
- A database tool to store extracted data for future use

He used publicly available IFC files and documents from the internet so the example was not overly optimized. The engineer could send an IFC model to the agent, which knew how to parse the IFC file and inspect elements and their properties. In the demo, the use case focused on selecting ducts, so the agent first extracted the first five IFC duct elements and their properties.

For product selection, the goal was not only cost but also LCA. To calculate LCA, the agent needed EPD information, so Geron provided an EPD document for one supplier’s ducts as a plain PDF. The agent used the PDF reader to extract the text content from the EPD. Because EPDs have some structure, even if not everything is perfectly standardized, the language model could read the content and use its understanding of LCA calculation to produce a reasonable summary.

In this way, the agent performed a basic LCA calculation for the ducts based on the EPD document.

In this way, the agent performed a basic LCA calculation for the ducts based on the EPD document.

The next step was cost calculation. Geron provided a publicly available price list for the ducts in PDF format. The system again used the PDF reader to parse the content and extract prices. He cautioned that language models should not be relied on for calculations in production, and that this simplified setup was only for demonstration purposes.

With both LCA and cost information available, the agent could support product selection. Geron then added a price list from a different supplier for comparable ducts. The agent read that second price list, compared options, and produced a summary of which supplier’s products should be selected for the project.

At that point, the workflow had identified the cheapest product and carried out a corresponding LCA calculation.

Geron then showed how to avoid repeating this extraction work in the future. Since the EPD and price list content had already been parsed, the relevant prices and LCA data could be stored in a database via the MCP/database connection. That way, on the next run, the system would not need to reread and reanalyze the same documents.

He also demonstrated generating an output file for downstream use. In the example, once the products had been selected and calculations completed, the agent created a CSV report containing quantities, LCA information, and prices. This could then be sent to a construction site or supplier to support ordering.

The second demo focused on creating a reusable skill from the workflow. Geron said that once he had a product-selection flow that worked, he wanted to save it so that it could be reused. He asked the agent to create a workflow skill based on what had just been done.

Because the system already had a “skill for creating new skills,” the agent could summarize the full prior conversation and the steps taken, then write them out as a new skill. Geron described this as the agent capturing all the phases and actions in a textual form that could be used later. Since skills are text documents, they can be shared and reused repeatedly. The agent then stored the newly created skill in the file system, where it could be reviewed and adjusted.

The third demo showed reusing that saved skill. Geron loaded the saved “duct flow” skill into the agent. Because the workflow was already described in the skill file, he no longer needed to explain every step again. He could simply provide the IFC model and start the process.

The agent then knew how to parse the IFC file, retrieve relevant data from the database, carry out the LCA calculation, look up pricing, and continue through the product selection process without needing the same instructions again. Geron noted that the language model he used was not the most advanced one, so it still needed some extra prompting in places, but the key idea was that the process could now run based on the stored skill and previously saved data.

In the final step, the system produced an order file in CSV format containing the calculated quantities and other necessary information, ready to be sent to the construction site.

Because of time, the session moved on without audience questions. The presenter and moderator noted that the demo highlighted how important it is to get the data right at the beginning, especially in environments where many legacy systems and disconnected data sources make integration difficult.

Richard Parker of AKT in London, part of the Tyréns Group, presented ADOT Engineer, an AI-native, agentic platform intended to help engineers automate calculation tasks and reporting workflows.

He described ADOT Engineer as a platform that assists engineers in building tools and workflows for automating daily engineering calculations. He first showed a promotional demo video rather than a live product walkthrough.

According to the video and Richard’s explanation, the workflow begins with a simple prompt. The user describes a problem or calculation need, and ADOT Engineer creates a plan for how to build the necessary calculation tools. Once the user confirms the plan, the tool is constructed automatically.

The platform can also generate tools from existing materials such as:

- Excel tools
- Example calculation outputs
- Reference project data

Users can test the tools with real-world input values to build confidence in them. Richard emphasized that ADOT Engineer exposes both the visual user interface and the code behind each tool, so the process does not remain an opaque AI black box.

Once a user is satisfied with the tool quality through testing, they can verify it and publish it into a private calculation toolkit. From then on, ADOT Engineer can reuse that verified, high-quality tool in later workflows.

The video then shifted to reporting. Once the user has what Richard called “an AI companion who can calculate and who you can trust,” the next step is to generate reports. The user describes the problem or report they need to deliver, and ADOT Engineer constructs an agentic workflow that combines:

- Project data from legacy systems
- The verified calculation tools created earlier
- A set of ready-made AI agents

Using those components, the platform can automatically write a report in a matter of minutes. Richard said the reports can include:

- Text summaries
- Tables
- Drawings
- Other visualizations expected in professional engineering reports

He stressed that, throughout this process, the system aims to provide maximum transparency about its actions and a fully auditable workflow. Once the user is satisfied, they can verify the report, download it as a PDF, and send it to the client or add it to project documentation.

He stressed that, throughout this process, the system aims to provide maximum transparency about its actions and a fully auditable workflow. Once the user is satisfied, they can verify the report, download it as a PDF, and send it to the client or add it to project documentation.

After the video, Richard discussed the philosophy behind the platform. He compared the intended role of ADOT Engineer to developments in the legal industry. In law, clients place high levels of responsibility and risk on professionals, and legal AI systems are being designed to support those professionals while preserving structure, accountability, and review. Richard argued that engineering needs a similar layer between raw AI models and professional output.

Where lawyers work primarily in the medium of language and text, engineers work in calculations, tabular information, and deterministic functions. ADOT Engineer is intended to connect engineering experts with AI capabilities while preserving the rigor, structure, and verification standards the profession requires.

A major design principle is verification and auditability. Richard said engineers need to be able to inspect not only the outputs and inputs of a tool, but also the code being executed underneath. There must be a credible peer-review process and an auditable trail of what happened under the hood, because engineering outputs carry safety-critical accountability.

He also described the scope of the challenge as much larger than saving a few minutes. The goal is to tackle meaningful chunks of engineering work—days rather than minutes. The platform does this by treating each engineering tool as the smallest reusable module of work. If small verified tools can be reused across many projects and workflows, then organizations get much higher return on investment from creating them.

Richard then described the user interaction model. The main interface is a dialogue between the engineer and the agent. The user describes the task or problem they want to solve. The agent then:

- Looks through the user’s portfolio of previously created or verified tools
- Looks at tools that have been shared with the user
- Looks at organization-wide verified tools that may already exist
- Suggests reusing appropriate tools rather than recreating them
- Connects to data from analysis systems through integrations

He noted that engineers rely on trusted analysis software for core calculations, and much of the value lies in taking results from those systems and extending them into final deliverables. For that reason, direct integrations with existing engineering systems are important.

He emphasized the need for strong human-in-the-loop behavior. The system must ask clarifying questions when the user is vague, because engineers often naturally write very short prompts while the scope of the problem may be large. The software is therefore designed to guide the engineer through the problem and its assumptions rather than simply returning a one-shot answer.

Richard also discussed where he sees the platform being most useful. He positioned engineering work along two dimensions. On one end are lower-level, repetitive, data-heavy, and laborious tasks: summarizing large numbers of elements, producing charts, automating calculations, and generating routine outputs. On the other end are higher-order design tasks involving creativity, coordination, geometry, and optimization.

He argued that AI should be used first to remove the drudgery from lower-level work so engineers can focus more on the higher-value aspects of design. In his view, the ultimate aim is to move engineers higher into the design space of creativity and optimization.

He also referenced his own background as a structural engineer with more than 12 years of project experience, and said he sees applications not only in structural engineering but across the Tyréns Group, including hydraulic engineering, environmental engineering, rock engineering, bridge engineering, and other disciplines. Feedback from within the group suggests strong applicability across those areas.

On expected impact, Richard said it is difficult to assign exact numbers, but he believes the platform can remove approximately 5 to 15 hours of work per engineer by automating mundane calculation and reporting tasks. He emphasized that once an engineer has created a useful calculation tool, that tool should be elevated to the organizational level and shared, rather than recreated in every project. He sees the current project-by-project recreation of such tools as a waste of talent.

He returned to the risk of using raw LLMs directly. In his view, direct access to foundational models is too risky in engineering. Instead, there needs to be a layered system:

He returned to the risk of using raw LLMs directly. In his view, direct access to foundational models is too risky in engineering. Instead, there needs to be a layered system:

- Foundational AI models at the bottom
- Direct connection to engineering data systems
- Agents to handle repetitive, laborious actions such as automation and chart generation
- A deterministic layer to provide reliability, accountability, and verification

This deterministic and verified layer is central to scalability. Once a tool has been peer reviewed and verified multiple times, that verification should not need to be repeated from scratch every time. That is what turns organizational investment in tool-building into scalable value.

He also highlighted the importance of output quality. The end product should not be limited to a web interface; the platform should generate high-quality scientific and engineering reports that can be read, inspected, shared, and submitted externally.

Richard closed by noting that the platform is publicly available, with a free sign-up option as well as professional and enterprise plans for heavier use and collaboration features such as sharing tools and workflows across organizations.

In Q&A, one attendee asked about inputting geometry from third-party software and how reliably the platform can read it. Richard said they already have a working integration with ETABS as one of the first systems they are connecting to. The system can create built-in tools that request information from such software, but the quality of extraction depends on how specifically the user identifies groups or element sets. If users are too vague, the system needs to ask more questions before extracting data, especially because pulling large amounts of model information can be time-consuming. He said the human-in-the-loop clarification process is therefore important for reliable geometry and data extraction.

Petri Casare: Hello, my name is Petri Casare, and I work as a BIM software developer at Sweco Finland. I’m here to present how we built our multi-agent and LLM-based platform that exposes our BIM and design data through a chat interface integrated into Microsoft and Autodesk tools.

I’ll focus on the architecture, how the agents work, and what we learned from the pilots.

In typical projects, design documentation, BMS data, and project information are scattered across Revit, AutoCAD, SharePoint, emails, and other legacy systems. Finding anything requires a lot of manual searching and often asking senior colleagues, “Have you done this before?” This is especially true in exceptional cases where our default templates do not help.

That leads to slower workflows, inconsistent solutions, and high cognitive load for designers.

To address this, we defined four key requirements. First, we needed a unified access layer across Revit and AutoCAD models, BMS and device data, documents, and other internal sources, so users do not have to hunt across systems.

Second, tasks had to be natural-language based, meaning designers can ask questions without deep knowledge of the underlying data models or where the information is stored.

Third, it had to be enterprise-grade on Azure, with good security and governance, and integrated into the Microsoft ecosystem.

Fourth, it had to be extensible so that we can easily add new agents, tools, skills, and data sources over time as requirements evolve.

The overall architecture goes from user to data. Users interact through our Blazor-made web app hosted on Azure App Service, and every request flows into the Azure Functions layer, where Semantic Kernel and the handoff model run.

There, the orchestrator coordinates three specialized agents: the project agent, document agent, and instruction agent, which talk to our Azure databases and storage. GPT-4.1 provides reasoning strictly through these controlled tools. I know GPT-4.1 is already outdated, but this proof of concept used that model.

To have viable data in our databases, we use a custom data pipeline from the AutoCAD and Revit databases into our own Azure databases via a sync uploader.

Semantic Kernel lets us define the tools and skills our agents can call, such as querying databases and generating documents. It also manages routing through the handoff model, which performs intent detection and task decomposition and routes parts to the right agents.

Semantic Kernel lets us define the tools and skills our agents can call, such as querying databases and generating documents. It also manages routing through the handoff model, which performs intent detection and task decomposition and routes parts to the right agents.

Compared with a single-agent approach, this gives us a much more modular platform. It is easier to extend, and each agent has clear responsibilities.

We run a small set of specialized agents with clear roles. For example, the project agent queries structured project datasets, metadata, and associated documents. The document agent retrieves device specs, instructions, and I/O connections from Azure storage and indexes them down to individual clauses.

The instruction agent is mainly for producing operating-description drafts by combining document outputs. That ensures that the text stays grounded to clause-level data.

Each agent exposes scoped tools to GPT-4.1, and the Semantic Kernel orchestrator picks the right system based on user intent.

Our data pipeline is built around two goals: capture design intent in a structured way, and do it without slowing down designers.

The Revit and AutoCAD add-ins extract the key content we need for automated design—device types, parameters, instruction texts, and so on—and package it into a consistent hierarchy.

To keep CAD tools responsive, the add-ins do not upload data directly from the user session. Instead, they hand the payload to a background process, so the designer does not feel any lag and performance is much more predictable.

That uploader then pushes the data to Azure through our secure APIs.

The result is no manual data transfers, no performance hit on Revit or AutoCAD, and a consistent, clause-addressable dataset that our agents can query reliably.

From the user’s point of view, everything happens in one chat window. A designer can type a query like: “List projects where the points of ventilation and heat recovery control and regulation has been done.” Then Semantic Kernel interprets the intent and decides which agents to involve.

Typically that means the project agent to scan structured project data, maybe the document agent for detailed instruction clauses, and the instruction agent if we need formatted output.

Those agents query the databases and indexes and pull back the relevant projects plus their operating descriptions, and GPT-4.1 stitches those into a structured response.

So instead of hopping through folders or asking colleagues, the user gets the answer in seconds through chat.

Those agents query the databases and indexes and pull back the relevant projects plus their operating descriptions, and GPT-4.1 stitches those into a structured response.

So instead of hopping through folders or asking colleagues, the user gets the answer in seconds through chat.

We designed this solution to live inside the Microsoft and Autodesk ecosystems our users already work in. On the Microsoft side, the chat front end is built on Blazor and hosted on Azure App Service. Authentication and authorization are handled through Azure AD, so users inherit their existing permissions.

Requests go to the Azure Functions layer, where Semantic Kernel with the handoff model runs. The project content is stored in Azure tables and storage.

For operations, Application Insights provides service telemetry, and SecOps gets detailed agent logs so we can trace requests and debug errors more quickly.

On the Autodesk side, we use our custom-built AutoCAD and Revit add-ins built with .NET and the Autodesk APIs. These are used in startup pipelines to produce our AI-ready data.

The local add-ins wrap up the document data, and an asynchronous background process then handles pushes to the databases.

This queue-based approach gives us predictable performance, consistent snapshots for querying, and resilient uploads even if the CAD session ends.

Security and governance were built in from the start. All project data sits inside tenant-controlled Azure tables and blob storage, and Azure AD role assignments follow the user all the way through the agent layer. No agent can retrieve more than the caller is entitled to.

On the observability side, we log every agent handoff, the tools they invoke, plus the prompts and responses. Those logs flow into the SecOps environment, which lets us trace end-to-end conversations, detect errors quickly, and debug with full context.

Operationally, we version each skill and agent and roll out prompt or tool changes in stages so we can monitor impact before broadening deployment.

We piloted this as a real working prototype, not as a mockup, in live projects, but with a deliberately constrained scope. The goal was to validate the architecture, the agent handoff model, and the data capture approach—not to optimize performance metrics yet.

What we observed early was mainly qualitative. Users could ask questions in natural language instead of navigating folders or remembering how things were done previously.

What we observed early was mainly qualitative. Users could ask questions in natural language instead of navigating folders or remembering how things were done previously.

From the technical standpoint, the most important insight was that the value did not come from any single agent, but from the orchestration itself—how agents, data capture, and design-tool integration work together.

That gave us confidence that the architecture scales even before we scale the pilots themselves.

There are still challenges to solve. First, data quality and coverage vary widely. Legacy projects and inconsistent naming directly affect what we can retrieve, so normalization and data discipline matter.

Second, there is the latency tradeoff. The more agents and tools we call, the richer the answer, but the harder it is to keep response time predictable.

Third, LLMs can hallucinate, so we have to keep the model grounded by constraining it to tool-based retrieval and clause-level references.

Finally, there is the meta-challenge that the AI field evolves extremely fast. Parts of our stack, like the Semantic Kernel orchestration pattern we used here, can become outdated quickly as newer agent frameworks emerge and simplify orchestration and reduce boilerplate.

So we have to design for adaptability, not for a frozen architecture.

Our roadmap focuses on three things: richer content, smarter retrieval, and a stronger platform.

First, we are adding manufacturer data adapters so the agents can reason over vendor catalogs, device specs, and not just our internal documents.

Second, we are implementing full vector and RAG pipelines with clause-level embeddings and explicit grounding hooks inside the instruction agent, so answers and generated text stay tightly connected to source clauses.

Third, we are raising quality with prompt evaluation, regression tests, and staged rollouts so improvements do not introduce regressions.

On the platform side, the next development step is the MCP server, which becomes a clean surface to Copilot and Teams so they can call the same orchestrated tools once that integration is ready.

Finally, because the AI ecosystem evolves so quickly, we expect to move from today’s Semantic Kernel orchestration patterns toward newer agent frameworks that reduce boilerplate and simplify handoff and tool wiring.

Finally, because the AI ecosystem evolves so quickly, we expect to move from today’s Semantic Kernel orchestration patterns toward newer agent frameworks that reduce boilerplate and simplify handoff and tool wiring.

To close, our three takeaways are these: we orchestrate specialized agents on Azure Functions using Semantic Kernel and GPT-4.1; we keep a reliable clause-level dataset flowing from Revit and AutoCAD to our own Azure databases; and we expose it through a Blazor UI inside the tools our designers are already using.

Thank you.

Q&A

Audience: I have a question regarding data security. You are giving your data to ChatGPT or your LLM. How do you manage that?

Petri Casare: We basically use Microsoft Azure Foundry, and we use models that assure us they do not use the data we provide in any third-party way.

Moderator: You mentioned evaluations as a future thing and prompt-related work. Are you considering evaluating the outputs as well with AI?

Petri Casare: There is a very large list of improvements we are planning to implement in this proof-of-concept tool.

Audience: Do you have any specific use case where you use AutoCAD data or Revit data for the user? For example, prompting specific questions and getting answers back—what kinds of use cases are there?

Petri Casare: In this phase, we built this for the task of helping users find relevant older projects where they had already designed something very specific. I remember when I was a BMS designer, I had difficulties when I needed something more specific, and almost every time I had to ask colleagues, “Have you designed this already?” This makes that search space smaller. You can ask in the chat interface, find similar projects, and use those as templates.

Audience: Regarding the roadmap, can you give an indication of timeline, and how much impact would a shorter roadmap have for the company?

Petri Casare: This roadmap was mainly for the bigger picture and does not really have a concrete timeline at the moment. The platform is now moving to the next development phase, and the main parts we are going to implement are still under development.

Jani Nevalainen: What if I told you that you could have a team of specialists working for you 24/7 on your project, running calculations and validations even without coffee breaks, while you focus on your design decisions?

That sounds almost too good to be true—maybe even like science fiction.

My name is Jani Nevalainen. I work as a software developer at A-Insinöörit Group, and today I will tell you how multi-agent systems for automated engineering can take you there.

Let’s start with the reality of our current workflow. Right now, structural engineering exists in silos. On one side, we have BIM models. In the middle, we have the physics and calculation engines. On the other side, we have project data.

The problem is that these environments are disconnected.

To bridge them, we act as human routers. We manually export a PDF or an Excel sheet from one tool and retype those values into another. This static file exchange is the bottleneck of our industry.

Every time we reenter a parameter, we are not just losing time. We are creating an opportunity for human error and ensuring that our data becomes stale the moment the architectural model changes.

We have all been there. You build the perfect Grasshopper script or Python tool, and it works beautifully until the software updates or the project requirements shift by 5%.

This is the rigidity problem. Because these connections are hardcoded, they act as brittle bridges. They do not bend; they break.

We find ourselves in the automation trap, where engineers spend more time debugging legacy scripts than actually performing engineering design.

We accidentally trade engineering hours for maintenance.

Even when our scripts do work, they often hit the scalability wall. Most automation today relies on static logic—fixed “if x, then y”—built for one specific scenario.

The moment you need to swap a calculation engine or incorporate a new data source, you are back to square one rewriting code.

This creates project silos because these integrations are not aware of each other. The automation we build for Project A is rarely usable for Project B. We are not building a knowledge base. We are building one-off tools that die when the project ends.

This creates project silos because these integrations are not aware of each other. The automation we build for Project A is rarely usable for Project B. We are not building a knowledge base. We are building one-off tools that die when the project ends.

Finally, we have the data bottleneck. We are still stuck in a relay race of heavy static files passing back and forth. Every time we export a file, we lose granularity, lose version control, and definitely lose the ability for real-time collaboration.

This file burden is what prevents us from scaling.

To fix this, we need to move away from brittle, tool-dependent links toward a software-neutral core where the data, not the file, is the source of truth.

In the old world, automation was a brittle script. If the tool updated, the script broke.

In the new world, we use orchestration. We have a coordinator, a digital project manager, that looks at an agent registry of specialists.

Instead of writing a fixed path, the coordinator identifies the expertise needed for a specific intent and hires those agents on the fly.

This makes the system resilient. You can swap out a calculation engine in the registry without rewriting a single line of your workflow. It also makes dynamic workflows possible.

As I said earlier, the traditional AEC industry relies on a relay race: manually passing heavy IFCs, Excels, and PDFs. That is no longer a good option if we want to create dynamic workflows using agents.

We are replacing that fragmented workflow with the BCMS, which stands for the Building Content Management System. Think of it as a shared context. Every agent sits around this round table.

Unlike a static IFC file, the BCMS is granular. It tracks individual parts and versions of the BIM model in a database.

To interact with it, we use MCP, a universal handshake that allows an AI to browse the database dynamically. It fetches only what is needed for the task at hand—no more, no less.

Finally, the LLM acts as the bridge. It takes the human command, no matter how it is delivered, and translates it into the precise data queries needed to fuel the machine. It maps the data into our engineering tools, and most importantly, it closes the loop. It takes the raw output and pushes it back into the BCMS.

This is the point where the engineer stops being a data-entry clerk and starts being the design pilot supervising a verified intelligence system.

Here we can see the brain of the operation: the coordinator agent.

When a command is given, the coordinator performs a triage. It does not just guess. It queries our agent registry, like a yellow pages of expertise. It identifies which specialized agents are available, checks their capabilities and tools, and performs a dynamic handoff.

It sends a structured work order to the experts, waits for the results, and then decides whether the user’s intent is fully satisfied or whether another handoff is required to finish the loop.

How do agents interact with the data? We use a dedicated BCMS agent that acts as a gatekeeper to our database. By using MCP, this agent can see into the project data dynamically.

Instead of downloading a massive 3D model, it performs part-level updates. It can fetch one beam, update its reinforcement, and track its status from draft to verified.

This creates a surgical, version-controlled environment where every change is tracked back to a specific agent action.

The technical backbone of the solution is a decoupled architecture. Traditionally, you needed the local BIM application to be open to run a script.

Here, the work happens in the cloud.

The BCMS acts as an asynchronous hub, allowing agents to work in the background.

This moves us from black-box automation, where you hope the script works, to a glass-box system where every calculation and data fetch is logged, time-stamped, and fully auditable.

One of the biggest hurdles in AI adoption is trust. To solve this black-box problem further, we separate orchestration from engineering.

We do not ask an LLM to guess rebar areas. We use it to prepare the data for a calculation agent that runs strictly deterministic formulas.

To go even further, designs can go through a validation agent—a digital peer reviewer—which uses a hybrid model.

First, it runs deterministic math to ensure the results do not violate physics or Eurocodes, for instance. Second, it uses an LLM to perform a contextual check against project standards.

This creates a safety lock. The final 3D model will not even see the data until this digital auditor gives the green light.

We are not just automating the work; we are automating quality assurance.

This creates a safety lock. The final 3D model will not even see the data until this digital auditor gives the green light.

We are not just automating the work; we are automating quality assurance.

Before the demonstration, here is the bird’s-eye view: we move from intent to a verified 3D model through a closed loop of agent-to-agent communication. The engineer provides the intent, and the system provides the proof.

Here is the 3D view of our BIM model stored in the BCMS, and on the right we have our BCM assistant.

In this example, we communicate with agents by typing into a chat, but of course we could have other interfaces as well.

With the BCMS we can fetch data at the object level and easily see the current, up-to-date information.

Let’s go further. First, we check the status of design for floors 1, 2, and 4. Our agents filter the request and return the status information back to the user. The data is visualized using the 3D model and different graphs.

That is one example, but we can go further and combine tools. For instance, we can compare planned schedule to actual status. In this way, we can create dynamic workflows on the fly by combining tools and agents.

Here we can see which elements are delayed, and we can see the conflicts between actual status and planned status, and then take action.

Finally, one interesting tool is environmental impact evaluation. In this example, we calculate the CO2 emissions for floors 1 to 4, and our agent gives us the total carbon footprint, but also the individual components on those floors and how they contribute to it.

So you can see both the big picture and the detailed breakdown quite easily.

This was just a small demonstration. There are, of course, many other tools we can integrate here, and in that demo we did not save any data, but the CO2 calculation itself was done on the fly.

Now let’s talk about the magic of the system: headless scalability.

Traditionally, your speed is limited by your hardware and your licenses. How many instances of your modeling software or FEM tool can you run at once?

This is where the infrastructure magic happens. Once we decouple the math from the desktop, your engineering tools can live as headless services in the cloud.

This is where the infrastructure magic happens. Once we decouple the math from the desktop, your engineering tools can live as headless services in the cloud.

While you work, hundreds of agents can be designing and verifying components in the background. Because the BCMS acts as the broker, you do not need a heavy BIM model to be open to run calculations.

The loop closes only when the modeling software detects a verified flag, and then it brings the final geometry into the model.

So we have traded idle loading bars for 24/7 asynchronous productivity.

To wrap up, this is not just about faster scripts. It is about a fundamental shift. We are moving from being human routers, manually moving data between software, to becoming design-data pilots.

In this new paradigm, the AI manages the data mess and the mapping, while the human remains the final professional validator.

Our next steps involve combining carbon and cost agents to make sustainability a real-time design metric, and of course expanding to other design domains.

We are finally building a design process that is as intelligent as the structures we create. Thank you.
