Mona Mili: Hello, and thank you for the introduction. My name is Mona Mili. I’m an architect and PhD student working in the field of building-integrated photovoltaics at the Fraunhofer Institute for Solar Energy Systems in Freiburg, Germany.

Today I would like to introduce my current research on multi-agent systems for design and construction optimization.

This research is done in the context of a bigger project where, together with six other Fraunhofer institutes, we investigate the automation of the whole renovation process. For some process steps there are already highly automated solutions, as we have also seen today, and for others we are developing solutions.

What we are showing in this process is an outline of an optimal process: a continuous workflow of work steps where everything is clearly defined, dependencies are clear, interfaces work perfectly, and interdisciplinary collaboration works perfectly.

But if we look into reality, this is often not the case. Processes often take much longer, dependencies are complex, actor interaction is problematic, and topic intersections are not very clearly defined. This ends up in delays, exploding costs, and so on. That is something we want to work on.

Our goal is to examine where we can optimize those processes. Our method is to first define the processes, formalize them, and analyze them. For that we use knowledge graphs, a method where, with edges and nodes, tasks and relationships are very clearly defined in a comprehensible way for a machine, not only for a human.

If we talk about processes, there are many sources that store information about them. Of course there is literature and know-how. There are also laws and standards that contain a lot of information. But there is also the experience of people who have built something like this before, and we think that is very valuable information.

So we looked into how processes are documented. There are different ways, but one common form is meetings and meeting minutes, where it is documented what was discussed and what the next steps are. So we first decided to look into those documentations.

Our proposal for approaching this big goal of process optimization is to automatically analyze design and construction processes first for their optimization potential.

What we think is our new contribution to process optimization is that with automation and automated analysis, we can be very fast and influence the process while it is running.

What we think is our new contribution to process optimization is that with automation and automated analysis, we can be very fast and influence the process while it is running.

On top of that, we can be very individual. This is not just research about generally optimizing some kind of process. During a process as it is running, our goal would be to create an agent that analyzes it and constantly gives suggestions on how to optimize it.

Before I go into more detail about what we actually did, I would like to give some context. There are of course many ways to optimize processes. For example, we are working with modular facade systems, so you can optimize a process through prefabrication.

You can also formalize processes. BIM is one concept for defining how data is transferred, and there is also the standard of Business Process Model and Notation. You see a diagram of that on the slide. It is a standard where processes are defined and formalized.

That is a digital way, but it is still made for humans—for us to understand, read, and analyze. Our approach introduces a graph-structured knowledge base, which is rather made for machines to understand. That is one of the main differences.

Another difference is that the BPMN diagram is static, while we have a flexible network where we can retrieve information anytime depending on what we need at the moment.

Knowledge graphs are also increasingly used as a base for AI analysis, and that is another reason we chose them.

I said that we want to look into process documentation and transfer it into our knowledge graph. How do we do that?

We built a multi-agent pipeline. In our case, that means the agents are working with each other and are not connected to an Autodesk program or something similar.

This pipeline has three steps. First, pseudonymization, because data security is very important to us. We want to reach out to architectural offices, and maybe that is a very German thing, but they are very concerned about security and privacy. For us as a research institute, that is also an important topic.

Second, we have an agent that structures the information.

Third, we have an ontology-mapping agent that gives our knowledge graph a structure in terms of an ontology.

Second, we have an agent that structures the information.

Third, we have an ontology-mapping agent that gives our knowledge graph a structure in terms of an ontology.

So our workflow looks like this: first the pseudonymizer, which takes the meeting protocols. It returns pseudonymized documents together with mappings, and we have a human in the loop here to be very sure that all sensitive data is removed.

Then we have the structured table generator, and finally the ontology mapper.

The pseudonymization agent is responsible for detecting all the sensitive entities in the meeting data. We convert each document into a pseudonymized representation.

The information gets passed from the PDF through a PDF reader in chunks, and those chunks are then passed through the workflow.

We developed two methodologies to find the entities we want to replace. In the first one, we use an LLM that was pretrained especially for this task.

Even though it was for German and trained for the task, we wanted two different methods to compare them, so we also use a general LLM.

We run both locally because we want to know what is going on with our data. We want to be in charge and ensure that no data goes anywhere.

So we do all of this first step locally, and only when the data is pseudonymized do we use bigger LLMs and agents.

In the second step, we take those protocols—those PDF documents—and process them in our system. There we use GPT-4 and GPT-4.5, which was the current state when we developed the tool.

Here we give a system prompt asking to extract the information in a structured way. We ask for tasks: what has been discussed, which tasks exist, who is responsible for each task, when the task was scheduled, when it was actually executed, and those kinds of things.

That is what we retrieve.

I have not yet mentioned PydanticAI, which is the agent that controls whether the response we get from the AI is validated against our schema, so that we have a clean data structure.

Here we want to have structured JSON tables, and they need to be in that format.

In the last step, we also developed two methodologies. We already have structured information, so the main part is done, but for a knowledge graph we need a clear structure, and for that we use an ontology.

In the last step, we also developed two methodologies. We already have structured information, so the main part is done, but for a knowledge graph we need a clear structure, and for that we use an ontology.

An ontology is like a dictionary in which everything is defined. Each word we use to describe something is defined.

We use an ontology called the Internet of Construction Processes Ontology, which was developed by RWTH Aachen University. The keynote speaker at the end of this conference is connected to that work.

This ontology functions as the structure for our knowledge graph and all the data.

If that was very technical, I want to give a simple example. On the left, you have a PDF. It is in German, but it does not matter what it says. In orange you see the task, and the name is blacked out.

Below that, you see the pseudonymized text, where the actors were replaced, so it now says “Actor 1,” and the task as well.

Then you see how the agent structures the information. On the left there are labels like task and actor, and then the corresponding information.

At that point, we already have the structure we want.

On the right side you see how the knowledge graph looks. In blue you always have the name of the entity coming from the ontology, and then the information attached to it.

Now we have well-structured data. What do we do with it?

There are a lot of options. For now, we focus on diagrams, time analysis, and conflict detection. We are in the first steps of using the knowledge graph.

We decided to analyze and represent four parameters: duration, dependencies, number of actors, and conflicts.

What you see is still a sketch—one first result. We have of course tested many protocols, but this is an early visualization of the analysis we are currently doing.

At the moment, we are also transferring this into a web app that we developed. In the end, you will be able to upload your PDF and with one click get your diagram.

Because we want to use this for many kinds of things, we also want to support different focuses.

For example, you can focus on the process itself. Then you see the processes with their names and how they are connected.

Because we want to use this for many kinds of things, we also want to support different focuses.

For example, you can focus on the process itself. Then you see the processes with their names and how they are connected.

But you could also focus on the actor. If I am responsible for a task, what else do I have to do? One actor can ask: what are my tasks?

You can also focus on information, meaning how information is transferred from one task to another, which is very important where information is needed.

These are examples of what we are currently developing. It is still work in progress, but all of these things can be done with our knowledge graph.

Coming back to our vision: right now we are looking into these protocols because we think they are valuable and because they are a way to unlock data that people already have. Architectural offices already have these PDFs without needing to transform anything. They do not need to do much—just hand us the PDFs—and we can do something with them.

In the future, of course, we want to look into more different information sources to build a more profound knowledge base and knowledge graph, so that we can use it for an AI that analyzes the process while it is running and gives optimization suggestions.

Thank you very much.

Moderator: As a research institute, is this project open source, or is it intended to be commercialized? What is the concrete output going forward?

Mona Mili: At the moment, we still need to develop a strategy for that. The idea is that most of our research needs to be public in the end. But if this proves to be a very promising idea, then maybe we build a startup that brings it to market, or we collaborate with tech companies and developers. Audience: If your goal is just to extract the knowledge graph, why not just use an LLM directly to do that? What is the benefit or tradeoff here in terms of reliability or cost?

Audience: If your goal is just to extract the knowledge graph, why not just use an LLM directly to do that? What is the benefit or tradeoff here in terms of reliability or cost?

Mona Mili: We use the knowledge graph and all of these intermediate steps also to be transparent and know what each step is doing. We want the knowledge graph in the end as a network where we store data. It is a way where the data is clearly defined, and the connections between information are defined in a way that an LLM, for example, can easily extract information from it. That is what we are using it for. It is more stable this way.

Also, you do not need to run the whole process all the time. You can store information in the knowledge graph, extend it, and in the end have a lot of processes and information in one knowledge graph. You do not want to see all of them all the time, but you may want to ask a specific question or analyze a specific aspect, and then you can refer to the knowledge graph as the base.

Audience: Could you summarize the key business benefits this kind of system could have?

Mona Mili: We are in close contact with architectural firms. We do interviews, round tables, and so on to evaluate our work and see what firms are actually asking for. On one hand, we see many of the questions discussed in the last two talks—that people are looking for files and want to know something about formal processes. We approach this from a different angle because we also see that there is so much information stored there, not only in separate files but in continuous workflows. We can learn a lot from experienced architects. Of course we can talk to all of them, but we can also use data analysis to try to extract this knowledge from the stored data that is lying around. That is where we hope to create benefits for future projects.

Henry Mulder: We start with content filtering and summarization.

Henry Mulder: We start with content filtering and summarization.

Another key role is as a force multiplier, which we focus on a bit more in this presentation. Agents can automate repetitive tasks and provide expert assistance, enabling teams to achieve more.

That also helps address labor scarcity.

Then there is autonomous data exchange and interoperability. In our field there are many different formats and software tools, so multi-agent systems can handle data exchange between different tools with minimal interaction and help break down software silos.

Before we jump into cases, here is a technical overview.

Most of our agentic applications follow a pattern with an agent orchestration layer. Within that orchestration, we have many different kinds of agents—for modeling, drawing, developer help, and so on.

The request goes through orchestration, which selects what we need. We have context search and retrieval-augmented generation. Within that there is model context, knowledge-based information, and state updates referring to the actual tooling and model.

We look at the state—what is inside it—and use that to our advantage.

We also have chained task solving. Instead of doing one thing, based on the prompt we can do 10 or 20 different tasks within one chain, creating an initial workflow.

Then we have tool calling and execution. Many people talk about MCP, and that is very useful for us as well, since it lets us interact with available APIs. It is a protocol, though there are others too.

Lastly, we have an observation loop. After each step, we might go back and alter the whole step process. We may update the state, look at what is happening in a 3D model, see that something is not going according to plan, make adjustments, and try to finalize the job.

It is not only about the workflow; it is also about the data. Not just the prompt, not just the architecture.

It is not only about the workflow; it is also about the data. Not just the prompt, not just the architecture.

In the best case, we are working with multimodal information. Contexts can include BIM information, model geometry, lighting conditions, scans, information from the job site, materials, spaces, rules, and even user skills employed through agentic applications.

We now have code generation based on APIs. One important issue here is safety: we do not execute any code unless the user looks through it and then executes it, unless it is in a very sandboxed environment.

In this case, we let the user check it and then perform the action.

These capabilities sit on top of some of our modeling products, so you can essentially generate code using our own API offering, execute it in real time or as scripts, see how it behaves, and then take it into your own projects.

Right now we are focusing on example cases where we can understand what the user wants and whether the output is correct after the user has checked it. We are at the stage where the agents can provide good outputs, but not yet at the stage of embedded structural validation.

Audience: I’m curious about your statement about the new interface—from highly technical interaction to natural language. From an end-user perspective, what do they need to learn? Do they need to be able to express their intention eloquently to the agent? Is this just shifting the expertise?

Kim Nyberg: I think you still need to be a domain expert. You need to know the terminology and what you want to do. It is not as if you can just pick anyone off the street—or me—and make them a detailer.

Kim Nyberg: I think you still need to be a domain expert. You need to know the terminology and what you want to do. It is not as if you can just pick anyone off the street—or me—and make them a detailer.

There is still a lot of value in knowing the trade. Just like with developers: sure, everybody can write code now, but should I use AI-written code directly in production with sensitive data? Maybe not.

It is the same here. You still need domain expertise to get good results. We can do cool demos—create Christmas trees and all kinds of things with structural steel—but when you actually want to do something constructible, you still need to be able to express yourself.

What changes is that you do not have to go through menus and hunt around in the UI. You can create filters or actions through the APIs that do exactly what you want.

So this is a new way for expert users to get even more out of our systems, and also a way to onboard new users into fairly complex software.

Henry Mulder: Over time, what I would hope is that if you are a domain expert, you can spend more of your time thriving in that expertise instead of spending years trying to grasp some specific software.

Tommy Merolastero: Have a nice afternoon. We are very happy to introduce some of our ideas on how knowledge graphs allow AEC product data to “speak AI.” My name is Tommy Merolastero.

Teo Arvian: And my name is Teo Arvian.

Tommy Merolastero: Unfortunately, Otto could not be here, so I will take charge of this first part. Last year, we emphasized what kind of AI agent system is under development. This time we are digging a bit more into the details of how product data can be attached within BIM models using knowledge graphs and related methods.

First, we need to discuss how BIM can be joined into a bill of materials. That is one crucial part where we need to focus in order to make this product data flow happen.

In Finland, for air ducts and mechanical design, we have developed a national Finnish standardization for IFC-based mechanical design. The nomenclature part means that we can identify, on a general level, each individual object in the model.

That means that, by using this information attached to product catalogs, we can enrich the data with GT numbers and specify both the data and the object itself more precisely. We want to move from an engineering bill of materials, when we design something, to a bill of materials for manufacturing. That helps us make product-level information flow through the whole construction value chain.

This can be done automatically. We discussed that last year. Now we are stepping into the system level. What we are focusing on is merging the design phase into the procurement phase. When we have the nomenclature in place and define the general product codings for models, we need to join that to structured BIM models. Without identification of product information in a BIM model, the system is broken. That is something we want to fix.

When we have the nomenclature objects in place in the models, we can export IFC for the procurement phase. That means we can define the engineering bill of materials, and using the product data registry we can derive the manufacturing bill of materials. This allows us to digitalize the whole procurement value chain using Peppol. There is ongoing development in the Nordics on how to adopt Peppol in the procurement part of construction.

There is now a forum being established, and the work is going on to show how we can turn bill-of-materials information into messaging systems using Peppol to procure and order things.

What is this all about? It is all about linking the data. On top of the system I showed, we need linkages between different data sources. In Finland, we have a national interoperability platform for these codings, which means we need to link that to information standards.

We have an information flow from production in construction companies that needs to be linked. We need to link the supply chain to cover logistics and all procurement and ordering processes so that we can order the concrete, real products.

That is also linked to the information flow for financial information. That is one critical part we are usually missing. In every company involved in the construction value chain, financial information is very important because of CSRD reporting and similar requirements that financial departments are very interested in. These linkages should be in place, and that is one core part of the whole flow.

There is also ongoing research in Finland on how to define quality criteria for BIM models. That is one part of the foundation where we define a good basis for AI models. We need to be aware of how the geometry is represented in BIM models to make machine readability happen together with these linkages.

We need to define the information content and understand the variation in different information parameters within the models. These are all things we need to be aware of to make the BIM models as good as possible, and also to make good analysis and enrichment of bill-of-materials data possible. The core thing is that we need to define and understand these criteria overall in order to make this AI foundation happen.

Now we will showcase a couple of examples of how this can be done in real-world cases. Teo, the stage is yours.

Teo Arvian: I will talk a little about how we are using IFC files and how we combined that with air handling unit information. What Tommy showed is based on standardized IFC models, and that is the more important thing: to have understandable semantic information as input when we are moving toward AI.

This is one use case and a couple of ideas on how to handle information better once it has been categorized. It is not like a large language model. It is optimized, standardized, understandable semantic information that we can tell AI to use.

Earlier today there was also discussion about scalability. If we want to make business with AI or these kinds of tools, we need scalability and business understanding. We can do many pilots or one project, but if we want to support 100,000 projects with these tools, the system has to scale.

In my opinion, and not only mine, we need some kind of ontology. Right now, we do not have one ontology that covers the whole building lifecycle. This is a nice picture of that, made by Seppo. There are many ontologies that contain information in different phases of the construction project, but we need links to different ontologies as well. I do not believe, at least not now, that one ontology can solve everything. I think we need multiple ontologies.

Because I am a mechanical designer, I also want to show that we could have more information in IFC. We talk a lot about products and components such as dampers, fire dampers, ventilation, and so on. I want to remind everyone that there is an IFC port possibility inside the IFC specification that allows us to follow the route from the diffuser. If I click something in the ceiling, it goes straight to the air handling unit, and I have every component in the route. This is a starting point for how to build graph databases.

Of course, I want RDFs as well. I want to put IFC into a graph database. There is a tool for that also, made by Jyrki Oraskari: an IFC-to-LPD converter. There are tools to do really nice things. I do not exactly know why we are not using them more.

This helps explain why we need graph databases. This is the initial information we can provide when we are designing by simply clicking the right buttons in our software. We have an IFC model with IFC ports. Every component knows the route to the air handling unit. Then we have the air handling unit technical specification in some database somewhere, and we can link these together. That is how I can start to analyze the network.

When I put that information into the graph database, this is just an example, not a real situation, because I could not yet manage to do it with IFC ports exactly as intended. But the idea is that with the graph database, I can click something, follow things, and add more information. That becomes really useful when we embed IFC information into such a system.

One use case is analysis. Years ago, when I was developing digital twins, we looked too much at graphical interfaces all the time: click something and see information that maybe comes from multiple sources. What I want is analysis. With this kind of method, if I have bad air somewhere, I can click the diffuser, follow the route, and, if I have linked my IFC or graph database to the building automation system, I can see measurements. I can analyze what the problem is and why there are too-high CO2 levels, for example.

When we use ontologies and standardized data, we are able to produce scalable analysis environments. Just put the files in the correct place and you are ready to go. That is the idea of what we are trying to figure out in the data platforms. We need to get rid of IFC files and file-based systems. We need to be inside data platforms, and then the TET model Tommy mentioned will start to work.

Tommy Merolastero: In the last part, I would like to introduce one idea that is actually pretty new in Finland: how to make Model Context Protocol, MCP, part of this game. AI agents are evolving, but we need linked data and structured data to be available to the AI models.

MCP has been described generally as a USB stick for AI. We can outsource different tasks for AI using MCP. In Finland, we have defined interoperability definitions as part of the framework I presented. We have defined three different domains: reference data, logical data models, and terminological vocabularies for the built environment.

What is important here is that there is now a pretty new MCP implementation. We will provide this to you. It is defined in GitHub. We have defined an MCP server that can read all these interoperability guidelines: terminology, reference data, and logical data models, including how the data is structured.

As a case example, we used this MCP solution for reading the MEP nomenclature and joining that to real product information. I used Basta, a Swedish product data service, just as an example. I used an Anthropic AI model to put these two things together. I asked the AI model to merge the product information with the nomenclature, the Finnish code set, and it immediately worked because the MCP server can read the logical data model and join that to product information where we can identify the product with a specified nomenclature.

That means whenever we define a new nomenclature or more code sets for BIM models, we can teach that to the MCP server. This makes it fully scalable. We can give the AI model the concrete definitions of the data, which means hallucination is drastically reduced. I cannot say there is absolutely no hallucination, but it can be much more robust than general-purpose LLM-based AI models.

My experience so far, even with only a few trials, is that the MCP server works very well for the construction domain when we want to perform specified tasks and create a learning process for the AI model to enrich data. That is one part of the linked-data approach that makes graph-based data more efficient than previous approaches.

This is ongoing development and research in Finland, and I see it as one major solution for improving the construction value chain and data processes.

I want to close with one discussion point: we need to define the architecture. Enterprise architectures have not been very popular in recent times, but when we develop agentic AI across the whole AEC community, and also within one organization, we need to define the enterprise architecture in order to get a return on investment.

We need to know what data is in place. We need the data. We need to know what data processes exist in the community and within each organization. These are the parts that enable agentic AI at both the organizational and community level.

Audience member: Teo, you talked about digital twins 10 years ago. How does that differ from the digital twin in the future, after the kind of work with the TET model and everything you talked about?

Teo Arvian: In my opinion, the definition of digital twin differs depending on whom you ask. But the definition from 10 years ago is still very good. It has to include some kind of analysis and simulation. Some people say a point cloud is a digital twin; I think that is rubbish. We need analysis and simulations. We need to know how a building is behaving tomorrow, after a week, and so on.

For me, that kind of thing is a digital twin. It is an abstraction of reality, and the level of abstraction depends on the business case. You can see that in built environments. Building owners often have models, but they are not really in use.

The question is whether they will be in use in the future. I hope so, when we have scalable creation and updating of digital twins. Updating is the most problematic part, because we cannot even update drawings in buildings consistently. So how do we update building information models and all the information that changes? That requires a really big process change in facility management.

Tom Pattito: Hello everyone. Thank you for joining. Unfortunately, Boris could not make it, so I will have to entertain you.

Today I will talk about how we use AI to turn late-stage compliance into real-time design intelligence and guidance inside BIM.

As you all know, regulations and national codes are expanding and tightening. Climate requirements are becoming more demanding and detailed, liability is shifting toward designers, and complexity is just increasing.

Yet we still treat regulation as something we check after design decisions are made, as static external checks. This mismatch is becoming unbearable. In every building project, compliance sits as the barrier between architectural intention and obtainable permitting, but the problem is timing and bandwidth.

We design first and check compliance only after. It is like printing a chapter of a book and checking for typos only after it has been printed. This is the root cause of most delays, review cycles, and permitting blockers.

Compliance today relies on multiple costly consultants, each reviewing only a fragment of the regulations, and not all at the same time. There is no single consultant who knows all the regulations at once.

Ultimately, architects are expected to make sure that design intent, compliance, execution, and timing all align. They become the mediators of the entire process, translating clients’ ambitions into buildable design, aligning with regulations, coordinating multiple consultants, and still upholding their own design vision and office standards.

The whole process today is human-dependent and fragile. Architects design first, consultants review later, and authorities respond last.

Of course, this creates long feedback loops, weeks of interpretation, multiple redesign cycles, permit rejections and delays, and a lot of cost for the industry.

As one example, in a residential project, after almost a year of planning, the team discovered that because of a single definition in a district master plan, the technical roof counted as a full floor. Four apartments disappeared overnight. Millions in client revenue were gone. It was not a design mistake; it was just a single sentence in a PDF. But these mistakes were invisible during the design process.

Compliance has already changed elsewhere. In banking, AI-driven fintech monitors transactions continuously and flags suspicious behavior in real time. The same is true in health and legal tech. In those industries, the rules are complex and non-negotiable, but processes shifted from post-review to real-time guidance.

Compliance has already changed elsewhere. In banking, AI-driven fintech monitors transactions continuously and flags suspicious behavior in real time. The same is true in health and legal tech. In those industries, the rules are complex and non-negotiable, but processes shifted from post-review to real-time guidance.

Architecture has stayed behind, even though the stakes are high, the rules are dense, and mistakes are costly. We are still anchored to late-stage reviews and manual feedback.

Why do LLMs not solve it alone?

Architecture is special. It requires measuring live geometry, understanding relationships between elements, understanding floor-to-floor conditions, and providing citation and traceability, among many other things. Compliance is not a Q&A exercise.

Geometry matters. This is not just a language problem. It is a geometry and context problem. Buildings are spatial systems, and regulations are conditional spatial logic. LLMs alone hallucinate and work on probability, but answers must be deterministic, parametric, explainable, and geometry-aware.

Compliance checks should be based on geometry and context, not as a checklist but as a continuous reasoning layer—always present, always aware of geometry, always aligned with the regulation.

So the real question is: how do we embed regulation in the design environment itself, inside the workflow?

The answer is an AI-native compliance engine embedded directly inside BIM tools, working constantly in the background on live, evolving models.

It is like having one consultant who knows every regulation, has seen thousands of models and edge cases, and is continuously monitoring the BIM model in real time. When a design decision creates a potential issue, this engine flags it in the model within a few seconds, explains why it matters with broad cross-model context, cites the relevant regulation with a reference, and guides the user through actionable planning alternatives.

The architect remains in control. Responsibility stays human. AI only provides continuous visible reasoning.

To make that possible, we had to build a compounded compliance intelligence stack orchestrated by a multi-agent workflow that understands geometry, context, and building codes simultaneously. There are multiple layers working together and depending on one another.

At the foundation, we formalized human regulations and converted them to computable logic and rules. Then we move to BIM interpretation, which interprets complex, messy, ever-changing BIM models. On top of that sits a hybrid reasoning engine that combines rules, geometry, and context to evaluate conditions. Finally, there is real-time validation continuously inside BIM.

Perhaps the bigger gains will not come from doing the same tasks faster, but from deleting entire legacy steps and bridging between design actions and reviews. AI is enabling a system that acts as a catalyst for everyone involved in the project: developers, consultants, architects, and authorities.

To unlock this potential, we had to address three core challenges.

First, regulations are not machine-readable by default. Building codes were never written for machines; they were written by humans for humans. Turning regulatory texts and sketches into something a system can reason over requires interpretation, spatial abstraction, engineering judgment, and symbolic logic—not just training a model, but building an accurate regulatory reasoning layer.

Second, BIM models are not clean, standardized datasets. They are inconsistent and messy, firm-specific, and often incomplete. They reflect design intention more than technical correctness. Understanding a BIM model means knowing what a corridor is, what an exit is, what counts as usable space or structure when a room tag is missing, and what is merely an annotation versus an actual element.

Most generic AI tools can see shapes, but to reason about architecture they must understand geometry, typology, hierarchy, use, and constraint, all of which are interconnected and often conflicting.

Third, trust and responsibility are non-negotiable. In architecture, compliance decisions must be explainable, traceable, and defensible. Black-box outputs simply cannot cross that boundary. An AI system guiding compliance decisions must defend its reasoning by pinpointing the issue, highlighting it on the model, and citing the regulation accurately.

When you build a system that connects AI to regulations, BIM data, and human responsibility in real time, then AI can be used in the right way.

For decades, drawings were just lines on paper. BIM changed something fundamental. Models became organized, measurable, and data-rich. Walls have structure, doors have properties, rooms have areas and quantities—buildings became computable.

But compliance did not evolve with BIM. We still export 2D drawings, still check PDFs, and still review them manually or externally to the design process itself.

AI alone does not solve compliance, and BIM alone does not solve compliance. But by tapping directly into the BIM model, using it as live context for agentic workflows, the process becomes an ongoing conversation between design intent, code logic, and human decision-making.

This combination enables four things:

Real-time feedback: issues surface during design, not externally or in static snapshots.

This combination enables four things:

Real-time feedback: issues surface during design, not externally or in static snapshots.

Accuracy grounded in context: rules are evaluated against actual geometry and intent.

Scalability across jurisdictions: rules can be updated, expanded, or swapped based on location.

Holistic compliance logic: cross-referenced requirements from multiple disciplines are evaluated together, not as frozen snapshots or static non-contextual documents.

This means we can streamline all regulatory disciplines that are inherently data-driven in a BIM model. These are fields where compliance is grounded in measurable conditions such as areas, widths, heights, and distances between elements. These allow checks such as occupant load calculations, egress and corridor widths, required numbers of sanitary fixtures, and much more—and, most importantly, reasoning across all of them simultaneously and continuously in the background while architects keep working as they did before.

The system is designed to support compliance across the full architectural planning lifecycle, from early zoning and massing through schematic design, permitting, and into later bidding and coordination stages. Regulatory intelligence travels through the stages where most decisions are made.

This is not theoretical. Early signals show impact, and these results suggest that what used to take months with costly back-and-forth can become much faster if we use AI correctly by connecting it to the right data.

Looking forward, this approach scales far beyond a single plugin. Once regulation becomes structured and executable in real time, compliance can become a shared visible process, not fragmented or lost. That can eliminate rework loops and accountability gaps. It allows everyone to afford compliance architecture at scale, moving compliance from a review output to a design input.

We believe AI should amplify human agency, not automate it away. The real opportunity is not replacement but reallocation—freeing time for better thinking, stronger judgment, and better design decisions. The shift is significant, but it is not about removing human judgment. It is about supporting better judgment earlier.

Eyan Sharifan: Good afternoon, everyone. I am Eyan Sharifan, and today I am presenting “AI and HBIM integration for the sustainable and predictive rehabilitation of 20th-century serial social housing heritage: a state-of-the-art review,” which is part of my PhD project being conducted at the University of Lille in the LACTH laboratory.

First, we should start with the significance of social housing in Europe. Twentieth-century serial social housing is one of the largest and most vulnerable segments of the European built environment. Rehabilitation is the preferred strategy because of growing environmental and social imperatives, but these structures are failing across four dimensions: material aging, climate sensitivity, energy inefficiency, and fragmented documentation.

These social housing stocks are very important because of their scale, their historical significance, and their current urgency. They were industrialized and standardized when they were built after World War II. They are symbols of modernity and social progress.

But rehabilitation of this social housing is not the same as the traditional rehabilitation we have in older heritage buildings. There are some important differences. For example, these buildings contain dwellings and have different material characteristics from traditional historic buildings.

Demolition is not a good idea because it is not sustainable, so we try to rehabilitate them instead. But this rehabilitation process has gaps. We therefore need a stock-scale decision-making process, and AI may help fulfill that need.

As you can see, the technological promise remains unfulfilled because of fragmentation. Traditionally, we have HBIM—heritage building information modeling—on one side and artificial intelligence on the other. Both are well defined through research, but their combination and integration for social housing heritage is missing.

The core insight is that, despite significant advances, current digital tools for social housing rehabilitation remain weakly integrated.

Based on this, the review addressed four major questions:

How are AI and HBIM currently combined in the literature to support building rehabilitation?

To what extent do these approaches address sustainability, prediction, and decision support?

How frequently is 20th-century serial social housing considered?

What limitations, gaps, and methodological bottlenecks emerge from the analysis?

The methodology was a systematic and transparent review protocol. We reviewed studies indexed in Web of Science and Scopus between 2014 and 2025. Based on the PRISMA guidelines and our selection criteria, after title and abstract screening, 41 studies were deeply analyzed.

These studies were categorized into four segments: energy rehabilitation, HBIM, artificial intelligence, and socio-technical dynamics.

These studies were categorized into four segments: energy rehabilitation, HBIM, artificial intelligence, and socio-technical dynamics.

After reviewing and analyzing all studies, we identified four key findings: uneven pipeline maturity, limited predictive capacity, the serial housing blind spot, and the omission of human factors.

The first finding concerns digital pipelines. As I mentioned, there are many mature and automated studies in HBIM, for example scan-to-BIM and semantic segmentation. Machine learning can significantly reduce modeling time and improve consistency and documentation.

However, when it comes to AI integration, the situation is fragmented and disconnected. AI-based predictive models for energy optimization and related tasks are often developed outside HBIM environments. They rely on simplified archetypes instead of integrated building data, severely limiting their operational use.

The second finding is the limited predictive capacity and limited decision support. These social housing stocks need stock-scale decision-support systems for rehabilitation.

Although many studies claim decision-support objectives, implementation remains focused on isolated short-term performance metrics. What we actually need is a long-term, end-to-end framework that links HBIM environments with AI-driven multicriteria evaluation and uncertainty management for long-term survival. Such frameworks remain extremely rare.

The third finding, which is very important, is that serial social housing is a blind spot in the literature. There are many studies on monumental heritage and individual heritage buildings, but research on housing stock at scale is very rare. We believe the importance of these stocks has still not been sufficiently recognized.

The last finding is that critical human factors are routinely omitted. As I said before, rehabilitation of these stocks concerns whole-building rehabilitation and should be sustainable.

In Europe, for example, there are several programs working on this challenge. One is the New European Bauhaus, which calls for heritage that is sustainable, inclusive, and beautiful. To achieve these goals, we need to consider human factors, occupant behavior, social issues, and cultural value. Rehabilitation has direct social and political consequences. Current HBIM-AI frameworks are fundamentally unequipped to evaluate these dimensions.

If we map the landscape of gaps, we can see that most studies are concentrated in the lower-left area: documentation for monumental and individual heritage. When it comes to housing stock at scale and to prediction and decision-support systems, we can almost say we have nothing, or the studies are extremely rare.

The scientific problem space is therefore strategic rather than merely descriptive. We cannot achieve sustainable, territorial-scale rehabilitation of 20th-century social housing with current tools. This review establishes a precise foundation and the justification required to build new integrated AI-HBIM frameworks that can operate at both building and stock scale.

The conclusion can be summarized in three key takeaways.

First, the landscape is fragmented. AI and HBIM are still separate and are not integrated in a way that fills the existing gaps.

Second, the focus is misaligned. Current research over-optimizes short-term energy metrics, whereas we need long-term approaches.

Third, there is an urgent need. Across Europe, several programs are calling for sustainable rehabilitation, and AI may be able to help this process.

As for the next steps in closing the integration gap, future research should focus on developing an integrated model for real cases that can eventually be used more widely. This review is the state of the art within my PhD project, which will continue with real case studies in France, specifically in Lille and in other cities.

We will work on case studies referred to as model innovation cases, and we will try these tools and this integrated framework on them. After that, the integrated framework may be applied to other case studies across Europe.

The integrated framework will finally link HBIM knowledge environments with AI-driven prediction and design, explicitly for the unique conditions and scale of 20th-century social housing.

Thank you very much for your attention.

Eyan Sharifan: I do not know of a program specifically for the Netherlands. The New European Bauhaus that I mentioned is a European program, and there are other European programs as well. There were some studies related to rehabilitation of social housing in the Netherlands, but not necessarily integrating tools like HBIM and AI.

I think the outcome of this research will be an integrated framework that can be adapted to each case study by incorporating different kinds of data—for example heritage value, architectural design, and information from the dwellers. You can put all these different data sources into it, and that makes it useful for each country.

Christopher Mulhart: Hello everybody. I am Christopher Mulhart. I have been in the BIM space since about 2008, and over the last three years I have been investigating convolutional neural networks and related methods in a more practical way.

This is not to say it is the death of BIM, but the reality is that construction happens in a 2D drawing. That is what we are going to talk about.

I am here on behalf of my colleagues Hammad and Khan, who could not be here today. We are going to talk about what I tried to make into the most boring title possible: “Agentic versus convolutional neural networks: a comparison for object detection in 2D geometry.”

The reason I bring this up is that architects and engineers ultimately have to deal with people like me—people who used to be carpenters and just wanted to know where to put the stuff exactly as intended. The result of that need is the 2D drawing. That is the thing that actually happens.

One example is a door schedule. This is what you would expect to see, and we will talk about why that is important.

The real title I thought would be more interesting is: “Why you cannot yet just use Claude Code to successfully read construction drawings.” That is really what we are going to investigate.

On the other side is another architectural drawing, another construction document. It has tiny tags everywhere, and that is the kind of material we are dealing with. I spent a couple of hours last week trying Claude Code, and even Opus 4.6, the latest version, cannot do it. I am not saying that pessimistically. I am actually very optimistic about this work.

So what is going on in 2D drawings?

According to Autodesk, there was $65 billion a year in rework because construction drawings were not coordinated and were missing information.

Why? Because owners ask people to build things, architects design them, then hand them to engineers who check them. The architects then get approval to send them to the general contractor to build. The GC checks the drawings, sends them to subcontractors, and they check the drawings too. The whole thing starts over again when the project gets greenlit. There are many points in the process where human brains have to check things—for compliance, spelling errors, and many small but critical issues.

Why does this matter?

Because semantically checking that tags are actually in a schedule is extremely boring work—but it is also extremely important. You need to know how many doors are there and understand the details associated with them.

A company called Revizto Check in the United States wrote a 270-page book on how to read construction drawings. They describe three broad categories of issues: surface-level issues, which are basically whether something is present or absent; subsurface issues, which require some inference; and root-level issues, which are true constructability problems, such as a structural wall conflicting with a mechanical duct. That was the promise of BIM.

I want to make this concrete with a story. Harry—who is my wife’s younger brother, but I love him like my own brother—was doing his university degree and got a chance to intern at a construction company. He was given too much scope for his experience and made a $100,000 error.

He ordered more windows than there were openings.

Why? Because he trusted that the schedule would be correct. He did not know to count the openings; he just trusted that the architect would have updated the schedule and that everything would be perfect. This is a real problem, and it is why we need technologies like this. We need to teach people how to read construction drawings, but we also need tools that help.

What am I really going to talk about? Convolutional neural networks—broadly known as computer vision. These systems are cheap, efficient, and accurate, but they are weak at learning on the fly. In our experiment, we used a YOLO model, a type of computer vision model. We trained it on construction data that took about three weeks to annotate, meaning we manually drew bounding boxes around objects and fed that into a pipeline. Then we asked it to find those objects in new data.

It is really cheap—less than a penny to look for this sort of thing—and the inference time was around 30 seconds. So the pipeline was drawing input, CNN/computer vision, and then post-processing.

It is really cheap—less than a penny to look for this sort of thing—and the inference time was around 30 seconds. So the pipeline was drawing input, CNN/computer vision, and then post-processing.

Of course, there are also vision-language models. Because I am perpetually paranoid that AI is going to take my job, we also built a similar pipeline using Gemini 3.0, Claude 4, and related models. We looked at the whole page, then broke the page into 5-by-5 and 3-by-3 segments. We tried to improve results that way, but we were always struggling against the context window—that is, how many tokens you can spend relative to how much information the model can absorb and return. There are limitations not only in cost but also in the time required per class of object we asked it to find.

This is where it gets interesting. In construction and architecture, design intent is important, but the exact location and accuracy of things is critical. So we decided to play these approaches off each other. We asked whether we could integrate a voting system between the VLMs and the CNNs and see whether each could contribute to a more accurate result.

What we found relates to the key metric called intersection over union. This measures how well the predicted location of an object overlaps the true location. We found that the total accuracy improved dramatically when we combined the systems. This is a mixture-of-experts architecture: a more deterministic computer vision model and a more probabilistic VLM playing well together.

Breaking this down further, we studied the timing.

The YOLO models took about 30 seconds per page, making them by far the most responsive. What surprised me was the latency we introduced in the hybrid model when we played the systems off each other.

Then there was cost. I do not think most people are going to train their own YOLO model, so they probably will not get those dramatic cost efficiencies or build one from scratch. They are more likely to use a VLM. But those context windows compound costs quickly, and I will say more about that in a second. We did see a cost reduction in the hybrid system relative to a pure VLM workflow.

We also looked at precision and recall, which are what we really care about in machine learning. How well did the model recognize what it saw, and how well did it recall what it thought it saw?

Again, the CNN was way faster and more responsive, but the hybrid systems really did show a strong increase in total accuracy and especially recall, which is extremely important.

The LLMs and VLMs still had very low recall, and that is the critical measure. If you think of the brain, it is like recognizing someone’s face but not remembering their name. That is the thing we need the VLMs to do better. Even when testing the latest 4.6 models, that recall is not quite there yet.

That may be because only a tiny fraction of the internet consists of construction data. There is also a recent paper arguing that multimodal large language models are shape-blind, and I think that matters a lot for the construction industry. These models may be very good at analyzing text and fairly good at numbers, but we work in a world where everything is represented by some kind of shape with data inside it.

Now to the results.

What you are looking at here is a construction drawing. The little circles with triangle hats are elevation markers. The blue bounding boxes indicate what the model found. This CNN model was about 84% accurate. I went through and added red boxes to show what it missed.

In this case, the VLM was actually a little bit better for some reason, but it was extremely expensive. What cost me a penny in one workflow cost several dollars in the other. That is just one page. So if we are asking these systems to look through hundreds or thousands of pages, cost becomes a serious consideration.

The best results were definitely in the hybrid system, where we tried to leverage both models together.

I also want to step back and talk more about the VLMs and why they performed better when operating within the context of a CNN than when operating alone. If you imagine taking a sheet, breaking it into a 3-by-3 grid, and feeding those grids back to the LLM, or feeding the whole document with some assistance from the CNN, you can compare the outcomes.

In these examples, red highlights where the object actually is and blue shows what the LLM thought it saw. Again, there are highly geometric shapes everywhere in technical drawings, and the LLM by itself, even with a larger context window, was terrible.

Even when we added more prompting and used a voting system, it still was not very good on its own. But when we gave it just a little input—the intersection-over-union-based support from the CNN—the results improved.

So what happened?

The CNN still has the problem that it cannot learn on the fly very well. It struggles with imperfect shapes, rasterized tags, and all the realities of construction drawings, which are never perfect. Even within the same architecture firm, depending on the design team, you can get two entirely different types of PDF documents.

In the VLM case, many of the results were extremely ambiguous. It missed small tags, as you saw in the examples, even with an expanded context window. Keep in mind that these were VLMs from the previous generation, but I do not necessarily see them getting dramatically better for this specific use case very soon.

They hallucinated in highly complex areas. One important thing we found was that in the hybrid case, when the VLM was wrong and the CNN was right, the VLM could still override the CNN. That speaks to overconfidence. It was just too confident, and it would push the bounding box away from the correct location.

My final “why” is this: why does all of this matter?

Because we are at an engineering conference on AI. If AI technology is not extremely accurate in the output it gives you, then can it be trusted? Is it usable in its current form for anything beyond helping you create a visualization or write an email? In our domain, pinpoint accuracy has to be king.

I fundamentally believe we are in an age of staff augmentation, not team replacement.

And yes, I get it—BIM, and I love BIM—but PDF is still the way nearly all projects are permitted, contracted, and built. It all happens through the 2D drawing.

The prompt control I mentioned is extremely critical. You need very tight guardrails around how the prompt is managed. That is how you manage the confidence level of the LLM.

The prompt control I mentioned is extremely critical. You need very tight guardrails around how the prompt is managed. That is how you manage the confidence level of the LLM.

A simple example is that I like to call the LLM “OCR, but worse.” It had real difficulty distinguishing between a numeral 1, a lowercase l, and an uppercase I. Traditional OCR systems, which have existed for a long time, are very good at that. So we need to think about these tools in combination rather than assuming one of them can do everything.

That is my final point: these hybrid systems work well today to solve construction problems right now. In the future, perhaps world models or newer architectures will change the landscape, but for now we have immediate construction problems to solve.

Back in 2008, I thought that 10 years later everything would be in BIM. That was nearly 20 years ago. Yesterday I met with my old boss and joked that we should have built Vico for today—it probably would have worked.

Q&A

Audience member: From a data science perspective, accuracy is not always a sufficient metric. You also have to consider false positives, false negatives, and related rates. And I wondered why fine-tuning was not mentioned—hyperparameters like learning rate and batch size matter a lot, and people using these models may not have a data science background. How do you deal with that?

Christopher Mulhart: I do not expect a normal user—and by that I mean someone like a project engineer such as Harry—to care about batch sizes, workers, or any of that. That is why technology innovators and people building solutions are a critical part of this workflow. It goes back to my point that you cannot just use Claude Code to solve this; you will get terrible results.

On our side, this involved a lot of experimentation. We did some things that most people would consider slightly dangerous. I decided to build my own computer, so we built one, put it in my kitchen, and ran experiment after experiment after experiment.

That gave us the initial inputs to understand how to tune the models correctly—what batch sizes to use, where the memory caps out, and how to make these systems function properly on an AWS server. It is one thing to have large, memory-hungry models, but another to make them practical for field use.

So yes, it was a lot of experimentation to determine what was optimal for the result we wanted, so that we could build something meaningful for the end user who does not care about the internals. They just want to press a button and get a result. That is the beauty of the GPT ecosystem: you press a button and get an answer, and you assume it is right. In our case, we wanted to produce output that people could see visually, because construction is a visual industry.

Audience member: If you give more context to the VLM, will it affect the accuracy?

Christopher Mulhart: Absolutely. These things have to be very carefully containerized, and the way you structure the prompt matters a lot. Earlier in the conference someone mentioned that when they gave too broad a prompt, the outcome was terrible.

We are seeing that inconsistency in the output on the same document with the same prompt can already be too risky to use. That is a major consideration. This might work well for my own workflow, but how do I scale that to the next person or the next company? That is where the risk comes in, and maybe that is why LLMs are not always going to be the answer. That is really the whole point of this presentation.

Speaker: Bragan

We keep seeing headlines across popular media about how the AI future is already here. That reflects both the opportunity in front of us and the way work is changing through AI. What I find especially interesting is the second half of that idea: our AI future is already here, but it is not evenly distributed.

As we have heard throughout the day, the value of AI is being captured unevenly. A small proportion of firms are actually able to execute and implement AI effectively. One question I have always had is: why is that the case?

There is a real adoption gap in AI. In a report by Places Solutions on AI intelligence in construction, more than 400 people in our industry were surveyed. The results were surprising. On the ground, adoption is happening: 74% said they use ChatGPT or a similar model daily or weekly. But when asked whether their company is actually ready to use AI and withstand the coming disruption, 68%—about two-thirds—said their companies are not ready for the future.

Why are we seeing AI being used, while companies themselves are not ready? In our industry, AI is seen as high potential but also high risk. We see headlines repeatedly about people accidentally copying and pasting or uploading information they should not be putting into ChatGPT. More than one in ten people are seen to be doing this now.

In AEC, we work with confidential data: project data, owner and client data, and internal intellectual property. Many companies are rightly concerned about increasing exposure to these tools. A recent Dodge Construction Network survey found that one of the biggest barriers to adoption is concern about the security of company data. Data leakage through everyday use is a real issue.

So how do we resolve this? My view is that education is the way forward. We can implement learning and training programs that improve AI adoption in a safe and responsible way. Continuing on from the Dodge Construction Network survey, we are seeing this on the ground. Forty percent of teams are starting to allocate AI budgets, and 38% are creating implementation teams focused on rolling AI out safely and securely.

In this presentation, I want to show you exactly how to do that. I will walk through how to design, deliver, and operationalize an AI capability uplift and training program. My goal is that each of you walks away understanding how to map AI literacy in your organization and what training to provide to engineers and other people on the ground so they can start using AI and help address the adoption gap.

A quick background on me: I was a civil engineer at AECOM, working in pavements and aviation from both design and construction perspectives, including railway and aviation projects. I have also worked in innovation, embedded in a government infrastructure authority, where I worked on AI training and capability uplift programs. We designed and delivered workshops across the entire organization.

I also write a newsletter called Last Week in ConTech, which helps people stay up to date on construction tech news. I am currently on sabbatical, helping out as platform lead and head of content for Formwork Labs, an accelerator backed by Brick & Mortar Ventures.

My interest in education goes back even further. Before construction, I worked for a software agency called AirDev, where we built education programs to help people learn low-code systems. Ultimately, more than 10,000 people came through our bootcamps and were trained to work on client projects.

So how do we actually build an AI capability uplift program?

I want to take you back two years, when we recognized that AI was coming and starting to be used in our organization, but we did not know how to increase adoption. After brainstorming and workshops, we came to the conclusion that if we wanted to increase adoption in a safe and responsible way, we needed a structured training program.

This is especially important in AEC for a few reasons. First, there was no existing training tailored to our project context. Most of what we saw came from OpenAI, Claude, YouTube creators, and others, but none of it really understood the realities of projects. Second, as a government infrastructure authority, we held a lot of confidential data and had strong public-sector constraints. External providers we tried to work with did not really understand that context or the implications for AI guidelines.

So we developed a five-step framework for building the program.

So we developed a five-step framework for building the program.

Step one was assessing capability. We needed to understand where AI literacy and adoption were in the organization.

Step two was mapping personas. We grouped people into broad categories and then developed training specific to those categories so they could move up in their level of AI adoption.

Step three was training them in AI literacy.

Step four recognized that AI is constantly changing. In just the last few months, we have seen AI agents being deployed and new tools emerge. We needed a training approach that was not only structured and formalized, but also dynamic enough to adapt to new ways of working.

Step five was building citizen developers—our power users. How do you empower them? How do you help them feel safe and responsible in how they use AI?

Let me walk through step one: the survey.

The first question was simple: how often do you currently use AI? That gave us a quick sense of AI literacy and capability levels in the organization.

The second question was: do you use AI as part of your work today? Many people use AI tools personally, but do not feel comfortable using them in a work context, so we wanted to understand the difference.

The third question was: what is your biggest concern about using AI at work? People avoid using AI for many reasons, and any training program has to address those objections directly.

Finally, we asked: what topics would you most like to learn about? If you want adoption, the training must be relevant to project contexts and specific use cases.

The results were interesting. We saw concerns around job loss and data leakage. People were genuinely scared of using ChatGPT or similar tools at work because they feared making a mistake, having it become public, and losing their jobs. That was especially true in a public authority context, so we had to address those fears proactively.

We also realized that many people simply were not sure they were allowed to use these tools. Even though we had sent communications saying they could, people still did not understand how.

We also realized that many people simply were not sure they were allowed to use these tools. Even though we had sent communications saying they could, people still did not understand how.

Another major challenge was knowing where to learn. Even if you care about AI, there are not many obvious places to go. There are a few people online, but you need to know who they are. Inside an organization, people often do not know whether to talk to innovation, IT, or someone else. Who actually owns AI, and who can help?

From that, we mapped an AI capability pyramid.

At the base, we found that about 90 to 95% of the organization were AI novices. These were people who had either never used AI before or were scared and apprehensive due to security and data risks. Many were not technical and did not understand the software behind it.

For them, we created an AI crash course: AI 101. We brought them into a room, got them to sign up for ChatGPT, and had them start using it through guided exercises so they could experience the value firsthand. The idea was to put the tools in their hands and let them use them safely and securely, reducing risk through supervised use.

Before that, we covered some quick theory. First: what is AI and how does it work? You put in a prompt, you get information back, and what you write changes the answer. People needed to understand that basic interaction.

Second, we addressed strengths and limitations. Hallucination is one of the biggest concerns people have. If AI hallucinates once, many people stop using it. So we explained how hallucination works, the importance of human-in-the-loop systems, and the fact that AI is just another tool. If people do not know when and where to use it, they will not adopt it.

Third, we shared company guidelines: how we approach AI, what safe and secure use looks like, what people are allowed to upload, and what they cannot upload.

Third, we shared company guidelines: how we approach AI, what safe and secure use looks like, what people are allowed to upload, and what they cannot upload.

We also mapped four capability areas and created guided tasks, demos, and exercises so every person in the workshop could start experimenting and learning. Our goal with this group was simple: help people understand AI, build confidence to experiment, give them a clear sense that they had permission to do so, and reassure them that they could use it safely without risking their jobs, as long as they followed the rules.

The next group was the AI explorers. These were people who had some experience with AI tools and understood the basic concepts, but had not yet gained real value from them. In other words, AI was not yet part of their daily workflow.

That matters, because until people are using AI on a daily basis, they are not realizing the productivity improvements we want. So the key question for this group was: how do we move from experimentation to habit?

We did that by responding directly to what people said they wanted AI for, and by creating use-case-specific training.

The first was AI for writing: how to use AI for emails, reports, and improving clarity. This was especially relevant because many employees used English as a second language. Helping them write faster, review AI-generated writing, and understand when and where to use it gave them confidence and practical value.

The second was AI for Excel. This was very popular. We taught them how to generate formulas and, critically, what they could and could not upload. Because we were using free tools rather than commercial licenses at that stage, it was essential to teach data anonymization before uploading anything.

This was also the stage where we introduced Prompting 101. For novices, we did not teach prompting frameworks because they were still learning what AI was and how experimentation worked. But once people started using AI and saw how output varied, they were ready to understand prompting. We developed our own framework, which we called the Stingray model, to help people work in a human-in-the-loop way and get faster outputs.

Our goal at this level was to turn occasional use into a consistent workflow habit, so that every time people opened Excel or started writing, they knew exactly how to use AI, when to use it, and how to do so safely without leaking data.

The next phase was the AI practitioner—what we thought of as an AI power user. These were people who used AI regularly and had embedded it into their workflows. Not everyone would get to this stage; moving from novice to explorer to practitioner happens quickly, and the people who reach it tend to be naturally curious and excited about AI.

For this group, we wanted to create power users, so we gave them more tailored training.

First, we moved to business-line-specific training. Many of these people sat in specific business teams, so we surveyed them and built process maps of how they worked today. Then we looked at how AI could fit into that process and reduce 20 steps to five or six using different tools. We trained whole teams in these new processes, using champions within those teams to demonstrate and validate the changes.

Second, we created communities. These were small groups of people excited by AI who often felt isolated in their teams. Power users are often the only one or two people in a team who really love AI, and they do not have many people to share that excitement with. Community helped us distribute information and maintain momentum.

Third, we ran AI office hours. We would sit in the lunchroom from 2:00 to 4:00 p.m. on a Wednesday, and people would come ask questions. We would spend 20 minutes jamming on their use cases and acting as AI coaches. Many people wanted to improve, but did not know where to go for help. By being accessible, we built stronger ties across the organization and helped power users build capability that they could then pass on to others.

At the top of the pyramid were AI innovators. These were typically in the IT team and were building bespoke AI projects. We were not really training them—they generally knew more than we did from a machine learning and technical standpoint—but it was valuable to recognize that they existed and that we could tap into them for support.

The goal of the pyramid was never to make everyone an AI expert. We simply wanted to increase the general capability of the organization. Someone who has never used AI can still gain a lot from experimenting and realizing how it could help. From there, we want them to become explorers and form habits. A smaller proportion will become power users, and we want to nurture them so they grow and start teaching others—much like Excel experts have traditionally done by building templates and sharing them across organizations.

To make that more concrete, over a 12-month program we started with the AI novice group and ran AI 101 workshops as quickly as possible to lift organizational capability.

After about three months, we had enough trained people that some began graduating into the explorer cohort. If 90 to 100 people were trained in the first three months, perhaps 20 to 30 would move into the next stage because they were using AI enough to be ready for more use-case-specific training.

By months five, six, and seven, we started to see demand from the business. Those first people who got excited by AI wanted the organization to use it more broadly. At the same time, we developed our power-user community.

That was really important because it became our distribution mechanism for new ideas and updates.

One thing I want to highlight is that AI capability continues to increase rapidly. While we were proud of our program, we realized very quickly that it was static. It could take months to develop a new training stream, pilot it, test it, and operationalize it. Meanwhile, AI capabilities were changing faster than our ability to train people.

OpenAI has used the term “capability overhang” to describe this. AI innovation is drastically outpacing our ability to adopt new tools and absorb capability inside our organizations. Graphically, AI capability is improving at an exponential rate, while our internal training capacity tends to improve only linearly, based on how many workshops and sessions we can deliver.

That creates a gap. A new tool or capability appears, and we simply do not have the ability to train people fast enough.

So how did we try to resolve that? By identifying power users in every workshop and pulling them into their own community.

That creates a gap. A new tool or capability appears, and we simply do not have the ability to train people fast enough.

So how did we try to resolve that? By identifying power users in every workshop and pulling them into their own community.

In a workshop of 30 people, you start to notice the two or three who are most excited. They stay afterward to ask questions. They are naturally curious. It clicks for them quickly. They understand what AI is, they can see the value it brings, and they want to be more involved.

For that power-user group, we built communities around them. We used Viva Engage in Microsoft, set up a Teams community, and created a weekly AI bulletin.

The purpose of the bulletin was to capture the benefits of new AI technology and contextualize them to our roles, our company, and our industry. That was valuable because it exposed the workforce to new capability changes through the people most likely to experiment.

These power users would naturally try new tools. They would adapt interesting ideas to their teams. Because they were curious and enthusiastic, they would tell their teams when they saw something valuable—such as an AI agent doing something meaningful for their use case. And because we had already given them the scaffolding and foundation for safe and responsible AI use, they knew how to evaluate and apply those ideas. They would also come to office hours and ask us how something worked and how they could use it in their team.

Over time, we mapped these people across the organization. It was interesting to see that they did not sit in one team; they were spread cross-functionally. That gave us a more dynamic delivery mechanism. We could direct AI teachings and training toward the most engaged users and the most responsive parts of the organization. My hope was that, over time, this would improve organizational adoption by helping us see more quickly what resonated, what worked, and what was worth formalizing into training.

To finish where I started: our AI future is already here, and AI is already being applied on AEC projects and across organizations. The winners, in my view, will be the organizations that are actually able to adopt and utilize AI across their workforce.
