To finish where I started: our AI future is already here, and AI is already being applied on AEC projects and across organizations. The winners, in my view, will be the organizations that are actually able to adopt and utilize AI across their workforce.

I hope you can take something from this and use it to build programs that support the people in your organizations who are curious, excited, and ready to start using AI in their projects today.

Q&A

Audience member: Excellent talk. I meant to ask this in an earlier session, but it applies here too. How did you align incentives in the organization to encourage AI adoption? We talk about culture, but some people are creating valuable IP that is then owned by someone else. Was there consideration around that?

Bragan: It is a massive challenge. We also heard earlier about culture and how some people are excited to experiment. We were in a difficult position because we were a public-facing government organization, which traditionally adopts technology slowly. A major concern was data leakage. In previous years, we had incidents involving people speaking to the media, which reflected badly on the organization, so there was a lot of fear.

One of our biggest challenges was getting the organization aligned and backing us with a mandate to own AI training. We approached it in a very structured way: we created a SWOT analysis of key players and stakeholders and tried to figure out where we could partner with them or how our strengths could help solve their challenges.

We held individual meetings with all of these stakeholders and tried to get engagement. We had detractors, and in some cases we had to go over their heads to get executive buy-in. It was challenging because almost every team wanted some ownership. We literally mapped the people, what they cared about, what value we could provide to them, and what they were afraid of.

Some teams were worried that if we got the AI mandate, we would effectively take over their jobs. There were a lot of political dynamics to navigate. So we got as much buy-in as we could, ran pilots with people who were already supportive, and built strong grassroots evidence. Then we could go to executives and say, “We are the ones actually doing this.” That helped us secure the mandate to move forward.

It was a mix of top-down and bottom-up support. We had some top-down backing, but we had to build bottom-up support too. One cheeky trick that worked well was getting the executive assistants for the senior leaders into the training. They loved it, then told the executives how valuable it was. That became a very effective pathway to support.

Moderator: Here is a question from the remote audience. AI adoption is great, and your program shows that more people are becoming enthusiastic. But as that happens, more use cases emerge. How do you prioritize the use cases that come out of increased adoption?

Bragan: The innovation team in the organization was actually quite sophisticated. We had three teams. Our team focused on the AI mandate and idea generation. We also had an R&D team that worked with pilot solutions and startups directly on projects. Then there was a third team focused on scaling. Once we saw ROI on a particular project, that team could scale it across the organization.

What happens is that you give people the scaffolding to understand how to use AI, but then the question becomes: how do you identify what is working, what has ROI, and how to scale it? We already had that pathway because we had a history of working with startups. So if a promising use case emerged, we could hand it over to the other teams. They would build the process map, identify an AI champion, pilot it in other teams and projects, and eventually the scaling team would take over.

So for us it was a bit different because we already had that innovation structure in place. The project had had an innovation team for seven or eight years by that point.

Audience member: I want to confirm whether I understood your presentation correctly. Is there an assumption behind the adoption gap that the key barrier is people, rather than infrastructure or culture? In the company I work for, people are interested, the culture is supportive, and the data team is in place. The problem is infrastructure and data readiness, not people.

Bragan: I can give more framing for our specific context. Being in government, we had curiosity, but also a lot of fear. Culturally, we were slower to adopt and use technology. Importantly, we did not even have commercial licenses. We did not have a ChatGPT commercial license or a Copilot commercial license. Those were in the works, but government procurement cycles meant they were still six to twelve months away.

Because we were already seeing people use ChatGPT, we designed the training around free tools. No commercial licenses—just how to use free AI tools, which may use data for model training, in a safe and secure way. We covered ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude, and we believed there were many valuable use cases—from writing emails to using Excel—that were not core engineering tasks but still delivered value.

Also, because we were a delivery infrastructure authority, other teams such as legal, finance, commercial, and marketing could benefit too.

Your infrastructure point is very important, and I have seen that in other companies. Questions like which hyperscaler to use are major discussions. But at the core, AI models like ChatGPT provide value to almost any professional. Engineers also have non-billable tasks outside their core technical work where AI can help.

We were essentially saying: we know this technology provides value. How do we contextualize it to our industry and our organization, and how do we deliver training so people can use it safely?

So yes, people were curious, but there was still a lot of resistance. In government, we had people come to AI office hours and challenge us directly, asking whether this was going to take their jobs. In the private sector, there is often more recognition that people need to improve. In government, some people were strongly against what we were doing.

Sarah Port and Carla Valca moderated a panel discussion on the current state and future impact of agentic AI across the AEC value chain. Panelists included Dr. Sam Zoharan, senior technology director Kimba from Trimble, Dr. Simon from Arcadis, and a chief strategy officer from Sweco.

After brief introductions, Carla framed the discussion in four sections, beginning with the present state of AI and moving toward near-term and longer-term future scenarios.

The moderators first asked each panelist how long they had worked with AI and to rate their AI enthusiasm.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said his first serious interaction with AI dated back to 2016. At that time, he was exploring how generative AI could be used in design—not in the current LLM sense, but in the design process itself—and how machine learning could be applied in manufacturing. Coming from an AEC background, he worked on using machine learning to determine material types and 3D-printing machine settings so that printed parts would achieve the intended tensile strength and performance. On enthusiasm, he said he would not give a number, but that he dreams about AI sometimes and feels excited to be part of a technological wave comparable to the industrial revolution or the internet.

Kimba said he became deeply engaged with AI in 2019 through machine learning, after having followed the field earlier. Over the last three years, he had been closely involved in developing AI tools both internally and externally and had tried to act as an ambassador for their adoption. He rated his enthusiasm at 101.

Simon said he also started in 2016 while co-authoring one of his first research papers. He trained a simple neural network for prediction, initially thought he had reached 100% accuracy, and then discovered the training and test data had been mixed. He rated his enthusiasm at 100, though he added that some mornings it is 100 and then drops to single digits after reading his inbox. Overall, he described it as a growing rollercoaster.

The Sweco representative said his first real experience with AI was around Christmas 2022, just after ChatGPT launched, when his brother-in-law showed it to him over Christmas dinner and they used it to make Christmas rhymes. He rated his enthusiasm at 50. Professionally, he finds the technology exciting and fun to experiment with, but personally he is concerned about fake news, crime, fraud, and wider effects on humanity. He said he does not worry about his own role disappearing, but he does worry about the social consequences if a large share of white-collar work vanishes quickly, since work gives people meaning, community, and structure.

The panel then moved into the present state of AI adoption in AEC.

Dr. Sam Zoharan described a maturity pyramid from the book Disrupted that maps how AEC firms are reacting to AI. At the bottom are firms that see AI as hype and remain reactive. Above them are “tinkerers,” companies experimenting with many tools and chasing shiny objects. He referenced a conversation about there being roughly 1,700 AI tools and noted how overwhelming it would be for employees to test even a fraction of them. Above that are “adopters,” companies that begin with an inside-out strategy: they assess their own business, clients, and ecosystem, then identify solutions that meet those needs. At the top are “strategic innovators,” which he said are still rare. These companies are not just looking for AI tools; they are rethinking how value is created and delivered, including workflows that are no longer centered entirely on humans but on what is now technically possible. He summarized the sector as spread across the full spectrum, from minimal response to strategic innovation.

Kimba then asked the audience for a show of hands of those using AI almost daily in their work. Nearly everyone raised a hand. He observed that two years earlier, even among early adopters, only a few hands would have gone up. While this audience was a particularly engaged group, he said it still showed that people are already experimenting and seeing daily benefits. At the same time, he stressed that the industry is only at the beginning of what is possible.

The discussion turned to where transformation in the value chain should begin. Carla asked whether the push must come from owners and developers, consultancies, software companies, or elsewhere.

The discussion turned to where transformation in the value chain should begin. Carla asked whether the push must come from owners and developers, consultancies, software companies, or elsewhere.

Simon said the earlier pyramid described organizational maturity, but the industry can also be viewed as a workflow made up of requirements, concept, design, and building. What he sees today is isolated pilots running in each of those phases. Because the overall delivery system is still highly complex, he agreed that full workforce replacement is not near. The industry is still running fragmented pilots across the chain.

Asked who needs to “push the button” to make the leap happen, Simon argued that the industry should stop making itself smaller. AEC often describes itself as traditional rather than tech-first, but it needs to rethink how it operates and perhaps how it has been organized for decades. That is where differentiation lies: in connecting the dots and bringing people together across the fragmented system. He said there are champions across all functions, not just in enabling functions, but also in architecture, engineering, and project management.

The Sweco representative said Sweco decided early that the technology needed to be made available to everyone. In May 2023, the company launched a vendor-independent internal tool called SwecoGPT and then focused heavily on adoption. At the time of the panel, they had reached 70–80% regular usage if other tools were included. They were seeing a great deal of experimentation in the business, with people exploring how to change the way they work. He gave examples such as changes to groundwater calculations in Sweden and to tendering, though he said much of it was still at pilot stage. For Sweco, the next phase is to scale these efforts up and transform full disciplines and workflows. On the client side, he said there is strong interest. Clients want to know how AI is being used and how it benefits them, and some ask Sweco to help them with their own AI journeys. Sweco also sells some AI products, though he said the company is not “there yet.” Considerable progress has been made in three years, but more remains to be done.

Asked whether clients are using AI as a reason to demand lower prices, he said the more common issue is internal: consultants who are used to selling hours may save 10 hours with AI but still charge as if they had sold those hours. He said the industry has a deeply rooted habit of judging value by hours, and that behavior needs to change.

Simon added that the issue resembles a gear system. If one gear turns much faster than the others, the system breaks. If AI makes proposal writing much faster, but design teams, construction, or fabrication are not ready, then all that has happened is that the front end has accelerated while the rest of the pipeline remains constrained. The whole ecosystem must move in coordination.

The panel then addressed how organizations should handle internal enthusiasm for AI when resources are limited and many use cases must be rejected.

Simon said this is a major issue, but one that can be addressed by putting a process in place. Organizations need a way to collect use cases from employees, an initial review by someone who understands the relevant business line, and then a technical feasibility assessment. The good news today is that technical teams can implement pilot versions much faster than in the past, which also means ideas can fail faster. He recommended creating an innovation pipeline with clear criteria and short timelines. If no results appear within four weeks, he said, they likely will not appear in three months.

Kimba was asked how product teams are prioritizing amid the acceleration in AI development. He said product management has also sped up because teams can now create market requirements and product requirements documents much faster. The process of turning customer needs into something engineering can work with has been accelerated. He said it is now possible to move from zero to launching products in less than a year, which would have been unheard of for complex software only a few years ago. He also described a move away from many fragmented AI interfaces toward a more unified platform, with stronger plumbing underneath so individual experts can contribute domain-specific capabilities and create agents that help customers. At some point, he said, companies have to stop building seven different chat interfaces and unify them.

Still, he noted that every morning brings new developments. Depending on which “rabbit hole” you are in, five or ten significant new ideas can emerge over a weekend. That makes it extremely hard to keep up, especially given the gap between the pace of innovation and the pace of training.

An audience member then asked about data security, especially in Europe and in construction, where project data can be sensitive and even strategically important. The question focused specifically on where data is stored and how tools like SwecoGPT are secured.

The Sweco representative said he was not the technical expert on implementation, but his understanding was that no data is sent to OpenAI, Anthropic, or similar providers. Sweco hosts the solution itself in a contained environment. That concern about losing data was one of the main reasons Sweco built the tool in the first place.

Simon added that Arcadis had built a similar tool, Arcadis GPT, and had seen very similar adoption numbers. Arcadis uses a cloud governance model and an Azure-first stack. Proprietary LLM instances are hosted there, and the frontend connects only to those controlled environments. Around that is a broader governance layer that ensures no external access or data leakage. Access requires company devices and security controls. For highly sensitive work, including defense and security projects, Arcadis can create restricted instances for specific teams or projects so that even project data stays within project-specific boundaries. He said Arcadis launched its internal GPT in June 2023 and that the process of building it taught the organization, together with privacy and governance teams, how to structure its broader AI journey. Within three months, the company had developed its first AI policy, using the internal GPT deployment as the baseline.

Kimba added that this is less a matter of construction-specific policy and more a matter of general AI governance and company-level policies. In practice, he said, the setup is similar to other cloud solutions and common data environments, except using private instances in Microsoft, Google Cloud, or AWS, potentially located in a specific country or region depending on customer requirements.

The panel then shifted to 2026 and asked what needs to happen to make the market ready for a bigger leap.

The panel then shifted to 2026 and asked what needs to happen to make the market ready for a bigger leap.

The Sweco representative said one major change will be how business value is defined. If a company can do the same work in 10–20% of the time, then value can no longer be based in the same way on hours sold. AI should be thought of as a force multiplier that enables people to do much more. He argued that there are many tasks that are not done today simply because of time constraints, so AI creates an opportunity to improve quality, make designs more constructible, and allow firms to take on more work. He also pointed out that there are already innovators in the market who are not waiting for software vendors and are building their own capabilities.

He said the market is showing early signs in small chunks, but the bigger opportunities are still ahead.

Asked whether the market is truly ready, he said organizations still need to focus on adoption and learning. At the same time, it is time to start looking at core processes and how to transform them. One of Sweco’s assumptions is that they do not know exactly where innovation will happen: within their own business, with clients, among software providers, in foundational models, or through startups. For that reason, he said organizations need to remain very open, technology-agnostic, and willing to try everything.

Dr. Sam Zoharan agreed and pointed out that the audience itself included people from 36 countries, which already shows the industry is trying to understand what to do. He also noted that clients themselves are starting to use AI. If AEC firms do not adopt it, clients may use AI to evaluate the quality and consistency of their output. That creates a new pressure on firms to improve the quality of documentation, drawings, and designs so that they align with client expectations and standards. He cited HNTB’s research with DOTs in the United States as an example of clients trying to understand what AI means for them, and said this kind of pressure will expand across the ecosystem.

Carla then raised the issue of data. AI is often labeled onto everything, but what makes it different as a technology is its ability to automate and scale. Yet all of that depends on data—both documented data and tacit, undocumented knowledge. She asked what data in the AEC value chain is actually the most valuable.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said the answer depends on the opportunity. He used a grocery analogy: if you are cooking a particular meal, you do not buy the whole store. Likewise, companies should not try to “boil the ocean” by centralizing everything into a giant data lake if the use case does not require it. With AI agents, complete centralization may not be necessary. Instead, firms should identify the opportunities they want to tackle and determine what data those opportunities require. The valuable data is context-dependent.

Simon agreed that high-quality data is critical and noted that many stakeholders say, “Do the magic, all the data is there,” but when asked where it is, the answer is often simply “in SharePoint,” meaning an unstructured data dump. He warned against oversimplifying the challenge. For large organizations, getting data ready requires real priority, strategy, dedicated teams, and long-term investment. Large language models are trained on past data; they do not know a company’s business model, clients, or workforce. Those gaps have to be addressed intentionally.

Kimba offered several concrete examples of valuable data. One is data that machines can understand precisely, because robots, fabrication systems, and field automation require exact, unambiguous inputs. Another is simulation-ready data, a point also emphasized by Nvidia, meaning data rich enough to support many kinds of simulations. A third major area is maintenance, because that is where the majority of lifecycle cost in buildings lies; design is a relatively small portion, construction a larger one, but maintenance is the largest by far.

The final major section of the discussion focused on 2030 and beyond.

The final major section of the discussion focused on 2030 and beyond.

The Sweco representative separated the issue into “what” and “how.” On the “what,” he said many of the hours sold today will become worthless in the future, just as firms no longer sell the kind of paper-based drafting services they once did. Those lost billable hours must be replaced by something else. The easy answer would be to assume demand will simply expand enough to absorb the productivity gain, but he said that is unlikely. Firms will need to innovate and create new services, many of them digital and AI-powered.

On the “how,” he said the value chain historically used to be highly sequential: architects handed work to structural engineers, who handed work to MEP, and so on. Over time, those stages have already become more iterative, especially in complex industrial and sustainable projects. If each step becomes much more efficient through AI, then the entire chain may compress further, with more cross-functional teams working together in the same room, alongside the client, more like agile software development.

Simon agreed that delivery models are likely to shift away from selling hours and perhaps toward something closer to selling compute or API-based capability. He said organizations are also seeing more people across the board develop foundational understanding of AI. Two years earlier, there might have been only a handful of AI champions low in the organization; now, perhaps one in ten people has a strong basic understanding of the technology. He also took it as a positive sign that governance is already becoming a serious topic, because organizations would not focus on governance if they did not see real value in the technology. By 2030, he hopes organizations will have their own proprietary models and some degree of AI sovereignty. He sees a transition from humans being “in the loop” to humans orchestrating technology, and then selling that technology-enabled capability to clients. He said he does not fear that humans will disappear from the process.

Kimba said software development is already changing substantially. Talented developers are now spending much more of their time on architecture and code review while agents perform the coding. He called this a fact, though he noted that legacy software remains more difficult. He expects a similar shift in AEC, where construction workers and designers may effectively have seven or ten PhD-level assistants carrying out work under their direction. Any company that seizes that opportunity could potentially become ten times more productive. He added that AI today is likely the worst it will ever be; from here it will improve.

Simon pushed back slightly on the idea that LLMs alone will achieve full PhD-level general intelligence by 2030. He noted that models already outperform nearly all humans on certain narrow tasks, such as high-level mathematics Olympiad problems, but they can also behave like toddlers in ordinary interactions and make obviously poor decisions. That matters because in agentic systems, many autonomous decisions must be made, and even a few poor ones can undermine the entire output. He said he is not yet convinced that simply adding more data and more compute to current architectures will produce true continuous learning, creativity, and robust PhD-level reasoning across the board.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said he does not claim to predict the future, but he does see several trends by 2030. First, more AEC firms of all sizes will hire AI experts to work alongside domain experts. He pointed to examples from the day’s presentations in which firms had recently brought in dedicated AI talent. Second, knowledge itself is being democratized. Clients may gain access to levels of technical expertise that were once the exclusive domain of consultants. What will remain differentiating is perspective: the unique interpretation and judgment that professionals bring, beyond what a GPT can provide. That perspective may itself become a service. Third, he expects the industry to move beyond LLMs into other types of models, such as large vision models and eventually large domain-specific models. He suggested that by 2030 the industry may have an early but meaningful “large building model” that connects more of the system together. Finally, he warned about “agent sprawl.” Just as many firms currently struggle with data sprawl, the increasing ease of building agents could lead to proliferation of poorly managed agents. Organizations will need to address where agents live, who owns them, whether they remain accurate, and how they are governed.

Carla added another issue she felt had not been sufficiently discussed: infrastructure. If the future involves many AI assistants embedded across organizations, there is also the question of whether there will be enough energy and compute capacity.

Kimba replied that he is not overly worried about energy availability itself, though access and infrastructure matter. He also pointed out that Chinese developers, constrained by a lack of access to leading GPUs, have produced extremely efficient models optimized for older hardware. That research is published openly, and safe versions of those ideas can be replicated elsewhere. He expects continued gains in efficiency, lower compute requirements, and AI-assisted research that improves models further.

As the conversation drew toward collaboration, Carla referenced a prediction from Sequoia Capital that the next trillion-dollar company will be a software company masquerading as a service company. She suggested the reverse might also happen: service companies increasingly developing their own software. She asked whether the ecosystem—startups, academia, service firms, and major software vendors like Trimble—can build this future together, or whether competition will dominate.

The Sweco representative said he takes a collaborative stance. Customers are already multi-vendor and struggle with tool sprawl and data sprawl. Agentic AI could help bridge those gaps and make it easier for customers to work with their data. He said much depends on where one believes innovation will happen. If agents require many systems to interoperate, then engineering companies may be well positioned to drive value. If innovation is captured fully inside the design software itself, then engineering companies may have less leverage. His view is that it will require a mix of actors and happen on multiple levels.

Simon said that personally he is collaborative, but he is less convinced that the business world, shaped by profitability and competition, will behave that way in practice.

An audience member then asked whether compute should explicitly become part of the P&L and operating discussion at board level, given that software costs may rise significantly because of AI.

Simon said the tech function has historically often been seen as a cost center, but that mindset is slowly shifting. Cloud consumption is increasing month by month, and this is already visible in financial discussions. At the same time, AI growth creates new business opportunities—for example, in designing data centers and the infrastructure that supports them. He argued that organizations need to stay broad and agile in how they look for those opportunities.

The Sweco representative said software cost is already part of the P&L in practice, and AI is becoming a more strategic topic because digital is increasingly embedded in delivery. The key question should not be how to minimize the cost, but how to create return from it—how to get a 10x improvement out of employees.

An audience member challenged this framing by asking whether the industry might look back in 2030 and conclude that it simply hired a great many AI engineers, increased operating costs, and justified it by increasing revenue, without real proportional returns.

The Sweco representative responded that, at least so far, token spending has not become a major concern internally because the absolute cost remains relatively limited, and even if it were separated out as a line item, it would still be viewed as an investment worth making. He said there is no real alternative.

Dr. Sam Zoharan argued that return on investment should not be judged only in dollars. It should also be measured in quality and speed. If AI helps teams produce better work faster, that can justify the cost. He also noted that client expectations are changing. If clients can generate a rendering of a building in two minutes, even if it is not architecturally sound, they may still expect much faster response times than before. He added that job displacement is a real concern and one that governments will have to address. Some jobs may have been transitional roles that technology has now caught up with, much like elevator operators disappeared once elevator systems became automated. In some cases, companies may choose not to add headcount, and some of the money that would have gone to salaries may instead go toward compute and token costs, creating a new balance.

Simon offered a small analogy from one of his software development teams using GitHub Copilot. After three months, he asked them whether they would rather keep the Copilot licenses or receive a free lunch every week. They chose the license. To him, that was a strong signal that they were getting real value from the tool.

Kimba concluded that token spending is simply the price of entry now. Every company must do it. In software, any company not using AI today is unlikely to remain competitive for long unless it has an extraordinary moat. He said software was perhaps ironically the first domain where AI seemed to threaten workers directly, but what has actually emerged is the need for very good software engineers who can work effectively with AI and avoid low-quality output.

In closing, Carla thanked the audience for contributing questions on accountability, competitive advantage, governance, and more. She emphasized that the formal panel was ending but that the discussion should continue through the rest of the event.

Speaker: Mehra Safi, LUT University

Good morning, everyone. My name is Mehra Safi. I am a PhD student and junior researcher in the Department of Electrical Engineering at LUT University, working in the Laboratory of Electricity Markets and Power Systems.

Our main activities are related to energy systems and distributed energy resources, including demand response, energy communities, distribution grid development, rural electrification, power system operation, and applied optimization in the energy sector. We have been involved in multiple national and international projects. Currently, we are working on projects including DriveTix, ProSoni, Invest4F, and Cawings, funded by different organizations.

My own work mainly focuses on home energy management systems and building operation. For example, in the DriveTix project we have several pilots in the UK, the Netherlands, and Budapest. One example is a home energy management system user interface in the Budapest setup. It is based on a whole-system platform and receives input data from homeowners, such as departure time and desired state of charge. Based on forecasted home load and PV production, it provides optimal scheduling for bidirectional charging of an electric vehicle.

Today, however, I will mainly focus on the Smart Ery project, which concluded a couple of months ago. It was funded by Business Finland. The motivation behind the project was that buildings account for 40% of total EU energy consumption. At the same time, the increasing deployment of rooftop PV systems and dynamic energy market pricing create opportunities for homeowners to improve efficiency.

The challenge is the uncertainty between different parameters in the home, the complexity of the building environment for optimization modeling, and the requirement for a real-time, fast controller. Because of that, we chose deep reinforcement learning as a model-free artificial intelligence method. It removes the need for complex building modeling, handles uncertainties, and is very fast and responsive.

Reinforcement learning has also become extremely popular. In 2023, it was at the top of searched keywords on GitHub, which shows how widely it is being adopted by developers.

Our system setup is a building equipped with an energy storage system, PV panels, and HVAC, with bidirectional interaction with the grid through a home energy management system.

Our system setup is a building equipped with an energy storage system, PV panels, and HVAC, with bidirectional interaction with the grid through a home energy management system.

I will not go too deeply into the mathematical formulation, but the objective function is to minimize cost or maximize profit from interactions with the grid. We have power balance constraints, energy storage system constraints, thermal comfort limits—specifically, the indoor temperature should always remain between 20 and 24 degrees Celsius—and constraints related to HVAC operation and the thermal dynamics of the building. The optimization horizon is 24 hours with a time resolution of one hour.

For modeling the building, we used regression models as a machine learning method. We modeled the building, the HVAC, and other components using linear regression. To do this, we performed a step-response test in a commercial building in Lahti belonging to LSK, a technology company in Finland.

We used different sensors to measure temperature, CO2, and humidity inside the building. The test lasted 8 hours and 40 minutes, with measurements at 10-minute intervals. During the test, we toggled the HVAC power from 75% to 40% twice.

The thermal dynamics can be represented in a way that is suitable for linear regression. The temperature change depends on several factors, but all coefficients are linear, which makes modeling straightforward. One interesting result was that the correlation between the HVAC setback power and airflow in the channels was very close to 1—specifically 0.99—which made the regression modeling easier.

For the reinforcement learning unit, we used a Markov decision process formulation. The state includes the timestamp, the current energy price and prices for the next three hours, PV production, state of charge, and indoor and outdoor temperatures. This gives the agent enough information about the environment it is trying to optimize.

In Finland, we have access to 24 hours of energy prices in advance, so we can provide future price information to the agent. Giving the next three hours of prices improved optimization performance by adding some foresight.

For better neural network accuracy, we normalized both the states and the actions. The actions are the decision variables selected by the agent: charging and discharging power of the energy storage system, HVAC operation, and interaction with the grid.

For better neural network accuracy, we normalized both the states and the actions. The actions are the decision variables selected by the agent: charging and discharging power of the energy storage system, HVAC operation, and interaction with the grid.

The reward function has three parts. The first term is similar to the objective function, while the other two are penalties for comfort violations and power balance constraint violations.

The agent uses an actor network and a critic network. The actor decides the control actions, while the critic evaluates the decision based on the current state and outputs a value function. We used a soft actor-critic method.

Before discussing the optimization results, we validated the regression model. In the HVAC step-response test, where the power was changed twice from 75% to 40%, the predicted values tracked the actual values with reasonable accuracy. The maximum absolute error was 0.3 using this simple regression model.

This regression approach reduces the need for detailed information about the building, such as materials and all structural details, and significantly reduces optimization time.

During training, the reward started very low and after 4,000 episodes increased to around 1 in normalized terms, which I believe corresponds to around 2,000 cents per episode.

After saving the best model from training, we fed in the state and obtained the actions. Across four representative days in fall, spring, and winter, the results show that battery charging and discharging closely follow market prices. In the plots, the red lines represent energy market price, the blue bars represent battery charging and discharging, and the purple bars show grid transactions.

For example, in the top-left chart, during low-price hours at the beginning of the day, the battery charges using electricity from the grid. When prices rise, the battery discharges and energy is sold back to the grid. In this way, the system maximizes profit by participating in the market.

We also examined temperature profiles. The red line shows indoor temperature and the dashed blue line shows outdoor temperature. On all days, the indoor temperature was maintained within the 20 to 24 degree comfort range. For instance, in one winter case the outdoor temperature was below minus 10 degrees Celsius on average, yet the controller kept indoor conditions right at the edge of the comfort zone.

We also evaluated economic metrics. One important observation is that in spring, when there is sun in Finland, PV production significantly reduces energy cost. In one case, the system achieved about 14 euros per day of profit while still consuming energy. So the homeowner is not only using energy but also earning money by participating in this program.

In summary, this soft actor-critic implementation for smart home energy management showed strong economic benefits. Because we used black-box modeling of the building, we removed much of the complexity associated with modeling thermal dynamics in detail.

This was an offline learning approach, which avoids disrupting employees in a commercial building during training. After deployment, the controller can be further adjusted using online learning to fine-tune the weights and biases of the agent.

For future work, we would like to collect more step-response tests. We only had data from one month, which limited the model for warmer seasons. Additional tests in summer, spring, and other warm periods would improve the agent’s ability to control the building under different outdoor temperature conditions.

Thank you.

Q&A

Question: If you move this system to another building, do you need retraining?

Mehra Safi: It can be transferred using transfer learning, starting from a pretrained model and retraining in the new building environment. For better generalization, it would help to do some step-response tests in the new building. Generalization is one of the hot topics in the literature right now: how to generalize an agent developed for one building to many buildings. Transfer learning is one of the potential methods.

Question: Does it also depend on the sensors being very specific?

Mehra Safi: Yes, it needs sensors inside the building. The policy is updated based on feedback from the sensors in the new building, which tells the controller whether its policy is performing well or not. Then it updates its weights and biases accordingly.

Question: Among the features in your state vector, which were the most relevant?

Question: Among the features in your state vector, which were the most relevant?

Mehra Safi: It depends, and all of them are important. If you want to use the battery effectively, you need the state of charge. But I think the energy price vector made a huge difference. Giving the next three future prices significantly improved performance. Indoor temperature, outdoor temperature, and the other variables are also necessary. The current time step could potentially be removed, but keeping it gives the agent more information. I would say the first five elements were the most important for informing the agent.

Speaker: Babak Hassani, Istanbul Aydin University

Good morning, everyone, and thank you for being here. My name is Babak Hassani, and I am a PhD researcher at Istanbul Aydin University in Turkey. Today I will present my research titled “Data-Driven Spatial Optimization in Smart Buildings Using Digital Twin Feedback Loops and AI Simulation.”

This research explores how digital technology and artificial intelligence can help buildings adapt to the constantly changing needs of their occupants. In particular, the study investigates how digital twin systems, combined with AI simulation, can support dynamic spatial optimization inside smart buildings.

To begin, I would like to highlight a fundamental paradox in architecture. Most buildings are designed to last for 40 to 50 years. However, the way we actually use these buildings changes much more quickly. Work patterns, organizational structures, collaboration styles, and even technological tools evolve every few years. As a result, a space that was optimized during the design stage often becomes inefficient or uncomfortable over time.

In other words, we design buildings as static objects, but human activity inside them is highly dynamic.

When problems emerge, we usually attribute them to mechanical systems such as HVAC performance or lighting conditions. However, in many cases, the real issue is not the building systems themselves, but rather the spatial configuration of the environment.

This raises an important question: how can buildings become more responsive to changing human needs?

That question forms the starting point of this research. The objective is to explore how buildings can evolve from static containers into adaptive environments. Instead of treating spatial design as a one-time decision made during the design phase, we propose a system in which spatial configuration can be continuously evaluated and improved throughout the building life cycle.

To achieve this, the research introduces a framework that integrates two emerging technologies: digital twin systems and generative artificial intelligence. The key idea is to close the feedback loop between real-world building data and computational spatial optimization.

The architecture of the system is organized into three interconnected layers.

The first is the physical layer, representing the actual building environment, including IoT sensors installed throughout the building. These sensors collect real-time data about environmental and behavioral conditions such as occupancy levels, acoustic conditions, and thermal comfort.

The architecture of the system is organized into three interconnected layers.

The first is the physical layer, representing the actual building environment, including IoT sensors installed throughout the building. These sensors collect real-time data about environmental and behavioral conditions such as occupancy levels, acoustic conditions, and thermal comfort.

The second layer is the digital twin. The digital twin acts as a real-time virtual representation of the building. It continuously receives and processes data from the physical environment and updates the digital model accordingly. In other words, it functions as a dynamic mirror of the building.

The third layer is the AI agent, which acts as the analytical and decision-making component of the system. Within this layer, we apply deep reinforcement learning techniques to simulate potential spatial changes and evaluate their performance.

Through this architecture, the system becomes capable of analyzing spatial conditions and exploring alternative configurations computationally.

A core innovation of the proposed framework lies in what we call the digital twin feedback loop. In many current implementations, digital twin systems function primarily as monitoring dashboards. They visualize environmental data, but they do not actively participate in decision-making.

In contrast, our framework transforms the digital twin into an active optimization system. The AI agent continuously evaluates spatial conditions and asks a simple but powerful question: what would happen if the spatial layout were adjusted?

To answer this question, the system runs thousands of simulations within the digital environment. Different spatial scenarios are tested, including variations in furniture arrangement, zoning strategies, and circulation patterns. Because all these experiments occur inside the digital model, they can be performed without disrupting the real building environment.

Through this iterative simulation process, the system can identify configurations that improve both environmental performance and occupant comfort.

From a technical perspective, the framework bridges the gap between building information modeling and artificial intelligence. The geometry and spatial structure of the building are represented using BIM models created in Revit, which provide the static geometry and spatial relationships required for simulation.

From a technical perspective, the framework bridges the gap between building information modeling and artificial intelligence. The geometry and spatial structure of the building are represented using BIM models created in Revit, which provide the static geometry and spatial relationships required for simulation.

Python scripting is used to manage the data pipeline, integrate sensor information, and control the simulation process. The system is deployed on a cloud-based infrastructure using Microsoft Azure, which enables real-time data ingestion, scalable storage, and continuous integration of IoT sensor streams within the digital twin environment. Finally, the machine learning component is implemented using TensorFlow, where reinforcement learning agents are trained to evaluate and optimize spatial configurations.

Together, these technologies create an integrated workflow in which building data, spatial models, and AI algorithms operate within a unified computational environment.

Before applying AI simulation, it is important to understand what type of data drives the system. The simulation model relies on multiple data sources. These include occupancy patterns, environmental conditions, spatial constraints, and behavioral usage data. Together, these datasets allow the system to replicate real operating conditions inside the building. This data-driven foundation enables the AI agent to evaluate spatial performance more accurately.

At this point, an important question arises: is AI-based spatial optimization actually feasible in real architectural practice?

To explore this question, we can look at one of the most influential industry examples in this field: Autodesk’s MaRS office in Toronto.

In that project, Autodesk applied generative design techniques to optimize the spatial layout of an office environment. The design process was treated not simply as drawing a plan, but as solving a computational optimization problem.

Autodesk collected detailed information from approximately 250 employees. This included several types of spatial preferences: adjacency relationships, identification of which teams needed to collaborate frequently, acoustic preferences distinguishing employees who required quiet environments from those working in collaborative settings, and daylight access and environmental comfort preferences.

All these parameters were translated into computational constraints and objectives. Using them, the generative design system produced approximately 10,000 different spatial configurations. Each configuration was evaluated against multiple performance criteria.

The algorithm had to balance competing goals such as spatial density, access to daylight, privacy requirements, and collaborative proximity. This demonstrates how artificial intelligence can explore a design space far larger than what is typically possible through manual design iteration. The result was a set of optimized spatial layouts generated through computational exploration.

However, this example also reveals an important limitation: the optimization process in the Autodesk project occurred only once, before the office was built. After construction, the spatial layout remained relatively fixed.

Our research proposes the next step in this evolution. Instead of performing spatial optimization only during the design phase, we propose a system capable of continuous optimization throughout the building life cycle. By integrating digital twins and AI simulation, the system can repeatedly evaluate spatial conditions as organizational patterns evolve.

This enables buildings to respond over time to changes in occupancy, work patterns, and environmental conditions.

To test the feasibility of this approach, we conducted a predictive simulation based on a typical open-plan office floor. Within the simulation environment, the AI agent evaluated multiple spatial configurations while considering environmental conditions and occupant distribution over a simulated three-month evaluation period in a controlled digital twin environment.

The results suggested several potential improvements compared to the baseline configuration. They indicated notable improvement in energy performance and occupant comfort. This improvement was primarily associated with optimizing spatial density in relation to daylight availability. Although these results are based on simulation, they suggest that spatial optimization can significantly influence both environmental performance and user satisfaction.

Despite these promising results, several challenges remain. One of the most important concerns is human agency. If an algorithm suggests moving furniture or reorganizing spatial zones, how much control should occupants have over these decisions?

Our findings indicate that the system should not operate as a fully automated mechanism. Instead, it should function as a decision-support system for facility managers and designers. In this model, the AI provides recommendations and analytical insight, while human stakeholders retain final decision-making authority.

To conclude, the future of smart buildings may not depend solely on improving mechanical systems or energy technology. Equally important is the ability of the building to adapt spatially to human needs. By integrating digital twin feedback loops with AI-driven simulation, we can move toward a new generation of built environments that continuously learn from data and evolve over time.

Such adaptive environments have the potential to improve energy efficiency, spatial performance, and occupant well-being simultaneously. The true intelligence of smart buildings lies not just in their sensors, but in their ability to adapt to the people inside.

Thank you very much for your time and attention.

Speaker: Knut Nordanger, SINTEF Community

Good morning, everyone. My name is Knut Nordanger, and I come from the research institute SINTEF Community in Norway. I am a mathematician by training, but now I do applied research at the intersection of AI and the built environment.

Today, I want to talk about a fundamental shift in how we interact with our buildings.

The key point I want you to remember is this: AI becomes much more useful for buildings when it is anchored in structured metadata.

Every building around us is generating enormous amounts of data: temperatures, airflows, CO2 levels, occupancy, equipment states. Yet if you walk up to a building operator today and ask, “Why is my meeting room cold?” the answer usually involves a lot of manual digging, either by navigating complex dashboards or even by visiting the room.

So the question we are asking is: can we make buildings conversational? Can natural language become the primary interface—not just a nice front end, but the actual way people interact with building data day to day?

To achieve this, we need to be grounded in actual real data. We need to be contextualized to specific buildings. We need the results to be explainable so that we can trust them, and actionable so that we can do something with them.

A nice-looking chatbot is not enough. In buildings, we need trust and traceability so that we can act on the results.

If this were easy, we would have done it a long time ago. The reality is that building data is fragmented across incompatible systems. We have thousands of data points with inconsistent naming, locked inside technical interfaces. Critically, the data lacks semantic structure. That means an LLM cannot simply read it and reason about it reliably.

Without grounding, you may get fluent and convincing answers, but not reliable building intelligence. That is the core problem we set out to solve: not only making data accessible, but making the meaning of the data explicit.

Our answer to this challenge is an architecture built on three layers working together.

First, there is the semantic layer: structured metadata that describes what exists in the building—spaces, devices, quantities being measured, and how they relate to each other. This semantic layer can be grounded in established ontologies such as Brick.

First, there is the semantic layer: structured metadata that describes what exists in the building—spaces, devices, quantities being measured, and how they relate to each other. This semantic layer can be grounded in established ontologies such as Brick.

Second, there is the live data layer: real-time and historical time-series data from sensors. This is the raw signal from the building.

Third, there is the reasoning layer. This is where the LLMs come in. But crucially, the LLM does not operate directly on raw data. Every query, answer, and interpretation is anchored in the semantic layer. The structured metadata is the ground truth that prevents the model from going off script.

We are not working with toy examples. Our testbed is the ZEB Laboratory, the Zero Emission Building at the new SINTEF campus in Trondheim, Norway. It is an active office building where I have my office. It has real occupants and real operations, with over 1,500 sensors, 150 controllable objects, and around 17,000 data points.

So it is a high-tech building, and this is where we deploy, test, and iterate. Having a real, living building as a testbed means we have firsthand access to messy real-world data and can experiment with it directly.

Let me dig into the details.

A familiar situation is that metadata is often available in tabular form—for example, as an extract from a time-series database, with fields like system, component, floor, room, point, and the value of interest. Humans can often read this and understand roughly what it means, but machines cannot really interpret the relationships.

The system does not know what contains what, what a point actually measures, or how one column relates to another in a meaningful domain model. A flat table is readable, but not genuinely machine-interpretable.

Now consider the same data structured as a knowledge graph. We now have explicit, machine-readable relationships: room 3312 is on floor 3; it contains a component RT-601; RT-601 measures room temperature. The data has not changed, but the structure has—and this structural shift is everything.

A graph enables us to answer questions like “Which sensors are in room 312?” and “What does RT-601 measure?” with zero ambiguity.

This already shows the value of explicit semantic structure in itself. The next step is to use formal ontologies like Brick to further standardize and strengthen the model.

Let me move to some examples.

This already shows the value of explicit semantic structure in itself. The next step is to use formal ontologies like Brick to further standardize and strengthen the model.

Let me move to some examples.

One of our first prototypes, developed a couple of years ago when chatbots were seen as the answer to everything, still underpins the main point. It is a chatbot that lets users query live building data in plain language. There is no query language and no dashboard navigation—just a question.

You can ask structural questions like, “What sensors are in room 312?” and get a direct, accurate answer. You can ask for live data such as, “Show me the CO2 levels for the past hour.” You can even request data exports.

The most important point is that the system does not jump directly from the user question to the final answer. Instead, it first resolves the intent in the knowledge graph and then generates the relevant time-series query.

So the graph is doing real work here; it is not just decoration. Importantly, the LLM never “knows” what the temperature is. It knows how to ask the database for it. That distinction is what gives us reliability and traceability.

What actually happens when a user types a question can be described in four steps.

First, the user types the question, for example: “What is the room temperature?”

Second, the system performs a hybrid search to map “room temperature” to the canonical ontology label.

Third, the knowledge graph confirms that this sensor actually exists in the room and returns the system and point identifiers.

Fourth, the system generates a Flux query to InfluxDB, which we use as a time-series database, and retrieves the live value.

The LLM’s role is to orchestrate these steps and present the result in a human-friendly way—not to guess or hallucinate the answer. Every step is observable.

One of the biggest practical challenges in building chatbots is that people say the same thing in different ways. “What is the outside temperature?” “Show me outdoor temp.” “External air temperature.” These are all the same query, but a naive keyword search would struggle.

One of the biggest practical challenges in building chatbots is that people say the same thing in different ways. “What is the outside temperature?” “Show me outdoor temp.” “External air temperature.” These are all the same query, but a naive keyword search would struggle.

So our system combines three mechanisms to handle this. First, the ontology defines the canonical concept—outside temperature. Second, the graph stores human-readable labels and aliases. Third, for anything the label index misses, we use hybrid search to find the closest ontology concept. All three paths converge on the same canonical identifier, which is then used to look up the actual sensor.

As a result, the user gets the right answer regardless of how they ask.

A few words on scaling, because getting the semantic graph in the first place is a real barrier to adoption. Traditional manual ontology mapping requires a specialist, takes weeks, and produces error-prone, hard-to-maintain results.

The shift we are aiming for is from hiring an ontology expert to having a building engineer with a spreadsheet spend a few hours. The output should be validated, consistent, and regenerable.

Domain experts describe what they know, and the system handles the rest. We use reusable ontology templates to solve this. The templates themselves require manual work, but once defined, domain experts can use them with their understanding of the building, which saves a lot of time when producing validated knowledge graphs.

So the graph is built. What can we do with it beyond answering questions?

This brings us to prototype number two, which moves from talking to buildings to interpreting them.

In a traditional model, a building management system sends an alarm: room 312, temperature low. The operator sees this, does not know why, and has to investigate manually. That takes time, wastes effort, and often leads to wrong conclusions.

Our “digital janitor” runs continuously, monitoring all sensors in a room simultaneously. When it detects an anomaly, it does not just flag it. It reasons about why.

It correlates the temperature reading with the window sensor, the heating setpoint, and the outdoor temperature, and then produces a diagnosis. For example: the room is cold because the window is open, and you should close the window. That is a simple example, but the key mechanism is that the LLM reasons across sensors using the ontology as a map.

It knows which sensors are relevant for explaining a temperature anomaly because the graph encodes these relationships.

What makes this different from a simple threshold alarm is that the reasoning is context-aware. It knows the room, its sensors, and what normal looks like for that specific space.

Internally, we combine the ontology, the structured room data, and time-series data with user-defined reasoning conditions—essentially a prompt—and send that into an LLM-based reasoning step.

Again, the key message is that the LLM is not reasoning in a vacuum. It is constrained by room context and by sensor evidence.

Let me show a simple example. In a dashboard for normal operation in room 3312, all 15 sensors may be reporting normally and there are zero active anomalies. On the panel, each sensor reading is not just shown as a raw number; it also comes with a natural-language interpretation.

More interesting is what happens when something is wrong. Suppose it is early morning and the room temperature is 10 degrees Celsius. A traditional alarm would simply say “temperature low.” The operator would not know whether this was due to a broken heating valve, a scheduling issue, or something else.

Our system produces something fundamentally different. In the detected anomaly panel, it may state that the room temperature is significantly lower than the heating setpoint of 21 degrees, and that this discrepancy is explained by the window being fully open, as indicated by the window-status sensor.

And this is the key point: it suggests a specific action—close the window to prevent heat loss and allow the heating system to maintain the desired temperature.

So the operator does not need to begin a manual investigation or go up to the third floor to discover the cause. The building has already explained why the room is cold.

Let me close with where we are and where we are going.

We have demonstrated two of the three capabilities.

First, talk: natural-language queries against live building data. We have done this, and it works.

Second, interpret: LLM-based anomaly detection with causal reasoning. We have also done this in our lab.

Third, act: automated execution of suggested interventions. This is ongoing work and will be presented at another conference later this year or before the summer.

There are two takeaways.

First, observable retrieval matters. Answers are grounded in graph lookups and database queries, not free-floating LLM output.

Third, act: automated execution of suggested interventions. This is ongoing work and will be presented at another conference later this year or before the summer.

There are two takeaways.

First, observable retrieval matters. Answers are grounded in graph lookups and database queries, not free-floating LLM output.

Second, interpretation beats dashboards. The goal is not more data; it is understanding. When buildings can explain themselves, operators make better decisions faster.

That is the shift we are after.

In conclusion, ontologies are not just a data-engineering concern. They are the foundation that allows AI to move from chatting about documents to actually managing real buildings.

Thank you.

Q&A

Question: Do you collect data from actual occupants—comfort satisfaction, interaction with heating controls, opening windows, and other issues linked to human behavior?

Knut Nordanger: That is something we would like to do. We do not have that system in place today for occupant feedback, but it is something we are looking into. We actually have a project that will investigate this from both a technical perspective and a human and ethical perspective.

Question: Have you tried the system with data from different buildings, which might be structured differently?

Knut Nordanger: So far, we have tried this in our own building. Scaling this to different buildings is an ongoing research area for us, and definitely a hot topic.

Question: Did your research suggest new sensors or new automations? For example, if a window is left open all night, at what point would it make sense to automate closing it? What is the cost-benefit threshold for automating such actions?

Knut Nordanger: Those are absolutely relevant questions. We are always looking at expanding the system. Our current focus is on taking the recommendation and implementing it automatically from a technical perspective. We do not yet have that optimization perspective worked out, but I think that is a natural extension.

Question: Have you considered adding a human feedback loop to make correctness of the results more deterministic?

Knut Nordanger: It is on our list, yes. We have not done it yet, but we would very much like to move in that direction. It is an interesting topic.

Speaker: I am from Prairie View A&M, part of the Texas A&M System. Today I’ll be presenting an agentic AI workflow for urban heat island analysis.

Let’s start with the urban heat island challenge. About 68% of the world’s population will live in cities by 2050. Urban heat islands increase temperature by 1 to 10 degrees Celsius. This is no longer just about comfort; it affects energy consumption and degrades air quality.

The good news is that we now have a lot of data. There are about 1,200 satellite images taken daily worldwide. However, urban heat island analysis still involves a great deal of manual work. It is cumbersome and fragmented, with many tools and many steps, so it is difficult to automate. In addition, interpreting urban heat analysis requires domain expertise and technical knowledge. As far as I know, there is still no end-to-end automated analysis workflow.

My research objective is to design a multi-agent AI workflow for urban heat analysis and demonstrate its capabilities through a case study in Houston, Texas. The workflow evaluates NDVI for vegetation coverage and NDBI for built-up coverage in correlation with land surface temperature. It also uses AI-driven analysis, including computer vision, cloud-based AI vision, and large language models, and assesses scalability for multi-city analysis.

I built a dashboard as the front end for users to conduct urban heat island analysis. In the background, a multi-agent workflow runs automatically. The dashboard allows the user to choose either a single city or multiple cities. There is also a setting for cloud coverage when retrieving satellite images from Google. I designed the dashboard for five cities, but it could be expanded to all cities in the world. At the end, the user simply clicks Run.

The pipeline in the background has multiple agents or steps. The first AI agent acquires satellite images from Google based on specifications such as cloud coverage, time of year, and resolution. The second agent preprocesses the images and begins generating composite products such as vegetation coverage and land surface temperature.

The pipeline in the background has multiple agents or steps. The first AI agent acquires satellite images from Google based on specifications such as cloud coverage, time of year, and resolution. The second agent preprocesses the images and begins generating composite products such as vegetation coverage and land surface temperature.

Then comes segmentation, which takes about 15 minutes to half an hour depending on the size of the city. This is a computer vision process in which the AI identifies each segment based on urban features. For example, it may recognize one part of the map as residential and another as highway, segmenting the city into different pieces according to urban characteristics.

Tiling then divides those segments into smaller units so that we can analyze individual tiles rather than an entire large city like Houston at once. Finally, the workflow aggregates all reports at the city scale and exports the results.

The key technologies include Google Earth satellite imagery, Python, and Flask for the dashboard, with AI vision and large language models in the background.

When I click Run Pipeline, you can see the process in the log. First, it acquires the imagery. Preprocessing and segmentation take about 15 to 20 minutes, but tiling takes about 2 to 2.5 hours. The whole pipeline takes about 3 hours. That means you can analyze an entire city like Houston in roughly 3 hours. You can start the process, go grab lunch, come back, and the whole analysis is done automatically.

I see this as valuable not only for experts such as urban planners but also as a teaching tool for students. Students can sit down, inspect all the tiles, and understand the process without downloading imagery, building analyses from scratch, or writing segmentation code. That is a very difficult process.

Once the run is complete, the dashboard shows the results. You can view layers such as land surface temperature, segments, and tiles. For a city like Houston, we ended up with about 6,000 tiles. Each tile includes land surface temperature, location, map statistics, and AI-generated analysis.

Once the run is complete, the dashboard shows the results. You can view layers such as land surface temperature, segments, and tiles. For a city like Houston, we ended up with about 6,000 tiles. Each tile includes land surface temperature, location, map statistics, and AI-generated analysis.

The large language model can describe a tile, for example by saying that it looks like a residential area with limited vegetation and that this contributes to higher heat. You can also ask follow-up questions. For instance, you can ask why a particular region is so hot, what is driving the temperature up, and the system will give recommendations and suggestions. In that sense, the AI does not just generate an analysis; it also allows interactive follow-up on the analysis.

The system also produces statistics automatically. You can access documentation, analysis outputs, and distributions of pixels by temperature. The dashboard includes a 4-by-4 grid over Houston, and each grid cell has its own analysis and mean temperature. At the city scale, the report identifies the hottest regions, the coldest regions, and provides downloadable files.

You can also question the large language model about the entire process. If you want to compare two specific tiles, such as tile 300 and tile 250, you can ask why one is hotter and the other cooler, and it will help you interpret those differences.

At the tile level, the system provides land cover classification, identifying whether a tile is residential, commercial, park, highway, industrial, and so on. It also provides an intensity assessment and identifies the primary heat drivers in each tile. For example, for an industrial area with no vegetation, it will indicate that the tile is industrial, lacks vegetation, has intense built-up cover, and that these factors are driving the heat upward. It also generates mitigation recommendations.

These recommendations can include interventions such as cool roofs, permeable pavement, and parking-related strategies. At the end of the process, the system also produces comprehensive reports. AI generates visualizations, statistics, reports, and raw data. If you want to export the results to other software for additional analysis, pixel-level temperature data are also available as raw data.

The case study area is Houston. It is the fourth-largest city in the United States and a very large city geographically. It is also hot and humid and is urbanizing very quickly. Canopy coverage is limited in some places, and vegetation is not abundant across intense urban areas.

After running the process, the reports include summary statistics, temperature distributions, vegetation density maps, and built-up intensity maps. The temperature distribution is approximately normal around 33 degrees Celsius.

One particularly interesting result comes from the correlation analysis. On the left is vegetation coverage versus land surface temperature, and the slope is negative, meaning that increasing vegetation reduces temperature. That is expected. But the graph on the right shows built-up area versus land surface temperature, and the slope is positive. More importantly, it is about seven times more dramatic than the slope for vegetation. In other words, in Houston, built-up intensity affects temperature about seven times more strongly than vegetation coverage does.

The 4-by-4 quadrant analysis also provides statistics for each grid box, including mean temperature, hottest areas, coolest areas, and related metrics. The workflow also produces hot and cold pixel distributions, again showing that temperature is normally distributed across Houston around 33 degrees Celsius.

In conclusion, this is, to my knowledge, the first fully automated tool of this kind. It may not be perfectly precise, because it relies in part on large language models, but I see it as a great teaching tool. As a professor, I think it is excellent for showing students immediate results about the urban heat island effect in a simple way that is accessible to nontechnical and nonexpert users.

The tool does have limitations. It takes about 3 hours for Houston, which is fine for a single city, but if you want to do batch processing for multiple cities, it could take days. So it is not as fast as one might imagine. Still, it provides tile-by-tile analysis for a city and produces a large number of analytical visualizations and reports within about 3 hours per city, which is valuable.

Cloud-based AI vision helps bridge the gap between measurement and planning by generating recommendations. That recommendation layer is driven by large language models. I will be honest: if I were an urban planner, I would not want to rely solely on recommendations from a large language model. But it can still be a useful quick-analysis tool, especially for students and early-stage exploration.

For future work, I want to extend the system to multi-temporal analysis. The current workflow analyzes only summer conditions, but it could be expanded to summer, winter, spring, and other seasons. I also want to use higher-resolution imagery and conduct multi-city comparative studies.

Question: How long would it take to do this without AI?

Speaker: Manually, it would require a great deal of technical expertise. You would need to understand mapping libraries, Python programming, and computer vision analysis. With this workflow, you simply choose Houston, Austin, or another city and click Run, and everything happens in the background. Producing all of these reports manually would be very cumbersome and would require many steps and significant expertise.

Question: How long would it take to manually interpret the satellite image with your own eyes and prepare the same kind of report?

Speaker: An expert can often look at the map and understand some of the main drivers. For example, in one of the slides there is a long line in the Houston map that corresponds to a highway. It is also one of the hottest features in the land surface temperature map, which makes sense because there is traffic, industrial activity nearby, no vegetation, and a lot of asphalt. So experts can interpret these patterns. But this tool is still useful, especially for students, because it lets them inspect small areas one at a time, understand what is driving the heat, and think about mitigation options.

Question: You talked a lot about academic use, but could this also be a great tool for policymakers, for example for quick analysis when planning new urban development? And could policies be built into the program so that the mitigation options align with city policy goals?

Speaker: That is a great idea. I had not thought about it, and it could become future work: integrating policy and recommendations into the workflow. Bart Big began by framing the issue at a macro level. He said that much of what is shown at conferences tends to be at the project or organizational level, but his work in the Netherlands focuses on how asset owners, research institutes, governments, and industry can move collectively. The challenge, he argued, is too large for any one actor to solve alone.

Speaking from his background in engineering and more than a decade in digital transformation, he described the Dutch situation as a useful case study. The Netherlands faces growing societal and economic pressures: aging bridges and infrastructure, housing shortages, and increasing demand, all while labor productivity in construction has stagnated or even declined over the past decade. He noted that this is not only an industry problem but a broader societal one. At the same time, many digital methods, even BIM, are still not fully implemented across the sector.

He emphasized that the challenge is not simply doing more with the same resources, but doing more with less. In the Netherlands, as in many European countries, a large retirement wave is approaching, which means substantial knowledge will leave the sector in the coming years. In his view, two things are therefore necessary: much stronger collaboration on innovation and knowledge development, and a better connection between societal challenges and the opportunities offered by technology.

He explained that about three years ago, a national platform was created in the Netherlands to address this collectively, and he now serves as its acting managing director. The platform works through programs and missions that bring together the ecosystem around shared challenges. He highlighted “Build by AI” as a way of ensuring that daily work bridges the gap between challenge and technological opportunity. He connected this to what is often called the twin transition: the sustainability transition on one hand and the technology transition on the other.

He noted that these transitions create dilemmas. Sustainability and resilience often have their own ecosystem; technology has another. AI itself introduces paradoxes, such as the energy use required to run it. Construction, he said, remains near the bottom of the charts in AI adoption in the Netherlands, although there are signs of progress.
