He was pleased that both European and national policy now increasingly recognize construction as an important sector for AI, not only because it is large, but because it enables the functioning of other sectors. High-tech industries, for example, cannot function without housing, roads, and logistics infrastructure.

At the national level, he said, the Dutch strategy includes several important design principles. One is federated data sharing. Another is the evolving role of people: moving from doing the loop, to being in the loop, to owning the loop. He stressed that the core challenge is not bringing more AI specialists into construction, but making construction professionals AI-minded. In his view, people account for 80% of the challenge.

After attending the conference two years earlier, he wrote a paper asking how AI could help address the renovation challenge for bridges and houses. He concluded that AI needs to be placed on the agenda in every innovation effort, including innovation programs where it is not always explicitly considered. The sector needs to learn by experimenting and applying AI in practice, build ecosystems where domain knowledge and AI expertise truly collaborate, develop a digital mindset, and establish governance around ethics, data use, and trustworthy sharing.

He said the first step in the Dutch effort was simply to start sharing what already exists. The platform began inviting people to present use cases and examples, and it launched a podcast series where CIOs from the industry discuss their challenges in applying AI. The purpose was to reach a broader group of business leaders, not just people already inside the AI community.

As the work matured, the focus shifted to putting the challenge first. In housing, for example, the real issues include permitting, long value chains, complex decision-making, and involving future buyers or users. These are the areas where AI can create value. He argued that the ecosystem must collaborate in open and trustful ways, using open standards and federated data sharing. Economically, he said, the sector has little reason to compete so aggressively when there is more work to be done than any one company can handle. The need is for collaboration, mindset change, scalable building blocks, and systematic sharing of best practices.

He then described a concrete initiative launched on January 1: a Dutch AI for Housing program. The government allocated €1 million not to execute a predetermined master plan, but to explore together with the housing ecosystem how AI can contribute. The program has four main components.

First, it aims to build around 15 simple value cases that demonstrate where AI creates value and where collaboration is necessary. Second, it is developing an AI mindset framework tailored to the sector, applicable to housing corporations, construction companies, and public-sector employees dealing with housing challenges. Third, it is working on the boundary conditions and frameworks needed to do AI properly, including technical and ethical aspects. He gave the example of startups having promising first discussions with clients, but then running into difficulties with privacy officers because the sector keeps reinventing how to handle such issues. Standardization and guidelines, he said, would be highly valuable. Fourth, the program continues to strengthen the ecosystem itself.

He hopes that in a year he will be able to present results not only for housing but also for infrastructure, showing how real collaboration can work across asset owners, engineering firms, contractors, and research institutes.

He also outlined a broader framework for implementation. Foundational enablers include data standards, mindset, and ethics. On top of that, use cases should be examined from multiple perspectives. One perspective is individual productivity: whether AI helps people work better or merely creates new problems. Another is workflow integration within companies, such as tendering processes where AI agents and agentic workflows are increasingly common. He remarked that this raises interesting questions about what happens if both sides of a tendering process automate decision support. A third and more difficult perspective is embedding AI into buildings, smart products, components, and projects at scale.

To make the discussion tangible, he presented two examples. One concerned roads and degradation prediction. The important lesson was not the technology itself, but the need to bring together data from asset owners, municipalities, contractors, and maintenance companies, combined with domain and AI expertise, to better predict when maintenance is needed. In a city like Amsterdam, he noted, opening roads repeatedly is highly disruptive.

The second example concerned older housing in the Netherlands. Many homes built around 50 years ago need renovation, but methods for scaling this work are limited. He explained that buildings from the same era often use similar systems, and AI can help identify which systems were used by combining visual data and computer vision with interviews with architects, building archives, and other information. These multimodal approaches can support better decisions about what to renovate and when. He stressed that this kind of effort needs to be done as a public-private partnership because it creates collective knowledge that benefits everyone, even if the business case for any single actor is less obvious.

He closed by saying that if the sector wants to improve productivity and meet its societal obligations, it must step up collectively. AI offers real opportunity, but the sector needs to work together, learn together, and apply together, and it needs to accelerate.

In the discussion afterward, he clarified that the Dutch work is national in scope but designed as a public-private partnership. Government has multiple roles in the sector, especially in infrastructure: asset owner, legislator, and permit provider. However, he stressed that the work is not only for government and asset owners. It requires shared structures and platforms where public and private actors can collaborate and co-invest.

Asked about contractual models such as integrated project delivery or collaborative contracts, he said that contractual arrangements are both a barrier and an opportunity. In his view, more is possible within current frameworks than many people realize. He cited examples such as procuring five schools at once or 300 bridges at once, which can create new dynamics. Longer-term programs make innovation and implementation easier. At the same time, he said, European legal frameworks are still not fully designed for today’s challenges. So contracts are both part of the solution and an area that still requires innovation.

Petra Svensson Claeson and a colleague presented the results of a Swedish project on generative AI and business models. Petra explained that the project, titled Generative AI: From Threat to Opportunity, was initiated to explore how AI affects the business models of architects and technical consultants. It was a strategic innovation project under Sweden’s Smart Built Environment program, and sustainability was a core part of the work. The team wanted to understand not only how AI could enable new business models, but also how those models could support the green transition.

The project was planned about two years ago, when generative AI was just beginning to gain momentum. At that time, there was little research and almost no established business models for architects and technical consultants in relation to AI. Because of that uncertainty, the team worked in an exploratory way through a series of workshops involving 30 companies representing roughly half of the Swedish technical engineering and architectural consulting industry.

Petra stressed that a business model is much more than a revenue model. It concerns the value proposition, how value is generated, how it is calculated, and how it is captured. Within the project, the team developed about 20 AI-related business models. They shared two central characteristics: first, they involved automation of cognitive work, meaning the storage, processing, and use of information; second, they were data-driven, with data collection, processing, and analysis forming the basis for decisions, execution, and follow-up. As others had also noted during the conference, good-quality data is at the core of everything.

The team was excited by the prospect that AI might finally disrupt the traditional business model structures of the industry. Petra noted that architects and consultants have largely worked with the same business logic for decades. They hypothesized that new technical skills and capabilities enabled by AI would create new business opportunities. To understand this better, they looked historically at how digital development in the sector had influenced technical skills, technical capabilities, business opportunities, and business capabilities.

They created a simple model dividing digital development in the sector into six stages. The first was the analog era, when work was done with pen and paper. The second was the digital era, where the pen and paper were replaced by computer, screen, and mouse, but work still remained largely siloed. Then digital development continued in ways that enabled more integrated work. BIM, for example, made it possible to work across disciplines and bring more participants into the process, but it also required standardization of data and methods. Parametric design then enabled more advanced analyses and data-driven decisions, including sustainability analyses that can help guide better choices in design. The current stage is one in which nearly everyone is experimenting with AI, often to improve existing work, make it more efficient, analyze information, generate content, or simulate options. The next step, she said, is the AI-driven stage.

The interesting finding was not only the technical progression, but how each stage had opened access to new markets and expanded the potential for new business models. Petra described three broad markets. The first is the project services market, where companies know the client, usually a property developer, and where professional expertise is the key resource. The second is an integrated delivery market, where digital solutions themselves become a key resource. In this market, new actors are entering and providing digital tools that support work, and the client may be someone anywhere along the value chain or even a competitor. The third market, which AI helps open, is the data market. There, data itself is the key resource, and the client may be anyone, potentially from another sector or another part of the world. Petra noted that despite these shifts, most firms still rely on the same traditional commercial logic: hourly billing, percentage fees, or fixed prices.

Her colleague then explained why those traditional models are under pressure. In Sweden, architectural and engineering services are commonly billed by the hour. That model assumes value is produced through human labor. But with each step in digitalization, and especially with AI, quality and precision increase while the effort required to perform tasks decreases. Machines increasingly produce value and deliverables. Firms still pay for hardware, software, tokens, and competence development, but those machine-related costs are difficult to charge for in the same way as human labor. At the same time, if AI allows the same work to be done in fewer billable hours, the old model starts to collapse.

As AI tools become more capable, more tasks lose economic value because AI can do them faster, cheaper, or better. Yet some capabilities remain valuable, and may even become more valuable. These include human qualities such as intent, strategic understanding, original thinking beyond statistical recombination, lateral thinking, judgment, contextual understanding, leadership, relationship-building, negotiation, and leading complex processes. Looking one another in the eye, as he put it, still matters.

Another major finding was that companies will need to operate with more than one business model, and that business models will evolve over time. Right now, many firms are trying to realize efficiency gains through AI while keeping a fixed-price or hourly model, particularly if they move early and gain a short-term advantage. Another strategy is to compete on quality and precision, possibly moving toward outcome-based models where the consultant is rewarded based on results. But he cautioned that both efficiency and precision will eventually become commodities as AI becomes widespread.

Because of that, the team believes firms must also prepare for the future by building knowledge and assembling unique data sets that can support future business models. Within the project, they identified four broad strategic directions for architects and engineering consultants.

The first is the “super consultant,” essentially the industry’s current trajectory: using AI to deliver better and faster within existing processes through incremental transformation. The second is to explore value in other parts of the value chain, extending into earlier stages, construction, or operations. The third is the “tech shark,” where firms develop proprietary AI tools to create new businesses and business models. The fourth is the “data hamster,” where firms gather and control data in order to create insight-driven businesses.

They briefly presented two example business models developed in the project. The first, called Tailor-Made, is a platform for homebuyers that allows them to make advanced adjustments to apartments before moving in and visualize how their furniture would fit. Residential developers would pay for the platform because it would help them sell more effectively from drawings and reduce the risk of last-minute buyer withdrawal. As with all the project’s business models, it had a clear data strategy.

The second example, Great Place to Live, was shown because it is built directly around data insights. It is a platform that calculates a social sustainability score for cities, allowing them to compare themselves and receive suggestions for improving attractiveness and social sustainability. Because it is data-driven, clients who contribute data could receive a discount or even free access.

Petra then turned to the question of implementation. Generating business model ideas was stimulating and relatively easy, especially with creative participants. The real challenge is how to move from ideas into transformation, particularly in an industry with a traditional and deeply established business logic. The opportunities are large, and there will not be just one new model but many.

She noted that there is already considerable research on how digital development affects companies, and emerging research on how AI affects them. One key finding is that top management must be deeply involved. The transformation requires major effort from companies and affects technical skills, culture, organizational structure, and more. Drawing on doctoral research by Rebecca Ungström, she said this is not a one-time transition but a continuous development process. AI is evolving at a hockey-stick pace, so companies will have to adapt continuously. The result will be both internal and external shocks, new roles, and changing relationships between roles.

The project has published all of its tools and results on the AI Business Guide website, including the literature review, expert interviews, business models, and practical tools to help companies of different sizes navigate the transition from one business model stage to another. The website is in Swedish, but it can be translated, and an English PDF version with all diagrams is also available.

In the discussion afterward, Petra was asked how participants’ attitudes had changed over the course of the project. She said that in the beginning many thought AI would not affect their businesses very much. By the end, there was a strong sense of urgency. Participants felt they had to get started immediately, but there was also excitement because the opportunities were so significant.

Asked how to help people inside organizations see data as a product rather than just information being entered into systems, she said this begins with a company-level data strategy. That is a top-management issue, but each company must start from its own circumstances: what data it has, what clients want, and what clients need. She added that, from a sustainability perspective, data created today enables better action tomorrow. For architects in particular, she said there is still a tendency to focus mainly on creativity, but AI will push the profession toward greater use of data to multiply and apply knowledge. She also emphasized the need for ecosystem collaboration, since small firms often do not have sufficiently large data sets on their own, and important data may sit with other actors such as operations teams. One insight from the project was therefore that the ecosystem will need to work much more closely together.

Professor Pia Sormunen and Dr. Oskari from Tampere University presented the AI Champion project from the perspective of research closely connected to business practice. Pia introduced herself as an associate professor at Tampere University who also works in the private sector at Granlund in design, facility management, and property management. She described this as having one foot in the university and one in industry. Oskari, a postdoctoral researcher, said his background is in information systems science, construction management, economics, and technology, placing him between several fields.

Pia began by describing the current construction process as messy and fragmented. Data is spread across many platforms and databases, and too often reduced to PDFs. She argued that this has to change. Her aspiration is that one day building handover will include all material, equipment, and building information in a smart data package. Some people say this already exists in the form of digital twins, she noted, but in practice those solutions are not yet working well enough.

Oskari added that even the simplified diagram they showed understates the actual complexity of the supply chain. Data is siloed, legacy systems are common, and the installed base is extensive. The key research challenge, he said, is how to embed AI into this kind of complexity.

Pia said the consequences are substantial. Time is wasted digging for information, and workers on construction sites spend too much time looking for products and equipment. She said only about 20% of contractors’ workers’ time is truly effective. In Finland alone, about €400 million per year is spent on quantity calculation. She questioned why this is still necessary when BIM models already exist. The sector builds 3D models, prints them out in 2D, and then recalculates quantities manually, losing valuable data in the process.

Oskari said the problem is that while data exists in IFC models, what AI systems need are not static IFC files or PDFs but usable structured data, for example in knowledge graphs and databases. If high-quality design data could be properly connected with AI, he argued, the sector could achieve remarkable things.

Pia then described a Finnish consortium project called Talotech 2030, involving 15 MEP companies and two universities, which has worked on these issues for two to three years. Through that work, they concluded that several actions are required.

Pia then described a Finnish consortium project called Talotech 2030, involving 15 MEP companies and two universities, which has worked on these issues for two to three years. Through that work, they concluded that several actions are required.

First, BIM must be fixed. She explained that MEP design alone can contain around 450,000 objects, and all of that data must be standardized. In practice, it often is not. AI is not “angel dust,” she said; it does not solve everything automatically. Data must be standardized and preprocessed for context windows and models to be used effectively.

Second, the sector needs smart databases rather than relying on the current idea of digital twins as they exist today. In her view, the future will still involve fragmented databases, because every company has its own systems, but AI should be able to work across those databases.

Third, once the data and database foundations are in place, AI can be integrated into everyday work and used to create new kinds of data products. If the sector understands products better, it can also understand and develop data products.

She then introduced the AI Champion project itself, which has just secured €20 million in funding for research and collaboration with companies. The project aims to address the fragmented supply chain and build much more efficient supply-chain management. The ecosystem includes a large set of partners, including international university collaborators and AI companies. The goal is ambitious: to create at least 100 AI agents to help deliver data within supply chain management.

Pia stressed that the work is not only about AI. It also requires study of processes, of how people currently work, and how that work should change when supply chains become AI-enabled. The project will investigate collaboration among AI, information management, and construction industry actors. It includes companies covering the whole supply chain: a design company, a construction company, and a product manufacturer. This, she said, is essential for building a realistic model of how the supply chain actually functions.

The project also has a major educational component. It includes funding for 13 doctoral students who will work on these issues, because human skills remain essential. She emphasized that future talent development is part of the transformation.

The project also has a major educational component. It includes funding for 13 doctoral students who will work on these issues, because human skills remain essential. She emphasized that future talent development is part of the transformation.

Oskari then gave a practical example using quantity takeoff. He noted that because quantity takeoff has such high economic impact, it has also become a focus of hype, with many AI demos circulating online. Some use large language models and tools like Claude to read static IFC files and extract quantities. These demonstrations can be convincing, he said, but they still suffer from serious problems of accuracy, consistency, and explainability.

If a quantity changes from one query to another, the user needs to know why. Is the cause the prompt, the data, or the randomness of the model itself? Without consistency, such solutions cannot be trusted in production. Oskari said he would rather see solutions where IFC data is converted into linked building data and knowledge graphs, which can then be connected to large language models. That would make the resulting data usable across the full downstream supply chain.

Drawing on his information systems background, he argued that digital infrastructure in the sector will always remain imperfect. BIM models will never be perfect because humans are involved. Product data will always have gaps. Different stakeholders will continue to use different systems. The supply chain is project-based, and the same data is handled by different actors at different stages. In that context, fully deterministic automation is impossible. Some level of incompleteness is unavoidable.

He referred to a classic illustration of “islands of automation,” saying that standardization can make those islands larger but cannot eliminate them entirely because infrastructure is open, evolving, and changing.

His hypothesis for the role of AI agents is therefore not that they replace all uncertainty, but that they act as translators and mediators where information is incomplete or imperfect. The sector needs to determine the minimum level of standardization necessary in IFC files and product data so that AI agents can enrich and carry data through the supply chain. He gave the example of digital product passports being introduced in Europe. Standardized product data may support automated environmental reporting, but procurement automation would require even richer technical data.

AI agents, in his view, can fill gaps in standardization, interpret incomplete information, and support continuity across fragmented systems. He said this hypothesis has not yet really been tested in information systems science, nor sufficiently in AEC.

Toward the end, he highlighted broader trends affecting the field. Intelligence itself is becoming a commodity, and AI capabilities that currently require the cloud may in a few years be able to run locally on mobile devices. Coding is also becoming a commodity: software development cycles that once took months or years are now shrinking to weeks, days, or even hours. This has major implications for legacy systems, installed bases, and digital infrastructure.

At the same time, he argued, the main bottleneck to AI adoption is no longer the technology itself but the inertia of the field. That is why multidisciplinary research is necessary. The sector must study existing processes, carry out radical business process reengineering where necessary, understand incentives, and use institutional theory to make sense of non-technical complexity.

He showed a comparison between the amount of automation that is already achievable in different fields and the much larger amount that may theoretically be achievable. In architecture and engineering, he said, a large share of tasks may eventually be automatable, although the exact number is debatable. The important point is that the industry is still at the beginning of that journey.

He also noted that while software developer job postings remain strong, major technology firms are laying off thousands of developers. In his interpretation, those developers are not disappearing; they are moving into the effort to fill the still-unrealized potential of automation, including in construction, because it is becoming economically feasible to build software and implement AI for the sector.

Pia concluded that transformation depends on people, processes, data, and AI together. Oskari summarized the core challenge as business and IT alignment: technological capabilities are advancing rapidly, but organizations need to understand in detail how to align them with real business processes, data governance, and operating models. The industry must prioritize real use cases with companies rather than imagining thousands of abstract possibilities. Because AI agents are becoming part of digital infrastructure, this is fundamentally a research challenge that requires close collaboration between academia and industry.

Tommy Arola, joined by Jonas Lehtovaara and Petri Mariavaara, presented the Finnish AI Playbook for the AEC sector. Tommy explained that the playbook had first been developed for the Finnish ecosystem and has now been published in English for the wider international AEC community. He said the work started in 2024 with the aim of supporting the localization of AI both at the organizational level and at the level of the broader Finnish community.

He noted that while AI applications are growing rapidly and big technology companies play a major role in shaping large language models, the question for the AEC sector is how it can participate in and influence this development. That led the team to focus on localization: what kind of solid foundation AEC needs for AI application, both within organizations and across the community.

Tommy said the team observed several characteristics of the current AI landscape in AEC: many applications, uncertain return on investment, high energy consumption, and growing concern about the environmental impact of AI. At the same time, organizations need to understand what AI means for their own workflows and practices. They also found that copyright and data ownership concerns are significant.

For that reason, the playbook was built around two major parts: a situation picture and practical content. It addresses data usage agreements, risk management, data traceability, criteria for good use cases, and new business opportunities. The playbook is organized into five thematic sections. Because the field is evolving so quickly, it is designed as a living document.

Tommy highlighted data agreements as a cornerstone. The playbook raises key questions organizations need to answer when agreeing on AI data usage, including how long data will be used and under what terms. Risk management is another major theme. Organizations need clarity at project and organizational level about how to trust AI solutions and how to manage associated risks. The team also created an infographic with key takeaways for both individuals and organizations, summarizing the main lessons from all sections of the playbook.

Jonas then discussed what the playbook means at the organizational level and shared three main observations from the work.

Jonas then discussed what the playbook means at the organizational level and shared three main observations from the work.

First, AI enables new kinds of business. He said these opportunities can be divided into two broad categories. Some business opportunities are extensions of things already familiar in the industry, such as value-driven consulting, but they become more visible and more viable as AI enables new ways of working. Other opportunities are genuinely new business models, such as those connected to the data economy, which have only really become visible in the last few years. He emphasized that organizations do not need to abandon everything they currently do. They can build on existing strengths, but doing so requires time, effort, and resources. Companies that want to be forerunners may need to invest more heavily in exploring these opportunities now.

Second, Jonas stressed that AI is not only a technical issue. It must also be approached as a matter of social and organizational change. Organizations need to train their people, ensure they have the resources to understand and use new tools, and prioritize foundational knowledge rather than chasing only the latest features. The playbook therefore emphasizes so-called meta-skills, including awareness of data security and other underlying competencies that support responsible use.

He also emphasized well-being. If organizations try to pile AI learning and implementation on top of employees already working at full capacity, the change will not be sustainable. People need time to learn, reflect, and absorb the cognitive load of change. Organizations must make sure they are not overburdening their teams during the transition.

Third, AI is changing roles and skills in AEC. Jonas gave the example of project engineers, whose work has traditionally been heavily knowledge-driven, involving data compilation and support for project managers. In the future, such roles may evolve toward something more like AI agent team coordination, requiring new kinds of skills. He stressed that AI is not simply replacing jobs; rather, it is altering existing roles and creating new ones.

He then identified three skill areas that he sees as especially important for the future. The first is critical media literacy: the ability to assess whether information from the internet or generated by AI is valid, trustworthy, and properly sourced. The second is awareness of information security and the risks associated with AI and digital technology. The third is critical reflection on workload, both for oneself and for colleagues, so that organizations create space for learning rather than trying to add AI responsibilities on top of everything else.

Petri then turned to practical uses of the playbook. He offered three general tips. First, in the long run it makes more sense to focus on the ethical use of AI and smart practices than on chasing rapid technological shifts. Second, the playbook contains a lot of material, so users can select the parts most relevant to their project. Third, AI training should start during project onboarding, because organizations cannot assume all participants have the same background or level of AI maturity. Projects also need a clear AI policy that everyone understands and commits to.

He then presented a case study from Helsinki: a major infrastructure program involving urban development and multiple light rail alliances. He noted that the overall effort extends to 2033. The goal is to build on strong knowledge management systems by adding AI in a responsible way: responsible to people and to the environment.

Within this program, they describe an “AI journey” in which everyone is involved. They have introduced a concept called Project AI. The first step is to teach people what AI is. The second is to teach how AI relates specifically to construction in Helsinki. From there, they move toward a project-specific knowledge environment.

Petri explained that they are building what they call a context sphere, underpinned by a knowledge graph. It includes all documents, decisions, timelines, and even discussions explaining why decisions were made, so that tacit knowledge from experts can be captured. The aim is not only to store explicit project information, but also the reasoning behind it.

The next stage is to provide people with AI tools that support their daily work. On top of the unique project knowledge, the intention is ultimately to give people agents and AI-supported flows they can use in their routines.

The next stage is to provide people with AI tools that support their daily work. On top of the unique project knowledge, the intention is ultimately to give people agents and AI-supported flows they can use in their routines.

Tommy concluded by emphasizing that the playbook is openly available and intended as a foundation for further work. Some organizations in Finland have already started using it to develop their own principles. He invited the wider community to use it, share feedback, and help improve it, stressing the importance of keeping AI use ethical and grounded in good practice.

In the discussion afterward, Jonas was asked about the “meta-skills” mentioned in the playbook and whether they can be developed through short-term training or require hiring already highly skilled people. He replied that it is unrealistic to expect everyone to track the latest developments constantly. The important thing is not always knowing the newest technology, but developing deeper and more durable skills over time. These include, for example, data literacy and the ability to assess information critically. He argued that such competencies can be learned by everyone over time; they are not only for the most technically savvy employees.

When the questioner pressed the issue of time versus skilled personnel, pointing out that people need extra time to upskill, Jonas agreed that time is scarce. He suggested that if organizations achieve efficiency gains through AI, they should not use those gains only to do more of the same work. Instead, part of that saved time should be invested in upskilling, reflection, and learning.

Pavlina Nikolova from iBinder presented a practical perspective on metadata, classification, and data organization as foundations for AI in construction. She introduced iBinder as a content management platform focused on data governance, secure collaboration, and AI optimization. Her presentation focused on a core challenge in AEC: project data is often treated as a one-off output rather than as part of a learning system. It exists during the project life cycle and then dies in the archive, making it difficult for companies to learn from past projects and preserve tacit knowledge.

She began with a report her team had produced several years earlier to understand how customers’ data needs were changing over time. They found a steady increase, with storage needs growing eightfold over five years. COVID was one factor, but she identified broader drivers as well: increasingly data-intensive processes, the use of larger files such as reality capture, federated BIM models, and digital twins, and the growing shift from local file servers to cloud collaboration. Companies now want access to their project data from everywhere, including construction sites, and they want to collaborate live in shared environments.

On top of this, compliance and retention requirements make the situation more complex. She pointed to the UK Building Safety Act, which requires certain building data to be retained for 30 years and to remain easily accessible. That means firms are not only generating more data, but also carrying greater long-term responsibility for it.

Every project, she said, generates enormous amounts of information. Documents capture critical decisions, lessons learned, and technical expertise, and collectively they represent a company’s accumulated experience. But most firms struggle to reuse that knowledge, largely because it is fragmented.

She showed the typical AEC technology stack as a set of silos around a central idea of knowledge. Companies may use a CRM for proposals and client relationships, estimating platforms for cost and takeoff, project management systems for drawings, references, submittals, and progress tracking, shared file systems for live working documents and multiple versions, and of course email with many attachments. Valuable information exists in all of these systems, but almost never in a form that makes it easy to reuse.

The cost of this fragmentation is significant. It is not just an IT problem; it affects margins. Teams spend considerable time searching for the latest version of documents, the right BIM files, or the right information. Rework caused by outdated files is another major problem. She cited an industry study suggesting that 5% of project cost goes to rework and mistakes, and that about 80% of those mistakes are due to misused or misplaced information or poor communication. On a €100 million project, she said, that would mean €5 million lost to mistakes rather than labor or materials. Beyond money, the sector also loses knowledge, because lessons learned from one project rarely transfer effectively to the next team.

She said the AEC sector is now trying to solve this in two main ways: through data consolidation and through better organization of the data already held. In practice, data consolidation often means integrations and interoperable solutions. When choosing a central platform, companies should consider their existing technology stack and how data will flow between systems.

Referring to an earlier point from the conference, she noted that today people talk about system integrations, but in the near future these functions may increasingly be handled by agents. Instead of manually moving information between tools, one could imagine giving an agent access to multiple systems and asking it to retrieve, summarize, and reuse information. For the moment, however, integrations remain the practical discussion.

She then described several ways to start organizing construction data. Because most construction documents are unstructured PDFs, drawings, and email attachments, one of the most effective approaches is to add metadata, use classification, and build proper permissioning models.

Metadata, she said, is one of the most powerful tools for organizing construction knowledge. Instead of relying on file names or folder structures, metadata allows systems to surface information based on what the project data actually is and what characteristics it has. Whether the file is a PDF, DWG, or Revit model becomes less important than the content and context associated with it. Teams can search by project characteristics instead of guessing naming conventions.

Classification adds structure at the document level. Some intelligent platforms can now automatically identify whether an uploaded file is a drawing, an invoice, a financial document, a contract, or something else. She stressed that organizations should not expect people to manually classify thousands of files one by one. Modern platforms can automate much of the metadata and classification work. Once that structure exists, AI can search and identify relevant information much more effectively.

She described this as the point at which project folders begin to become knowledge libraries.

Permissioning and security are also crucial and often overlooked. Moving data from one system to another is not straightforward, and firms must always consider how information is protected. Construction projects involve many stakeholders and involve constant internal and external sharing. Companies therefore need clear control over who has access to what data, at which point, which version is current, and who changed what. Sensitive and contractual data, especially financial information, must remain secure and controlled.

She then described a construction knowledge lifecycle in which each stage contributes to an organized knowledge base. However, she also raised the issue of ROT data: redundant, outdated, and trivial information. Companies must decide what to do with duplicate files, outdated records, and documents no one has touched in ten years. Some software tools can help by identifying outdated or duplicated data before AI is applied on top of it. That cleanup is important because AI will become confused if the underlying data is poor. The challenge is that this is not a one-time exercise; it must be monitored continuously.

When structured project data is captured and organized, she said, it creates a cycle in which teams work faster, make better decisions, and continuously grow institutional knowledge.

AI only works well on structured and organized data. Once metadata and classification are in place, AI can provide meaningful support: summarizing contracts, helping during tendering, and enabling many of the use cases discussed throughout the conference. She said one common pre-construction use case is the creation of a “golden copy” library of successful proposals. If past project data is centralized and organized, a company receiving a new request for proposal can ask for similar projects done in the past and retrieve examples of successful bids. This can directly help firms win more work.

She emphasized an important principle: it is better to bring AI to the data than to take the data out to use AI. If companies export data into different AI tools for each task, they recreate the same fragmentation problem they are trying to solve. Ideally, AI should be applied within the organization’s own data environment. That improves security, helps the system learn from past projects over time, and avoids what she called shadow IT, where employees go outside official systems to use AI on their own. If managers do not provide secure AI tools internally, staff will often find their own unofficial ones.

This is one reason, she said, that many companies are developing in-house AI solutions: they do not want to share data externally, or they need tools tailored to a specific use case.

She concluded with three principles for implementing AI. It should be practical, meaning people should not have to completely change their workflows. It should be purposeful, meaning companies should begin with real use cases and with organized data rather than adopting AI for its own sake. And it should be private, meaning data should remain inside the organization’s controlled environment rather than being moved around unnecessarily.

In the brief discussion afterward, she was asked where the AI runs in such a model, especially in relation to European versus American servers. She replied that there are two options: either choose a platform where all data is stored and where AI runs on top of that data within the platform, or build an in-house solution if the goal is to keep the data entirely internal.

Dylan Richman: Hello everyone. My name is Dylan Richman. I'm a research engineer at Bentley Systems. Today I'm presenting predictive design as an alternative to generative design, a term many of you are familiar with. To demonstrate predictive design, we're specifically looking at conceptual site design and building placement.

Before we get into that, let's talk a bit about generative models. Generative AI has been around for decades, but the recent explosion of large language models from companies like OpenAI and Anthropic has dramatically shifted the focus of AI researchers toward these generative approaches. And it's not just text. We also have image generation models that can create photorealistic images from simple text prompts.

This momentum has naturally led people to ask what we can do in our own domains with generative models. Researchers have started to answer this question, and we've seen generative models applied to floor plan layout, topology optimization, and site design, just to name a few.

Many of these models have genuinely impressive results, but once we start to think about how they actually fit into design workflows, some fundamental issues surface.

The first is a role shift. In a generative workflow, the designer goes from leading the design process to becoming an evaluator. It's a bit like pulling a slot machine: you give the model your context, pull the lever, and hope the output is usable. If it isn't, you pull again.

Second is constraint satisfaction. Real design problems have many complex and often overlapping constraints, and it is very difficult to encode these constraints and feed them into generative models.

Lastly is search-space collapse. Design problems have rich, diverse search spaces, and when we use generative models, we collapse those search spaces into individual designs. So instead of increasing what the designer can consider, we are actually narrowing it.

We started to ask: how can we give the designer the benefits of AI while still allowing them to remain in the front seat of the design process? That's where predictive design comes in.

We started to ask: how can we give the designer the benefits of AI while still allowing them to remain in the front seat of the design process? That's where predictive design comes in.

Instead of asking the model to hand us an answer, we ask it to show us what the landscape of good answers looks like. Say we have a building footprint that we're trying to place within a parcel boundary, and we have an access point at the top. In a generative approach, the model would place the building for you and maybe give you multiple possible locations, leaving you to choose the one you think is best.

In predictive design, we instead give the engineer a feasibility map. This is the output from a model that shows where the most likely spots are for placing a building, based on designs it has seen in the past.

To build a model that can learn something like this, we obviously need a lot of data. Specifically, we need a large dataset of real-world sites. So we first had to define what a site actually is. In our case, we're looking at commercial or industrial parcels that have at least one building placed on them and may have road access points.

We sourced parcel data from 31 counties across 10 U.S. states and got road and building information from OpenStreetMap. The total dataset was 21.7 million parcels, but after filtering for usable sites, we were left with about 575,000.

How do we go from raw geospatial data to something we can actually feed into a machine learning model? We first identify relevant commercial and industrial parcels, extract the parcel boundary, get the building footprint and access points from OpenStreetMap, and then apply a normalization process that allows the data to be fed into a machine learning model.

For the model itself, we chose Perceiver IO, an architecture introduced by Google DeepMind in 2021. It's a transformer-based architecture designed to work with varying modalities, making it well suited to this type of problem.

We take the raw inputs and feed them into an encoder, which turns them into tokens that the model can read. The model processes these tokens and outputs the feasibility map.

We take the raw inputs and feed them into an encoder, which turns them into tokens that the model can read. The model processes these tokens and outputs the feasibility map.

Similar to how LLMs tokenize words, we needed a way to tokenize geometric inputs. An interesting problem we encountered was how to do this. Points are easy: they have an X and Y coordinate, so they map naturally to a fixed-length token. Polygons are more difficult because they can have any number of sides.

Our solution was to decompose each polygon into its individual line segments. Since each segment consists of two points, they naturally map onto fixed-length tokens just like point geometries.

To put our results into perspective, we compared against a few baseline models. The first is a centroid model, which simply predicts placing the building at the center of the parcel. The second is what we call a uniform feasible model. This takes all locations where the building fits within the parcel and assigns them a uniform value. In one example, the ground-truth building was actually slightly outside this region because the building in the data was slightly outside the parcel geometry. The last baseline is a U-Net model, which is a common image-to-image machine learning model.

We evaluated these models on a few metrics.

The first metric is top constraint. This takes the highest-probability location and checks whether the building is contained within the parcel. Naturally, we want this value to be higher. The uniform feasible model gets this trivially because it only considers valid locations. The centroid model also performs well, which makes sense: if you place a building at the center of a parcel, the odds that it is completely contained are fairly high.

Our model lags behind on this metric, but that doesn't tell the whole story. The next metric is ground-truth pixel rank. This measures the ground-truth location and checks how highly the model ranks it. It's effectively a test of predictive accuracy. On this metric, our model easily beats the others.

The last metric is specificity. If the model predicts that the building could go everywhere, that isn't very informative. We want the model to identify more specific locations. In this case, a lower value corresponds to higher specificity. Again, our model leads on this metric.

The last metric is specificity. If the model predicts that the building could go everywhere, that isn't very informative. We want the model to identify more specific locations. In this case, a lower value corresponds to higher specificity. Again, our model leads on this metric.

Looking at the outputs side by side, we can see the difference. On one site, the centroid model picks the center, the uniform feasible model predicts a broad box of feasible locations, and the U-Net doesn't do a very good job. Our model is the best of the group: it accurately identifies where the building was located and produces a highly specific output.

Another site is interesting because of the building orientation. The building can only be placed in a very thin central portion of the site, which is easiest to see by looking at the uniform feasible output. Again, the U-Net does not perform well. Our model, while preferring the opposite side of the parcel from the actual placement, still does a good job identifying all of the valid locations and ranking them highly.

We saw similar behavior on another site. In that one, there were four access points on the right side, which in our dataset was indicative of a parking lot. The model was able to infer from that that the building should go on the opposite side of the parking lot.

A final site had the most degrees of freedom for building placement. Again, the U-Net did not do a great job, while our model identified a center column as the most likely place to position the building and captured the ground-truth location. It also had a notch on the right-hand side, which was effectively saying that if we're going to put the building off-center, it should probably still be vertically centered.

Another useful way to understand the model is to watch how the heat map changes as we rotate the building. In one example, there is an access point on the bottom right and the model initially predicts placing the building in the top center. As we rotate the building, the heat map evolves. Once the building becomes vertical, the model figures out that it should be placed on the left side so it doesn't block the access point, because we're probably going to put a parking lot on the access-point side.

We can see this in another site as well. There is a notch in the top-left of the prediction, something the model learned on its own. Essentially, you generally do not put a building directly in front of an access point because you don't want to block the road that will come from there. As we rotate the building, the heat map evolves while still respecting that rule.

Stepping back from the demos, the model works fairly well. It produces smooth, interpretable feasibility maps and outperforms the baselines we tested it against. The current inputs are intentionally minimal: we only consider parcel geometry and building footprint geometry. We do plan to incorporate more information, such as topography and setback information, to make the outputs more informed.

But that's also a key driver of predictive design: we don't need the model to learn every rule the way we might in a generative approach, because we're working alongside another model that already knows a great deal—the engineer or the designer.

To wrap up, we started by looking at what generative models have learned from text and images. It turns out we can also learn from sites. In this case, we trained a model not in a generative way, but in a predictive way, to help keep the designer in the driver's seat by giving them more information they can use.

Generative models have a lot of value in many cases, but the key idea of this talk is that we shouldn't only be looking at generative approaches. We should expand the ways we use AI to help designers and engineers.

Thank you.

Audience question: You're learning the probabilistic distribution of one object in one parcel. I work in interior space, and I can see the potential for multi-object layouts within one space. How could this probability map be visualized for multiple objects?

Dylan Richman: That's one challenge with this type of method. You would probably have to visualize it in a sequential manner, which is similar to how engineers do the designs themselves—placing one object at a time. In that instance, you would want the other objects to be fed as input into the model, so as you place each object, it can update its prediction based on the previous placements.

Audience question: Have you tried to incorporate zoning requirements or geotechnical constraints into the model?

Audience question: Have you tried to incorporate zoning requirements or geotechnical constraints into the model?

Dylan Richman: Not yet, but that would be an obvious next step, along with topography. That's one reason we intentionally chose the Perceiver IO architecture: it allows us to input basically any modality we want. Zoning requirements, for example, could come in as zoning polygons. As you add more data like that, you need more data in your training set, but the nice thing is that we have millions of sites around the world, and with satellite data we can expand the dataset to match that input complexity.

Cia Sukkaran: Hi, I'm Cia. As mentioned, I am a doctoral researcher at the University of West London, and I'm also a partner and principal at Studio Emergence. I've been an architect for more than a decade, and for the last ten years I've been very industry focused. More recently I also started my research journey.

This presentation is a mix of industry and research. It talks about my experiences as an architect, what I have learned over the last decade, and my computational design expertise. I started working in computational design in 2014 during my master's, and ever since then, both in Studio Emergence and in research, we've worked extensively with evolutionary algorithms, machine learning, and more recently large language models. It's been quite a journey going from a very design-centric architectural background to something more computationally driven.

The presentation has two parts. In the first, I'll talk about applications that architects and designers already use in everyday practice and how they are reshaping design agency overall. In the second, I'll discuss applications we've developed within the research unit and at Studio Emergence.

For the first part, we wanted to understand what AI applications are already commercialized and being used in the industry by architects and designers across the project life cycle. Once we had a consolidated list of 50 applications, we ran analyses to understand where those applications are situated in the overall workflow.

The 50 selected applications were categorized according to project life-cycle stages. Initially we reviewed around 400 tools, narrowed that to 100, and eventually selected the 50 most used. Even at a glance, it was clear that the distribution is lopsided across stages. We used the RIBA Plan of Work, since that was the landscape we were working in. Stage 1 is briefing, followed by concept design, spatial coordination, technical design, and then actual construction at stage 5.

We found that stages 2 and 5 have many applications, while the others are comparatively underserved.

When we started evaluating these tools, we needed a way to compare them despite their differences. As an architect myself, I also wanted to understand what they were doing to architects and to design agency as a whole.

When we started evaluating these tools, we needed a way to compare them despite their differences. As an architect myself, I also wanted to understand what they were doing to architects and to design agency as a whole.

We came up with two aspects for evaluation. The first is AI logic, or AL, which captures the underlying artificial intelligence logic of the application. It asks: how intelligent and self-directed is the tool? Does it optimize complex problems? Does it learn from them? Or is it basically a standard application that simply calls itself AI?

The second is architectural values, or AV. This was especially important to me personally. It captures whether the tool preserves architects' control and creativity. Does it allow meaningful human interaction? How black-boxed is the application? Does it simply produce options over which the architect has little control, or can the architect actively guide and shape the output?

We see design agency as the intersection of these two dimensions. Design agency is evolving with the advent of AI. The architect or designer using AI is situated at that intersection between AI logic and architectural values, and we were trying to understand how design agency shifts as these applications are used.

We ran a pilot study in which 10 experts rated a subset of the applications. We couldn't ask them to rate all 50 because that would have been too arduous, so each expert rated 5 AI applications across a number of values: three AI-logic values and three AV values. We also asked them to rate each application within our scoring framework.

At the low end of AI logic, the tool is merely assistive. At the low end of AV, the outputs are narrowly technical and not focused on the designer. At the high end, the tool automates complex problems while explicitly supporting design agency.

There was substantial variation in how individual experts ranked specific tools, but when we analyzed the averages and the ICC coefficient, the results were surprisingly consistent. Based on this, we assigned scores to the 50 applications.

There was substantial variation in how individual experts ranked specific tools, but when we analyzed the averages and the ICC coefficient, the results were surprisingly consistent. Based on this, we assigned scores to the 50 applications.

Once we mapped the applications, interesting patterns emerged. On a chart with AI logic on the x-axis and architectural values on the y-axis, with colors indicating project life-cycle stages, we could see that many stage 5 construction tools clustered in the lower-right quadrant: high AI logic but somewhat lower architectural values. Those were the applications serving manufacturing and construction.

By contrast, the upper-left quadrant contained more stage 1 and conceptual design tools: higher architectural values and lower AI logic.

This mapping helped us understand how different stages of the project life cycle are being served by different tools and how these tools are often operating in parallel rather than in an integrated way.

One conclusion was that design agency is not necessarily being replaced so much as redistributed. At stage 1, AI logic and architectural values are often more balanced. As we move toward stage 5, architectural values tend to decline while AI logic increases.

We also looked at secondary parameters, such as user type. Architects are generally situated in stages 1 and 2, while contractors are more often positioned in stage 5. There is not much overlap between those two groups.

We also examined geographic context. Many tools claim to be global, but when you look more closely, they are largely focused either on Europe or North America. A lot of global variation is ignored, especially in areas like building regulations.

Tool development follows similar patterns in terms of what is commercially available and what types of design logic are being used.

One major finding concerned building typology. Many of these applications are geared toward residential, mixed-use, and commercial work. Much less is aimed at institutional architecture. From my own architectural experience, I understand why: institutions involve huge hierarchical complexities and adjacency issues, especially in more complex spaces.

To summarize the findings: design agency is being redistributed; the current technological landscape is biased, with a gap in applications that score highly on both AI logic and architectural values; institutions are underserved; and sustainability tools are also underserved. There are a few robust tools, but not enough of them.

To summarize the findings: design agency is being redistributed; the current technological landscape is biased, with a gap in applications that score highly on both AI logic and architectural values; institutions are underserved; and sustainability tools are also underserved. There are a few robust tools, but not enough of them.

From that, we asked what gap we could address in the research unit. One obvious direction was institutional architecture, specifically school building design. There are not many applications that do this. Another reason to focus on schools is that in the UK there is already a lot of information available. The Department for Education provides schedules of accommodation, so if you know the number of pupils in a school, you can derive area requirements relatively easily. That makes the program easier to define.

There are also exemplary design options already published, which we could use as a dataset to compare our outputs. In institutional architecture, data availability is often a challenge, especially if you want to compare circulation patterns and internal organization.

We started with something simple: the adjacency matrix. We wanted to understand what the adjacency matrix for a complicated school program looks like. Even for one of the smaller schools, the matrix was substantial. On larger schools, the matrix had more than 100 rows, even over 150. It quickly became a conundrum to understand how to computationally solve all of those adjacencies, because schools have many rooms, strict spatial hierarchies, and many rule-based relationships.

That was our major challenge. One way to address it was to clusterize the program. We grouped spaces into clusters and treated those clusters, or building arms, as the units that come together. Then the computational rules and evolutionary algorithms work on those clusters rather than on every single room individually.

This approach worked quite successfully. For a primary school of about 100 pupils, we could reduce the problem to around 20 clustered spaces, and then later subdivide those internally.

For the prototype development framework, we created two phases. Phase 1 focused on capturing information: extracting numerical data from the schedule of accommodation; coding planning rules, regulations, and building regulations for the specific site context; and using the site boundary as an input to define the design envelope.

For the prototype development framework, we created two phases. Phase 1 focused on capturing information: extracting numerical data from the schedule of accommodation; coding planning rules, regulations, and building regulations for the specific site context; and using the site boundary as an input to define the design envelope.

Phase 2 used evolutionary algorithms. We selected evolutionary algorithms specifically because they provide Pareto-optimal solutions. When we tried other methods, the problem often became too complex, especially given the number and volume of spaces involved.

A key concern for me throughout this process was the role of the designer. As a computational architect using many existing applications, one of the biggest problems I encountered was not having enough authority in selecting what I was making. Many applications simply produce outputs that I cannot meaningfully pick and choose from.

So throughout our workflow, we inserted opportunities for user input. At different steps, the user can select certain options because, as designers, we all approach projects with intentions and preferences.

For example, the user can select plan-exploration typologies. They can also set input parameters such as site dimensions, road orientation, overall site orientation, and setbacks. These can also support later energy analysis and simulations. We also input building regulation requirements like height limits and permissible FAR.

Once all that information is assembled, we can run case studies. In the example presented, we had a small site, regulatory setbacks, and a school program derived from the schedule of accommodation.

The system generated multiple layout options based on rule-based optimization. We had rules that rank the plans generated in different ways, and then based on the ranking, the user can select what works and what doesn't.

One example was an optimized C-shaped plan for the school. We also developed an L-shaped planning option for the same site, with the same setbacks and program, again generating multiple plan iterations and optimized layouts.

We then compared the resulting school layouts against Department for Education layouts published by the government. The results were reasonably successful. The roadside entry, shown in blue, corresponded well with expected organization. Administrative spaces were around it, then halls and play areas near the entry, followed by learning spaces located further away.

We have also been working in a BIM environment so that the whole process can take place within software like Revit. Within that environment, users can generate walls, select different wall typologies, choose window types, and tailor construction assemblies according to local context. Since I've worked in different geographic regions, I know that wall typologies and other architectural details vary significantly from place to place. Architecture is extremely tangible and highly location-specific, so we kept the system open-ended enough for users to choose what they need.

Thank you.

Audience question: In school design, the designer often already knows where certain major elements should go, such as the gymnasium. Can the designer intervene and say that something has to be in a certain place, and then let the system arrange everything else around it?

Cia Sukkaran: Yes, we've kept it quite open-ended. Obstacles can be defined, and you can reserve part of the layout for a specific use. That area remains fixed based on the designer's decision, and everything else can be generated around it. So there is definitely a possibility to reserve a space for a particular thing and then generate the outputs around it.

Audience question: Our school designs usually involve many different angles and shapes. Can the system accommodate more architectural variation, or is it mainly about spatial connections at this point?

Cia Sukkaran: At this point it's more about spatial connections, but we're developing it further. Different site shapes are already incorporated. The next step is to allow more varied spatial organizations as well. For example, a user could sketch a shape and then the entire building could be organized around that shape. The whole system is already sketch-based, so the line typologies we used, such as linear and C-shaped, are built from that logic. In principle, guidance geometry from a user sketch can be used in future development.

Audience question: In Finland we have different rules for different cities. Is there similar variation in England?

Cia Sukkaran: Yes, absolutely, and that's part of the reason institutions are harder to address. Residential projects are still more consistent, but institutional rules vary a lot. What we've done is keep the first part of the workflow quite open-ended, so the user can input a lot of the requirements. Then the outputs can be brought closer to the building regulations of the specific region.

Audience question: If the system generates hundreds of layouts in a minute, is there a risk of information overload? And how do you optimize selection while preserving human creativity?

Cia Sukkaran: One good thing is that the outputs are rank-based. If you don't want overload, you can just look at the top-ranked options. If you don't like what you're seeing, you can shift to others. So the user doesn't need to review everything.

As for human creativity, it is still very much there because this is targeted at conceptual design. In practice, due to feasibility issues, budgets, and time constraints, iterative design and optioneering are often not done to the extent the architect would want. This becomes part of the toolkit: it presents options, but it doesn't force a selection. It gives you a wide range of optioneering that you can then apply your own creativity to and develop further. It is not the final design. It provides the breadth of exploration that is needed in conceptual design.

Cia Sukkaran: We already do bubble diagrams and conceptual zoning to understand volumetric relationships. This tool helps with that initial stage of case studies and site understanding. Work that might otherwise take a week can be done in a day, perhaps even faster, and that can significantly reduce the time needed for early presentations. It could also work for other institutional building types, such as hospitals.

Mauri Laxanen: Good morning, everybody. My name is Mauri Laxanen, and I work at Tampere University of Applied Sciences. My topic is the visualization of buildings in early stages.

The problem is familiar. In early-stage design, the building company gives some basic requirements and steering information. Then the architect goes back to the office, makes a fairly detailed model of the building, creates a visualization, and returns to present it. Then they discuss it, there are changes, the architect goes back to the office, makes a new model, and comes back after two weeks.

But when there is not yet a buyer or a committed customer, there are not many resources available, and this cycle cannot go through very many rounds.

So our question was: can we make those visuals as AI-generated images? Everybody knows you can make almost any kind of picture with AI, but the real question was whether those pictures are usable.

Our method was to select tools—ArchiAI and Stable Diffusion—and use versions that had been approved for working with real company data that could not be shared publicly. We held workshops with housing development professionals, our team, an architect, and researchers. Then we collected the pictures, evaluated them, and followed what kinds of discussions they generated.

The first key finding was that it is still very useful to make some kind of BIM model at the beginning, but it does not need to be detailed. A simple mass model is enough.

You get information from the building companies about how many blocks there may be and the roof program, what kind of houses there will be, and sometimes the authorities also specify how many buildings there are allowed to be. So you make a mass model.

Then you take a view from where you want the picture and create a screenshot from the model, and you keep this picture as a steering file for the AI. Then you get an image that reflects the actual project geometry and vertical organization. That was the first lesson.

The second lesson was that there are always mistakes. The images are not perfect, but we found that if we say clearly, “These pictures are made by AI, not by me,” then people excuse a lot. They understand the limitations and still accept the images as useful.

The second lesson was that there are always mistakes. The images are not perfect, but we found that if we say clearly, “These pictures are made by AI, not by me,” then people excuse a lot. They understand the limitations and still accept the images as useful.

What happened in practice was that people started to discuss. That was the main finding. The picture does not need to be perfect to trigger meaningful and useful discussion.

Our conclusion was that we found a method to generate usable images for architects in early design stages. Are architects losing their jobs because of this? No. They are not, because there are mistakes in the pictures and we still need highly professional architects to say what is good, what cannot be done, and what needs to be changed.

But what can be reduced is routine modeling work. That was our target. We get less modeling and more design.

Let me show an example that led us to these conclusions. We started with a very simple model. On the left was the Archicad model. We loaded in the terrain and roads as they exist, and then placed a very simple mass model. We selected a view and got an image from it. It included the right terrain, the correct location, and even the right kind of forest in the background.

It was not a glorious picture. It was not something we would have presented as a polished final rendering, but it served as a visual starting point.

At the time we began, we did not have much skill with the process. We were starting from zero and wanted to save work, so we simply took a usable picture and saw what would happen.

We also had planning regulations from the authorities, and we gave that information to the system in text form. What was surprising was that almost every detail from the regulations could somehow be found in the generated picture.

One example involved a large bicycle shelter with a green roof. That requirement was in the text input. Everyone in the room initially accepted it in the abstract. We had removed original nature from the site, so compensating with greenery seemed normal and acceptable.

One example involved a large bicycle shelter with a green roof. That requirement was in the text input. Everyone in the room initially accepted it in the abstract. We had removed original nature from the site, so compensating with greenery seemed normal and acceptable.

But as soon as we had the picture in front of us, people reacted differently. They said: not there, not that one. Suddenly people started to notice the actual implications. The building company began to think about the cross-section and realized there would be water lying on the roof. That would mean freezing and melting several times a year. The roof would need to last 50 years without rusting. They started asking how much natural material would be needed to build such a roof in the middle of the forest.

Then they realized that this kind of rule made more sense in dense city centers and not in that location. Everyone understood it immediately once it was visualized. They said the rule made no sense there.

The discussion continued. Someone said perhaps they should instead build the cheapest steel-profile roof, which would be acceptable but would not look very good. Then someone suggested that, because this is in the countryside, they could create a shelter more like those used for animals, with a more appropriate roof. And the design discussion continued from there.

They also began to discuss things like whether the red brick looked right and whether it was used enough. The image activated discussion that would not have happened in the same way from text alone.

At the same time, one person in the room barely said a word. He was working on his computer the whole time, listening to the discussion. After 10 or 15 minutes, he looked up and started guiding the architect. He made some adjustments, gestured to the screen, and then said: now that we have discussed this, can we go back to the roof? We have a new plan.

This person had the best tools and knew the workflow best. He was able to make a new picture during the meeting itself. They had a new version of the design in the same meeting—not after two weeks, not after three weeks, but in the same meeting—and they could continue the discussion immediately.

That is the real change.

That is the real change.

You can make pictures without reference images, but then there are difficulties. For example, it is very hard for AI to understand how many stories a building has. In one case, a building that should have had more floors came out as only seven stories. A mass model helps. It does not solve the problem entirely, but it helps a lot.

Nowadays you can also use not just the screenshot but the three-dimensional DXF file as steering data, which improves the control further.

There are still other mistakes. In one example, there was a tram shown in the image. It looked like the tram we have in Tampere, and even the rails were present, but the tram was not in the middle where it should have been. That kind of error did not disturb me very much because I was concentrating on whether the building composition was correct, but we do not know how customers might react. They may think that if the designers do not even know how the trams run, can they really design good buildings?

Still, this is not a major problem. It can be corrected relatively easily.

Once you have this frame—a mass model and some initial control—then comes the next advantage of AI: you can go out of the box. You can generate new ideas and different kinds of things, while still keeping the overall project within a reasonable frame.

That is valuable, because when you are looking for ideas, the aim is often to go beyond the obvious.
