Speaker: Professor Sigrid

Architects and artists have always driven technology. It is probably also a mindset of creativity. I would like to begin with a bit of history—my personal history—before the actual talk, because it is important to understand where I am coming from.

In the 1990s and early 2000s, I was working for interesting architects on internationally known and complex architecture projects. I saw many CAD environments where we believed technologies were arriving that would last. In one example, we used a FARO arm to digitize normal physical models into Rhino 1.0. That was really the start of Rhino. Fourteen years later, that project was actually built.

Then, in the year 2000, there was another project with Colin Fournier and Peter Cook for the first Peter Cook building in Graz. The problem there was also how to digitize a physical scale model into the CAD environment.

Around 2010, our field split in two directions. One part jumped on BIM, and I jumped on industrial robotics. In 2008, as everybody remembers, there was the car crisis, and suddenly you could get used robots off the shelf for the kind of price that, ten years earlier, we had paid for desktop computers. Sometimes, when new technology becomes available, you can suddenly do new things.

In the Association for Robots in Architecture, we combined industrial robotics with algorithmic modeling, especially the work David Rutten developed in Grasshopper. What was interesting for us was that we were total novices. We had no idea about robotics, but we could simulate and visualize, and we got what we saw. That is a little different from what happens now in AI, where very often things happen in the background and you do not really see what you get.

In 2012, we started using genetic algorithms to ask our system where best to place the workpiece in front of the robot so the robot could operate as efficiently as possible.

In 2012, we started using genetic algorithms to ask our system where best to place the workpiece in front of the robot so the robot could operate as efficiently as possible.

Another important topic, which we also heard about at this conference, is community building. That happened almost naturally. KUKA|prc spread around the world. Nowadays we have around 1 to 1.5 million downloads per year, just from online users. We also realized who those users were. They were coming out of architecture school, and instead of buying a car for the garage, they bought a robot and started prototyping. A lot of startup scenes began this way—for instance Branch Technology, and also what happened in Stuttgart. The construction industry created new business models simply because it had new tools.

Because ours was an online platform, we could also understand who the users were. This is good news for today: do not underestimate the digital youth. Let them do their work. You do not have to control them. We saw that people in my age group, in the mid-50s, represented only about 0.7% of those interested in robotics. I am still interested in robotics, of course, but the point is that CEOs—the decision-makers—are often the ones who are afraid of new technologies, not the people working for them. You have to create a working environment where the digital youth can actually do their job.

Now I move to 2020 and closer to the actual topic. At RWTH Aachen, we looked at the comparison between industrial production and construction sites. I am always interested in things where I do not know the answer, and construction sites are full of them: unstructured, dynamic, shared workspaces; a low degree of automation; and a major digital gap. If we really want to create impact, we need to create it on the construction site. We do not create it anymore mainly in planning—maybe only in little steps there—but the big steps are on site.

You can also see this in the comparison between highly optimized industrial production and normal construction-site productivity. Mass optimization is about 50 times faster than a normal construction site. So if we increase productivity from 1 square meter per man-hour to 2 square meters per hour, that is a 100% increase.

That is where robotics will probably be adopted.

That is where robotics will probably be adopted.

Another domain I am very interested in is demolition, or more precisely controlled deconstruction. Similar to medicine, robots enter markets where humans are less precise, or where the risk of a human dying is higher than the risk of the machine doing something wrong. That is why, from my point of view, we will see construction robots in deconstruction fairly soon.

When I started this line of work, I looked across the Dutch border to Kerkrade, where there was a very nice project called Superlocal. The idea was material harvesting. They had three high-rise precast buildings: one was demolished, one was deconstructed, and one was refurbished. For me, it became quite clear that we need assistance to be able to deconstruct in a better way.

One of the key issues is climate impact. Construction is one of the most critical economic sectors for current climate change, accounting for over 39% of global CO2 emissions. Current construction contains over 28 billion tons of concrete, which is 42% of the global building-material supply. Cement, which is the basic material of concrete, makes up 8% of global CO2 emissions. Those 28 billion tons of concrete represent a significant source of secondary building parts for reuse.

At the Chair of Individualized Production at RWTH Aachen University, an interdisciplinary team is developing new methods for material harvesting by increasing the levels of automation in deconstruction. The Roboton research project bridges this technological gap by transforming an existing manual demolition machine into an automated deconstruction robot.

The hardware of the existing Brokk deconstruction machine is extended by attaching motion sensors for each axis. The deconstruction robot is supported by a second mobile robot for environment detection and component recognition. A digital twin is connected to the robot control through a newly developed user interface. The Roboton project researches precise movements of the automated rock joints and Hilti saw. The movements are virtually preplanned and then adapted to construction-site requirements.

Concrete becomes a new source of secondary building material. The aim is to increase the lifetime of existing concrete construction. The Roboton research project enables automated deconstruction and the creation of new building processes for modular reuse of harvested concrete building parts. The future goals are to increase controlled deconstruction by 40%, increase worker safety through automation, reuse 70% of existing concrete building construction for the extraction of secondary building parts, and reduce global cement-based CO2 emissions by 1%.

The basic idea was simple: we took an existing demolition machine, added a Hilti saw, and created a new robot by integrating sensors and a new control system. This is where AI starts getting interesting, because it was not so easy to reverse-engineer the inverse kinematics as we do with industrial robots, simply because hydraulic systems behave differently according to temperature and other factors.

What was interesting for us was the system evaluation. We compared manual setup time with robot setup time and were able to decrease setup and disassembly time by 83%, while achieving an accuracy of 1.5 millimeters, which is usually not possible with a hydraulic machine. That is where things get interesting.

What we are working on now is this: once you have a digital machine, you can integrate AI and automatic sawing processes. You can create analysis of cut and joint dimensions. You can optimize the sawing process, for instance to produce precise dry joints. You can inspect hazardous contamination inline. You can automate assessment of the processed water, which is also very important, because it is not enough simply to extract material from concrete. The concrete can be completely different one meter or ten meters away from where you actually tested.

Another important AI integration is site supervision—the positioning of the machine relative to the component, and the machine’s spatial and situational awareness.

We also work a lot in refurbishment. One thing we really want to get rid of is scaffolding, because it is one of the highest risks on construction sites. This year, one of our systems won the Bauma Innovation Award. Bauma is one of the biggest fairs for construction engineering and construction machines.

The project is called MAX—the Mobile Assembly X System. It is a first automated plug-and-produce solution intended to revolutionize scaffold-free facade refurbishment. Based on an initial patent, an industrial robot works seamlessly with a crane. It was first tested in a controlled environment. The robot platform consists of a multi-sensor KUKA robot for automated assembly of facade panels. Vacuum grippers and an adaptive guiding system allow controlled lifting of the robot to ensure accurate positioning.

MAX was further developed under real conditions and integrated with a mobile Jekko crane. One key innovation is the automated learning of MAX to continue an assembly strategy manually performed by a craftsman. MAX is therefore the first plug-and-produce system for the future craftsman, quality-controlled via a tablet, with no further programming or preplanning skills needed. A second key innovation is that the Jekko crane is controlled by a KUKA robot via 5G for safe operation. MAX addresses the skilled labor shortage with speed, safety, and ease of use, and it opens the European refurbishment market—worth €400 billion—to small and medium construction enterprises.

The idea here was that we did not want to depend on a BIM model, and we did not want extensive surveillance. We wanted the machine to understand the pre-work done by a worker on site and then continue the sequence the worker had established.

At the Center Construction Robotics, which was founded in 2015, we established the Reference Construction Site—the first European testbed of around 4,000 square meters. We have also built a little community, hopefully a good one, across Europe. There are still blank spots in Finland and Norway, so we are open to further collaboration, because what we need to do is combine the strengths of users and enablers.

Across our projects, one thing is completely clear—and we heard it throughout the conference—the construction site is a market of different vendors, all with information. What we need is for those vendors to be able to communicate. My vision is that people must understand one another regardless of what information they share. It will not be as people imagined in recent years or centuries, where there is a single source of truth and the whole value chain is forced onto one platform. We have to understand that vendors are individual and that we need this individuality in our systems. This is also where AI can help.

What matters is that everybody speaks the same language. We look at the European construction market with the vision that we standardize only how documentation and data should be described, while the project information itself and access to the data remain with you and your partners. Then everybody can have a place in this European construction market.

How do we do that? We can describe information with ontologies and linked data to enable this standardization and to integrate whatever information you have. One of my jobs is to write these standards, and that is what we are currently doing in Germany.

Another important point is that teams are not only robots. Teams are also humans. People do not use software or platforms in the same way. So in every value chain there is a different degree of digitalization, and the system should be flexible enough that it does not matter whether information is handed over by voice, by a drawing, or in some other way. What matters is that the information is handed over.

What I have realized over the years, since the beginning in 2000, is that only 10% of planning data really reaches the construction site. As an architect, I was frustrated in 2003 that only 1% of my information got to the construction site digitally. So there is a huge gap between BIM models and construction sites. We can keep trying to improve areas where we are already relatively good, but if we do not bridge this gap, then there is no way digitalization—and later circularity—will really work. Otherwise we just keep encapsulating and redoing the same information over and over again.

One idea we explored as early as 2022 was to combine everything on the construction site so we could make use of the information we actually produce and the machines we already have. One example of an AI-and-knowledge-graph pipeline is a question like: what is the skeletal construction process for a certain week of the year? The model retrieves the data from BIM models, shows how far the construction site has progressed, and gives you the information.

Another example involves something you cannot simply get out of models: if a robot wants to control a lift, what would that look like? If you have a shared environment with humans and machines, then you have to combine different capabilities. The idea is not that every simple machine on a construction site must be integrated with cameras and sensors. Instead, machines can share capabilities. One robot can provide the eyes and vision control, while another can simply do the work.

That information then has to be contextualized. Our idea is that we are not going to draw and model everything. Instead, we create simple control by generating text-based context in the background, placing that instance into the triple store, and assigning the situation to any machine working together.

Another example is that workers can control a machine directly. They can use a mobile phone and tell the lift to come down before material is transported. All this is orchestrated through our edge server on the construction site: you give the command, and the edge server sends it to the actual machine.

Logistics is also extremely important. If you look at the surveys, only 30% of work on construction sites is the actual act of construction. Around 30% is logistics, and 10% of the cost is just transport and searching for tools and materials. This is another place where we can intervene. If we optimize logistics, we increase efficiency by at least 10%.

That can also be one of these AI pipelines, where AI communicates with a local large language model, and in the knowledge graph you have BIM information, processes, timesheets—everything useful for the construction site. In one logistics example, a human asks for material. The human is located on the construction site, so the system knows exactly what wall or area the material belongs to. A logistics worker provides the material, and the process in between those two people should be autonomous.

Our vision is that all logistics should happen at night. When workers arrive on the construction site in the morning, the materials should already be where they need to be. The documentation should already be done by robots, uploaded to the BIM, and workers should know what was built the previous day. During the night, robots can work autonomously much more easily.

If you have this capability matching among machines on the construction site, you can ask for availability in the same way you ask for an Uber, and the closest robot comes to you. You can also assign tasks and processes to the machines. I heard in the previous talk the desire for machines to become teammates. That should be possible. You could tell a machine, “Bring me some tiles,” or “Bring me some facade panels,” but when it drives past the wall, it could also tell you how far the wall has progressed and whether the drywall is finished.

The logistics person can see in real time where the machines are and what they are doing. And if they pass by a place, they can receive another command: “Please have a look and check whether the drywall is OK.”

These are small examples from our own playground, but they can obviously be prepared at the next level. For instance, a robot can arrive at the mounting space and ask another robot to help transport panels into its lift, where our MAX system can then install the panels. These are all components that could work, and in many cases already do work, on our site.

There is one major obstacle: connectivity. Everyone knows the question, “Where is my router? Do I have a connection or not?” Signal loss and signal-strength estimation in nomadic networks are important. If anyone is thinking about launching a startup, I can tell you this is a new profession we need. We must be able to predict network performance over the project timeline and determine where and when to place routers so there is no shadowing and no robot loses connection.

This is a very new area. We just published, this month, the requirements for ICT infrastructure on construction sites. What we need is a zone of implicit trust, because not every actor in the value chain can simply arrive with digital devices, drones, and other equipment; usually there is a lot of interference, and then again we end up discussing who is responsible or liable for what.

So what do we need for an AI-enabled construction site? Briefly, we need a robust ICT infrastructure. We need sensors and machines—a cyber-physical system. And we need data that is structured with minimal information so that machines and humans can understand it.

I would also like to see that, in our planning processes, we model less and draw less, because the models should only be a representation or visualization of what we have actually organized.

I would like to invite you as well. The next huge project we started this year is a new excellence cluster, where we are diving deeply into new materials, new processes, AI fleets, and the interaction between human workers and machines. We are looking for construction companies, and we are looking for workers we can interview. We would like to do this Europe-wide. So if you are interested in having your workers collaborate with us, I would especially like to collaborate with Finnish companies.

Everything I showed from 2010 onward was made possible by my team. They are the great thinkers in the background. Thank you very much.

Q&A

Question: What do you think will be the mainstream application in the next five years that we will actually see on construction sites?

Professor Sigrid: I think logistics, and also everything related to deconstruction. There are many possibilities to improve there. I also think workers will appreciate having things prepared for them. They do not want to clean up, and they do not want to put away their tools. If machines can do that, I think workers will be happy.

Question: How much does this change with humanoid robots? A humanoid robot seems like forcing a construction site to behave like a manufacturing plant, but construction sites have stairs, hands, feet, and natural limitations compared with something like a tractor.

Professor Sigrid: To be honest, our strategy is more about the digitalization of construction machinery. The Brokk robot you saw has been on the market for 40 years, so the robustness of the machine is already proven. I think industrial robots and humanoid robots still need time to be fully proven, especially when it comes to the ratio between their weight and what they can lift. So currently I am focusing on construction machines.

There are many things I could still talk about. For example, we upgraded a crane to act as a logistics center. A crane is perfect not only for transporting material but also for documentation. You can use machine data and AI to understand quite well what it is carrying based on the data curves alone. It can distinguish whether it is carrying iron bars or something else, so there is a lot AI can help with even without vision control.

Question: I am an architect, so I was curious when you said at the end that you wanted less data from us in our models. We always think we should put so much data into them. Could you elaborate?

Professor Sigrid: We explored this in a European project called TARGET-X together with architects. We all sat around the same table and asked what information is actually important for architecture. From my point of view, architects are there for surfaces, room structure, and those kinds of things. They are not there to tell everybody exactly what is inside the wall or the structure. That has always been a discussion between architects and civil engineers: who is responsible for structure and detailing, and so on.

If the whole value chain sat together and defined the minimum information each party really needs, then I think we would have fewer problems with one another. I have worked as an architect, for structural engineers, and for construction companies, so I know these problems.

Sometimes on construction sites we have to relax a little and not try to control everything. Sometimes it is good to let things go because the result comes three or four months later. We need systems that help us avoid panic and information overload, especially information nobody actually needs.

When I was drawing the Kunsthaus Graz in a 3D model, I do not know how often I redrew it. That is why we then brought in RhinoScript and other tools, because I was simply tired of redoing plans. That is the normal process: you try something, then an engineer says they want a bigger beam or a smaller beam or a thicker wall or another material, and then the construction company comes in and says they know how to build it more cheaply. All the drawings you made just go into the bin.

In German there is the word Leidenschaft for passion, and within it is the word for suffering. Suffering comes before passion. My passion is communication. We need to communicate in a very simple way. The question is whether we have to upskill people to match the technology, or whether the technology has to downscale to people. I think the latter is more important.
